actions.thought: With 5♥T♠ in UTG (first position), this is a clear fold. This hand is too weak to open in the worst position at the table. 5-T offsuit has poor connectivity and weak high card value. As a professional, I maintain a tight UTG opening range with strong hands and premium pairs only. Folding weak hands out of position preserves my stack for better opportunities while building an exploitatively tight image at this early stage of the tournament.

I’m on the button with Kc6c and 100BB effective. K6s is a playable, positionally advantaged hand with backdoor flush and reasonable postflop equity — not a premium, but a good steal candidate. With only the SB and BB left to act, I want to take initiative, discourage loose callers, and avoid a limp-multiway pot. A standard 3x BB open to 300 applies pressure without overcommitting. Plan: take the lead and c-bet favorable flops (king-high, club draws, or low uncoordinated boards); fold to a large 3-bet since this hand is often dominated and doesn’t fare well against strong ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs 8d. The Button has raised to 3x the big blind. Q8o is a weak hand to play out of position, even in a 4-handed game. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against a wide range, and the hand is not strong enough to 3-bet. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after folds from UTG and SB. Pot is 450, calling 200 more (total 300 from me) for pot odds of ~3:1 (25% equity needed). K5s suited is a strong defensive hand vs. a wide button opening range (~25-40% of hands), with good equity (~38-42% vs. typical button range), flush potential, and king blocker. OOP postflop but deep stacks (99BB) allow set-mining/flush-draw play. 3-betting OOP is suboptimal with this hand; folding is too tight given pot odds and range advantage. Standard call to realize equity and keep pot multiway potential low (already HU).

Flop is Jc4c9d, rainbow-ish with club flush draw. I have Kd5d: king-high, backdoor diamond flush draw (one diamond on board), no pair, no straight draw. OOP vs button raiser who has wide range including Ax, broadway, suited connectors, pairs. Pot 650, I act first. No strong value or draw to bet/raise. Donk betting air OOP is poor; check-raise bluff too thin without reads. Best is check-fold to aggression, as king-high has limited showdown value and poor playability OOP if called. Realized equity preflop; now control pot and fold to bets unless very small.

I raised preflop and have Kc6c on Jc-4c-9d — nut flush draw plus a King overcard. Heads-up vs the big blind who checked, this is a strong c-bet spot: I have lots of equity to improve and I can fold out many non-club hands. I want meaningful fold equity but also to build the pot when I'm often ahead by equity, so I’ll bet ~50% pot. If Elon 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate and fold most of the time (I’m not comfortable getting committed without a made hand and I’m dominated by Acx), but if he calls I’ll continue based on the turn. Bet 325 into 650.

Facing a half-pot continuation bet of 325 into 975 on a Jc4c9d flop as OOP with Kd5d (king-high, backdoor diamond flush draw). My hand has no pair, no straight draw, and only backdoor equity. Button's wide preflop raising range (25-40%) includes many Ax, broadway, pairs, suited connectors that connect well here (overpairs JJ+/99, AJ/TJ, flush draws, etc.), while my king-high blocks few strong hands and has limited showdown value. Pot odds ~3.2:1 require ~24% equity, but vs. polarized c-bet range (value + bluffs), I have ~30-35% at best, but calling OOP realizes poorly on scary turns/rivers and bloats pot with air. Check-raising is a bluff too thin without position/reads. Standard check-fold to aggression preserves stack for better spots in deep-stack tournament.

UTG with 6♠2♥ — a very weak, non-coordinated offsuit hand with poor straight/flush potential and terrible kicker. In 4-handed play ranges are wider, but this hand is dominated by most hands opponents will have and plays terribly postflop. Calling 100 or raising to the min (200) would commit chips in a very marginal spot and open me up to 3-way or isolated aggression from better hands. Preserve stack and wait for a playable hand in position — fold.

K8s is a strong starting hand on the button in a short-handed game. Since the UTG player folded, I have a clear opportunity to raise and put pressure on the blinds. A 3x raise is standard to seize control and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play a pot in position.

AQs suited is a premium hand that plays well multiway but is best as a 3-bet here OOP vs a button open range, which is wide and contains many Ax weaker, broadways, and suited connectors that we dominate. 3-betting for value, fold equity, and to isolate/deny equity. Calling invites BB action and keeps us OOP passive. Size to 900 (3x the raise) is standard with deep stacks (~96BB).

K5o is a weak hand that becomes even more difficult in a 3-bet pot. I'm out of position in the BB facing a re-raise from Elon (SB) who 3-bet to 900 after Flash's initial raise to 300. Elon's aggressive re-raise from the SB indicates a strong range (likely JJ+, AK, AQ). My K5o has roughly 30-35% equity against this range at best, but the pot odds don't justify the call. Calling 800 to win 1300 (2-to-1 odds) requires approximately 33% equity, and I'm likely below that threshold. Furthermore, I'll be out of position postflop with a marginal holding. Folding preserves my stack for stronger opportunities and avoids putting chips at risk with a weak, easily dominated hand.

Elon has 3-bet from the small blind after my initial open. While K8s is not the strongest hand, I am on the button and have position for the remainder of the hand. The price to call is 600 more into a pot of 1300, giving me roughly 3.16:1 odds. Given the playability of suited Kings and the advantage of position, calling is a standard play here to see a flop and evaluate.

Preflop 3-bet with AQs suited vs wide button open was correct for value, fold equity, and isolation. Flop 7h9hTd completely misses our hand: no pair, no draws beyond backdoor spade flush and runner-runner straight possibilities with overcards. Board is coordinated, hitting villain's button calling range hard (e.g., 98s, JTs, 87s, flush draws, pair+draws). As OOP first to act with air and low equity (<20% vs range), c-betting risks bloating pot or getting check-raised off nothing; better to check and realize our equity for free or fold to aggression, preserving stack early in tournament.

On a board of 7h-9h-Td, I have an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or J completes it). My opponent, Elon, 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked the flop. While I could bet as a semi-bluff to capitalize on the folded 3-bet range (like AK/AQ), checking behind is a more prudent play in a 3-bet pot on such a coordinated board. It allows me to realize my equity and see a free turn card without the risk of being check-raised off my draw. If I hit my straight on the turn, my hand will be well-disguised.

Board pairs the 7s on turn, adding trips and two-pair possibilities to villain's calling range (e.g., 7x, T7, 97), while also completing diamond flush draw (though less relevant). My AQs remains ace-high with minimal showdown equity against button 3-bet calling range (pairs, broadways, SCs that connected like JTs, 89s, hearts). No draws or pairs for me. Preflop 3-bet and flop check-check sequence reps strength but also allows pot control OOP. Betting risks (1) villain calling/raising with better (trips, two-pair, str8s) or (2) folding worse, denying free card. Equity ~25-30% vs range; best to check again, realize equity cheaply to river, or fold to justified bets, preserving deep stack early (hand 2/150). Bluffing suboptimal as board favors villain value and paired board reduces fold equity.

The turn card is the 7d, pairing the board. Elon, who was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet, has checked for the second street in a row. I still have an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six). While I could bet to try and take the pot down now, checking back is the most prudent play. It guarantees that I see the river and realize my equity for free. If I hit my straight on the river, I can then value bet. If I miss, I can evaluate whether a bluff is appropriate based on Elon's action on the final street. Checking behind maintains pot control and prevents me from being blown off my hand by a check-raise.

River pairs the 9s, further capping the board and improving villain's calling range (trips 99/77, two-pair 79s/7Ts/9Ts, full houses). AQs remains pure ace-high with no pair, straight, or flush, <15% showdown equity vs button 3-bet calling range (Ax, broadways, SCs like 89s/JTs/87s/TT/JJ that connected heavily). Preflop 3-bet + check-check-check history reps strength but doesn't change our lack of value/draw. OOP first to act: Bluffing suboptimal (low fold equity on villain-favoring paired board, risks 1900+ pot OOP early), value impossible. Check preserves stack (hand 2/150, deep ~88BB), realizes equity cheaply vs bluffs/draws, easy fold to justified bets. Hero-calling unlikely profitable even to small bets given range.

The board has double-paired on the river with 7-9-T-7-9. My King-high (Kc 8c) has very little showdown value, as even Ace-high (like AK or AQ in Elon's 3-bet range) will beat me with a better fifth card (A vs K) using the two pair on the board. Elon has checked three streets in a row after 3-betting pre-flop, which suggests he likely has a marginal hand with some showdown value (like Ace-high or a small pocket pair) or has completely given up. While a bluff might occasionally work, the double-paired board makes it difficult to represent a specific hand like a full house after I checked the flop and turn. I will check back to minimize losses and see the showdown.

I am UTG with 7c 3c. This is a very weak hand, even in a 4-handed game. Opening with 73 suited from the first position is generally unprofitable and puts me at a disadvantage post-flop against three opponents who have position or closing action. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

Preflop on the button with J9s in an unopened pot after UTG folds. Excellent position with a suited connector that plays well multiway or heads-up postflop. Standard to raise for value/isolation/steal blinds. Deep stacks (~100BB), so 3x BB open (300 total) is appropriate sizing to build pot while maintaining fold equity against blinds. Limping is too passive; folding is too tight from button.

K2 offsuit is a weak hand that should be folded from the small blind. Despite having decent pot odds (450:250), this hand lacks the equity needed to profitably continue. K2o is easily dominated by stronger kings and plays poorly out of position post-flop. Elon's button raise could represent a wide range, making this a marginal situation at best. Early tournament position (hand 3 of 150) calls for disciplined hand selection. I'll preserve my stack and wait for premium holdings to get involved.

I’m in the big blind with J♦7♦ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). Heads-up the button’s opening range is wide and J7s has decent postflop playability (flush/straight potential and decent equity). Calling costs me 200 more (total commitment 300) and creates a 650 pot, so I need ~31% equity to break even — J7s should be around or above that vs a wide open. A min‑reraise to 500 is an option but too thin here: it risks a 4‑bet or bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll call to realize equity and play a flop in position against a single opponent.

Flop: 8♠ 9♠ K♠. I hold J♦7♦ — no pair, only a one-card gutshot to a T and a two-card (runner‑runner) diamond backdoor. I’m out of position versus the preflop raiser whose range contains a lot of spades (made flushes/strong draws) and Kx hands that currently beat me. Leading out OOP with a marginal hand is unnecessary risk. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: if he checks back I get a free turn to reevaluate; if he c-bets a small size (~≤1/3 pot) I’ll call to see the turn and chase the straight/backdoor flush, but I’ll fold to larger barrels (≈≥1/2 pot) since my equity is too low against likely made hands.

Flop is Ks9s8s, a very draw-heavy board with flush draws and straight draws (e.g., QT, JT, T7). I have middle pair (9s) with a jack kicker, which blocks some straight draws like JT. As the preflop raiser in position against BB who called and checked, my range is perceived as strong. Middle pair has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards, sets, better pairs, and draws. Betting for thin value (vs weak Kx, Ax, bluffs) and protection against draws is standard. Check might allow her to realize equity for free or bluff, but c-betting in position maintains initiative. Pot 650, bet ~60% pot (400) balances value/protection/fold equity without overcommitting deep stacks.

I have J♦7♦ on a K♠9♠8♠ flop with no spade, no pair, only a one-card straight (four tens) and weak backdoor diamonds. Elon (preflop aggressor) c-bet 400 into the pot (pot now 1050), so a call of 400 needs ~27.6% equity (400/(1050+400)). My realistic equity (turn/river tens + backdoor diamond runners) is well below that and he’s representing many spade/King/9/8 combos that already beat me. Raising is too thin OOP and will bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

2c7s is one of the worst possible starting hands with no connectivity, suitedness in bad combo, or high card value. In UTG position 4-handed, it has zero playability or fold equity if raising. Standard preflop charts fold this 100% of the time. Pot is small at 150, dead money not worth risking 100+ to play trash. Preserve stack for premium hands.

Playing A9o on the button in a 4-max situation is favorable. After Elon folded UTG, it's just me against the two blinds. A9 offsuit is a solid hand from the button position with decent playability and showdown value. I'm raising to 300 (3x the big blind) to apply pressure and gain fold equity. If either blind calls, I maintain position and can play the hand postflop with an ace-high holding that can connect with many flops. My stack size is healthy, and this sizing is standard aggression from the button. The goal is to either win the pot immediately or build it with a hand that has reasonable equity.

I’m in the small blind with Q♠T♠ facing a standard button open to 300. Pot is 450; I need to put in 250 more (300 total). QTs is a strong, playable suited broadway — lots of post‑flop equity (flush/straight/Two‑Pair potential) and good implied odds. Folding would be too tight; a min 3‑bet to 500 is an option but I don’t like a tiny 3‑bet size here because it gives little fold equity and leaves me OOP vs the button. I’d prefer to see a flop and use the hand’s playability to extract value or fold as appropriate. If I call and see heads‑up vs the button I’ll c‑bet favorable flops (spade/T/Q/connected textures) and play more cautiously on dry boards; if the BB also comes along I’ll tighten down and realize equity. Call is the best balance of risk/reward here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 4h. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I have decent pot odds (calling 200 to win 900), 84o is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. I'll fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I called preflop with Q♠T♠ for playability. The flop 4♣ 8♠ 4♦ completely misses me — I have no pair and only a backdoor spade possibility (I would need both turn and river to be spades; ~4% to complete). With effective stacks ~9.6–11k and being out of position to the button, leading out OOP with a marginal hand is a losing long‑term line. I want pot control and information: check and let the opener c‑bet. Plan: fold to a large, polarizing c‑bet; call smaller c‑bets to realize equity/float to the turn and re‑evaluate on spade/Q/T or a safe card. For now: check.

Minni checked the flop after calling my pre-flop raise from small blind. I'm in position with A9o on a 4c-8s-4d board. While the paired board reduces the likelihood of strong made hands, I have top pair with a 9 kicker, which is likely ahead of her checking range. She called 250 pre-flop from the small blind, indicating a reasonable hand but nothing premium. Her check suggests weakness or caution.

I should bet here to:
1) Maintain the aggression I established pre-flop
2) Apply pressure and win the pot immediately against likely weak holdings
3) Build the pot when I'm ahead with top pair
4) Charge any drawing hands she might have

A bet of 450 (about 65% of the 700 pot) is aggressive but justified. It's sizing that applies real pressure while remaining reasonable relative to stack sizes. If she folds, I win the pot and build my stack. If she calls, I'll have position on the turn and can reassess based on her action.

I checked the flop OOP with Q♠T♠ intending to call smaller c‑bets and fold large/polarizing ones. Claude fires 450 into the pot (pot now 1150; I must call 450). Calling costs 450 to win 1600 total, so I’m getting ~28.1% pot odds (450/(1150+450)). My hand has no pair, only two overcards and a backdoor spade flush that requires both turn+river spades (~4%). The chance to pair by the river is only ~24%, and combined with the tiny backdoor equity my overall equity vs a reasonable c‑bet range is marginal and likely below the required break‑even point once his value portion (4x, pairs, overpairs) is considered. I’m out of position with deep stacks, so raising as a bluff here is too thin and calling commits me to difficult decisions on later streets. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

Q3o is a weak, marginal hand with poor equity. Playing from UTG (worst position) with three active opponents remaining behind me significantly reduces profitability. The hand lacks the strength to justify a call or raise, and the pot odds don't compensate for the positional disadvantage. At 10,250 chips with a healthy stack, folding preserves my chips for premium hands in better spots. This is a standard fold from UTG.

I’m on the button with J♣4♠ — a marginal hand, but with only the blinds to act and deep stacks this is a good spot to attempt a standard button open. Raising isolates the blinds, uses position and fold equity, and avoids playing a weak hand multiway out of position. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 to keep pressure and pot control. Plan: fold to any 3‑bet, and if called play aggressively in position — c‑bet favorable flops, check/controlled approach on dry boards and fold to heavy action when I miss.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni raised from the button, and I am in the small blind, meaning I would be out of position for the rest of the hand if I called. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff in this spot, especially early in the session. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision.

T6 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, offsuit, and limited straight or flush potential. Facing a button raise to 200 (small 2x raise), likely a wide stealing range from Minni, but calling 100 into 350 offers pot odds of ~3.5:1 (22% equity needed). However, my hand has poor equity against her range (~30-35% at best) and I'll be out of position postflop with little to connect meaningfully. No strong blockers for 3-betting as a bluff. Stacks are deep (~100BB), early in tournament (hand 5/150), so folding trash preserves stack for better spots. Minni has shown tendency to call pre but fold to flop c-bets, but still, not worth defending here.

UTG in a 4-handed game with pocket Kings is a clear value open. I want to build the pot and narrow the field while denying free or cheap cards, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300). This sizing gives fold equity, keeps weaker hands and big bluffs in play, and leaves room to respond to a 3-bet (I’ll re-evaluate and likely 4-bet or call depending on sizing and opponent tendencies).

I am on the button holding 7c 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from UTG to 300. There is no reason to play this hand, especially against an UTG open. I will fold and wait for a better spot to preserve my stack.

Pocket eights is a premium hand 4-handed, crushing UTG Minni's wide raising range (likely 22+, AJo+, KJo+, suited broadways, etc.). Equity is excellent (~55-60%+ vs that range). 3-betting takes initiative, builds pot for value when ahead, isolates vs BB who may fold wide, and denies equity to Ax, broadway hands that call. Standard size 3x the raise to 900. OOP but manageable with strong hand; can cbet flops well. Folding BB unlikely to wake up without premium. Calling is playable but passive; prefer aggression early to maximize stack growth. History shows Minni folds to postflop aggression sometimes.

Calling with Ad9d. This hand has strong equity (~40-45%) against Elon's 3-betting range, and the pot odds justify the call at approximately 1.6:1 (needing 38% equity). While I'm out of position postflop, the high card and flush draw potential makes this a profitable play. Early in the tournament, this is a spot where I can profitably see a flop with reasonable equity and manageable commitment.

I opened UTG with KK to build a pot and deny cheap cards. Facing a 3-bet to 900 from the aggressive SB and a cold-call from the BB, I prefer to 4-bet for value and to isolate rather than just calling into a multiway pot where an overpair loses equity. A min-re-raise to 1500 is too small (gives cheap flops and little fold equity), so I’ll make a standard value 4-bet to 2,700 total (≈3x the 3-bet). This sizing pressures speculative hands and many marginal broadway hands, narrows the field, and still leaves room to maneuver post-flop; if I face a shove I’ll re-evaluate but KK is usually a call absent a very tight read.

88 is a strong hand but facing a 4-bet to 2700 from UTG Minni after my SB 3-bet, with BB Claude having flatted the 3-bet OOP, this spot calls for a fold. Minni's UTG open + 4-bet range is polarized but value-heavy (QQ+, AK), crushing 88 (~35-40% equity). Multi-way pot OOP with vulnerable set-mining hand risks bloating pot without initiative. Claude's flat suggests medium strength Ax/pairs that connect better postflop. Standard discipline: fold premium to 4-bets here early in tournament (hand 6/150) to preserve deep stack (100BB) for superior edges. No history suggests Minni 4-bets light; her aggression has folded to postflop pressure but preflop 4-bet is serious.

Calling with Ad9d. Against Minni's 4-betting range, I have approximately 35-40% equity, which exceeds the 28.6% break-even threshold on pot odds of 3.5:1. The diamond flush draw provides strong post-flop playability. Early in the tournament, this is a profitable spot with reasonable equity and manageable risk. I avoid folding away the 800 already invested while keeping my options open post-flop.

With Ad9d on a 6sKs5d board, I have strong equity against Minni's 4-betting range. My flush draw provides 9 outs (~36% equity to complete), plus two outs to top pair with the ace, giving me roughly 35-40% overall equity. Since Minni checked from UTG (unusual for a 4-bettor), I should apply pressure with a flop bet rather than allow her to dictate the hand.

Betting 2000 (roughly 1/3 of the 6300 pot) achieves several objectives:
1. Applies pressure and might fold out AQ/AJ type hands she could have
2. Builds the pot with good equity on my draw
3. Maintains stack depth (4850 remaining) for continued play on turn/river
4. Prevents her from controlling the hand through checking

At this stage of the tournament (hand 6 of 150), this is a profitable play that leverages my strong drawing potential with reasonable equity against her likely range of premium pairs or AK/AQ variants.

Preflop I 4-bet to isolate with KK and Claude called — his flatting range to a 4-bet is relatively strong but includes AK, Kx, and medium pocket pairs (and some suited broadways). I just flopped trips on Ks-6s-5d: this is an extremely strong hand versus his range — nothing currently beats me, and the main concern is being outdrawn to a future full house if he holds a pocket 6 or 5. Claude's flop bet is small (2000 into an ~8300 pot), which is either a probe or a thin value bet; many of his hands will still call a large raise (AK, Kx, mid pairs, spade/backdoor draws). I want to both extract maximum value and deny free/cheap turn cards that could give him equity, so I should polarize and charge his calling range now. Given the effective stacks and the strength advantage, I shove to maximize EV and force him to pay the price to see more cards.

Calling Minni's all-in raise to 7,200. With Ad9d on a 6sKs5d board, I have strong drawing equity:

**Equity Assessment:**
- Diamond flush draw: 9 outs (36% equity)
- Two additional outs to top pair with the ace (counterfeited by the king, but still may improve)
- Overall equity vs Minni's range (AA, KK, AK, QQ): approximately 35-40%

**Pot Odds Analysis:**
- Current pot: 15,500
- Cost to call: 5,200
- Pot odds: 2.98:1
- Equity needed to break even: 25%
- My equity (35-40%) significantly exceeds the break-even threshold

**Strategic Considerations:**
1. Minni is all-in after her re-raise (stack shows 0), so no additional betting streets
2. Early tournament positioning (hand 6 of 150): doubling up now is extremely valuable
3. My hand has strong one-card equity against her most likely holdings (overpairs/sets)
4. Even at 60% to lose, the pot odds make this mathematically profitable
5. This call leaves me with 350 chips if I lose, but potentially quadruples my stack if I win

The math is clear: this is a profitable call with excellent pot odds and sufficient equity to justify the decision.

K7o is a weak hand to open from under-the-gun, even in a 4-player game. Given that Claude is extremely short-stacked (300 chips) and likely to commit his stack soon, and Minni has a dominant chip lead, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger hand rather than risking chips in a marginal spot.

Preflop on the button with 67o, a speculative connector with straight potential and good playability. Flash folded UTG, leaving SB Claude who has entered for 300 and BB Minni with a massive stack. Pot is 150 with 100 to call, offering excellent implied odds deep-stacked (100BB effective). Position allows controlling the pot postflop. Calling is +EV rather than folding a playable hand cheap or raising light OOP opponents who may have wide ranges. Will see flop and play fits/draws aggressively.

With only 300 chips remaining and holding 8-4 (one of the weakest hands in poker), I should fold. I'm out of position and critically short-stacked. Good stacks preserve chips for premium hands and winning situations. This hand offers poor equity and bad position, so folding is the correct risk management decision to maximize my tournament life.

I'm in the big blind with 7c9h facing a single limp from the button and the small blind folded. This is a marginal, offsuit hand and I'll be out of position throughout the hand. With deep stacks and a wide limp range from the button, I don't want to bloat the pot OOP with a hand that plays poorly postflop — a small isolation raise will likely get called and put me in tough spots. I prefer pot control and a free/cheap flop to see where I stand; if the button c-bets I can fold to heavy aggression without improvement or call small bets with backdoor equity. Therefore I check.

Board is K♦ A♦ 9♦ and I hold 7♣9♥ — middle pair (pair of nines) with a weak kicker and no diamond backdoors. Villain limped, so his range is wide and includes many A/K combos and some two-diamond hands (which would already have a flush). I’m out of position with deep stacks, and this is a textbook pot-control spot: my hand is vulnerable to overcards, top pairs, and flushes, and betting here will often either get me raised/called by better hands or commit me OOP to a bloated pot. I’ll check to keep the pot small, allow bluffs to bet, and call small c-bets to realize equity. Plan: check; call a modest c-bet (roughly ≤1/3–1/2 pot) to see the turn, but fold to large bets or raises which mostly represent hands that currently beat me (A/K/flush). If villain checks back, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn for a cheap showdown or a turn-barrel plan.

Flop is K♦ A♦ 9♦, giving me the nut flush draw with 7♦ (four diamonds to the nut flush). No pair or straight draw, but pure nut FD in position heads-up vs BB who checked. This board is extremely draw-heavy (flush draw, straight draws like JT, QJ, T8). Minni's check suggests weakness, possibly a big ace without diamond, mid pair, or missed entirely. Semi-bluff betting is standard here: deny equity to their draws, get value from weaker hands, and fold out better if needed. Deep stacks (10k effective vs their 20k), pot 250. Bet 2/3 pot (~167) rounded to 175 for fold equity and to build pot if called. Position allows controlling if they raise.

I have middle pair (9s) with a weak 7 kicker on K♦ A♦ 9♦. Elon’s flop bet is small (~175 into 425 ≈41% pot) — exactly in the range I planned to call to see a turn rather than bloat the pot OOP. Pot odds: calling 175 to win ~600 gives ~3.43:1 (~22% equity needed), and versus a wide limp/c-bet range I have sufficient showdown equity to continue. Raising OOP is risky here (vulnerable to A/K/diamond combos) and folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call the flop, realize equity and fold to significant raise or large turn aggression unless I improve (or he shows weakness). If he checks back or the turn is a harmless card, re-evaluate for a thin value or pot-control line.

I have trip 9s on the turn (board Kd Ad 9d 9s). This is a very strong made hand that beats most of Elon’s flop c-bet range (Ax, Kx, weaker pairs), though it is vulnerable to the small number of combos that already have a full house/quads or a nut two-diamond hand. Elon bet small on the flop then checked the turn, which reads as medium strength or pot-control. I want to extract value from Ax/Kx and worse pairs and deny a free river that could make a full house for some of his holdings, so I’ll bet for value and protection. A half-pot bet (~300 into the 600 pot) should get called by worse hands while keeping the pot controlled out of position; I’ll re-evaluate if he raises big (large raises likely represent the rare full-house/quads or the nut diamond combos).

Facing a turn donk bet of 300 into 900 pot from Minni in the BB after checking flop and calling my cbet. Board Kd Ad 9d 9s is still extremely draw-heavy, pairing the 9 but not changing flush draw dynamics. I hold the nut flush draw (7d, 9 outs) with position and deep effective stacks (~9.7k). Pot odds to call are excellent (~4:1, ~20% equity needed; FD has 35%+ vs typical range). However, calling keeps pot small and allows her to realize equity cheaply with worse draws or weak made hands. Her line (check-call flop, donk turn) suggests possible weak pair (9x, mid A/K), trapped big hand unlikely given history. Semi-bluff raise maximizes fold equity against better hands (sets, two-pair), gets value from worse (inferior draws, weaker pairs), and builds pot for when I hit. Raising to 900 (3x her bet, ~pot-sized) balances pressure and pot growth. If she shoves, I have fold equity and can call profitably with ~36% equity heads-up. Position allows control post. Standard play with premium draw.

I hold 7♣9♥ and made trips on the turn (board K♦ A♦ 9♦ 9♠). I bet 300 for value/protection and Elon raised to 900. His raise is polarized — it can be full houses (A9/K9), higher trips (AA/KK), or a diamond flush, but it also contains lots of Kx/Ax and some bluffs. Folding trips here would be too weak because I’m ahead of a large portion of his range and would be giving up value from worse K/A hands and single-diamond holdings. Re-raising is unnecessarily polarizing and can get me jammed by the thin portion of his range that already beats me. Calling 600 more (900 total) controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me re-evaluate on the river. Plan: call the raise now (900 total); on the river I’ll fold to a shove that looks like a full house/quads or call/shove for value only when the action and runout are consistent with hands I beat.

I have trips (9s) with a weak 7 kicker on K♦ A♦ 9♦ 9♠ Q♣. Elon turned a big raise (900) but then checked the river — that line is polarized (full houses/strong flushes vs bluffs/medium K/A hands) and his river check looks more like caution than absolute strength. Checking lets me take the showdown for free, but a small value bet will extract from the many Kx/Ax and thin calls that beat a check and still keeps the pot controlled against the small universe of full houses/quads/solid flushes that crushed me on the turn. Pot is 2,400 — I size down to ~600 (≈25% pot) to maximize calls from worse hands while limiting the risk if he check-raises. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls I go to showdown; if he check-raises big (an all-in or very large shove) I’ll respect that line and fold as it most likely represents full house/quads.

On the river, the board is Kd Ad 9d 9s Qc. I hold 6s7d, which missed the nut flush draw entirely and has no pair, straight, or any showdown value—pure air. Minni's line is strong: check-called flop cbet on a diamond-heavy board, donk-bet turn pairing the 9s, called my semi-bluff raise (900 into 900 pot), and now leads river for 600 into 3000. This polarizes her range to value hands like 9x (trips), Ad9s/Kd9s/two-pair, or possibly a diamond flush. No straights complete on this board. Pot odds are ~5:1 (600 to call to win 3000), requiring ~17% equity, but with nothing, I have near 0% vs her likely value range. Continuing the bluff lacks fold equity on river after she called a raise; folding preserves stack for better spots. Tournament phase early (hand 7/150), no need to spew chips.

Preflop UTG in 4-handed play with K6o, an offsuit king with a weak kicker. This hand is not in a standard UTG opening range (typically 66+, ATs+, KJs+, QJs, AJo+, KQo). Facing no raise (pot 150 from blinds, to call 100 to limp), raising is too weak and limping is suboptimal as it plays poorly postflop OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacks (88BB), but discipline key to maximize over 150 hands. Opponents show aggression in prior hands, but no fold equity needed here.

I'm extremely short-stacked with only 300 chips (3 BB) on the button after UTG folded. With Ad3d, I have an ace, which is a strong card in short-stack poker. My options are: fold (giving up the 50 in the small blind already posted), call 100 (leaving me with 200 and likely committed postflop in a weak spot), or push all-in for maximum fold equity. 

Pushing all-in for 300 is the optimal play because: (1) My stack is so small that fold equity is crucial to survival; (2) An ace has decent equity if called; (3) From the button, a small all-in push puts significant pressure on the blinds to make a decision; (4) If both blinds fold, I win the 150 in the pot immediately, which represents a 50% chip gain. The math favors an aggressive all-in push over calling and being out of position postflop with a marginal hand.

I’m in the small blind with 4s7s facing a button all‑in for 300. The pot is 450 and I need to add 250 more (300 total) — a very cheap price: calling 250 to win ~700 gives ~2.8:1 pot odds (need ≈26% equity). 4♠7♠ has reasonable equity vs a wide shove and has suited/straight potential. A min‑raise to 500 only creates a side pot against the big blind and unnecessarily commits more chips out of position with a weak hand; folding is too tight given the cheap price. I’ll call to see a flop and reassess based on whether the big blind also gets involved.

I am in the Big Blind with QTo. Claude (Button) raised to 3x, and Minni (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700, and I only need to call 200 more. QTo is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a button raise, especially with the excellent pot odds of 3.5:1 offered by the multi-way action. I will call to see a flop.

I have 4♠7♠ on a 9♠ 9♣ A♥ flop — essentially no real equity (only a runner‑runner spade flush possibility). Claude is already all‑in for 300, leaving me effectively heads‑up vs Flash who called preflop and will have many Aces, 9s and pairs in his range. The pot is already 900 and any feel‑out bet here will have very little fold equity and only risk bloating the pot out of position with air. I’ll check and exercise pot control: see how Flash reacts, fold to meaningful aggression, and only continue if I pick up a real draw or improve on the turn.

I have Queen-high on an Ace-high, paired board. This board generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Since Minni checked to me, I will check as well. Betting here as a bluff into two opponents on an Ace-high board is low-frequency and risky, so I'll see if I can get a free card or see what Claude does.

I have 4♠7♠ on 9♠–9♣–A♥–5♥. I missed completely — no pair and with the turn heart the backdoor spade flush is now impossible. Flash (BB) who called preflop and checked the flop will have many Ax, pocket nines or other pairs in his range; a probe bet here has very little fold equity and just bloats the pot out of position with air. Pot is already 900 and I’m not facing a bet, so I’ll exercise pot control: check and see what Flash does on the river. If he bets a reasonable size I’ll fold this hand; if he checks I can reconsider a cheap showdown or a river bluff depending on the card.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high on a board of 9s-9c-Ah-5h. The pre-flop raiser (Claude) checked back on the flop, and Minni (SB) has checked again on the turn. While it is possible no one has an Ace or a Nine, betting here into two opponents is risky without a made hand or a significant draw. I will check to see the river for free and potentially realize some showdown value if the hand remains checked down, or evaluate based on Claude's action.

I paired the 4 on the river (4♠7♠ on 9♠ 9♣ A♥ 5♥ 4♥) but it’s a very weak second pair. Flash has been passive all streets (check-check) and his range is heavy with Aces and pocket 9s that beat me; only a few worse hands will call a river bet. Betting has little fold equity and little thin value — I’d simply bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. With no bet to face and the pot already 900, the prudent line is to check and take the cheap showdown rather than bluffing or overplaying this weak pair.

The board is Ace-high and paired with nines. Since the pre-flop raiser (Claude) has checked the flop and turn, and Minni has checked all the way to the river, it's likely no one has a strong hand like an Ace or a Nine. However, with two opponents, Queen-high has very little showdown value. While a bluff might occasionally work here, checking is the most disciplined play in a multi-way pot that has been checked down, as someone could easily have a small pair or a weak Ace they are planning to take to showdown for free. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if there is a significant bet from Claude.

I'm holding 5h Kd in UTG, the worst position at the table. While my stack is critically short at only 900 chips (9 big blinds), this doesn't justify playing K5o from UTG. K5 off-suit is a weak hand with poor playability and limited equity against the range of hands that can call or raise from the stronger positions behind me. The blinds are 50/100, so folding now costs me position naturally in the rotation where I can find better spots with premium hands. Given that I'm short-stacked, I need to be selective with my marginal hands and make my stand with stronger holdings where I have better equity and position advantage. Folding preserves chips for a higher-EV spot.

Qc-Tc (Q♠ T♣) on the button is a clear open-raise candidate — good playability and position against the blinds. With UTG folded and deep stacks behind, I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and narrow the field. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB): large enough to gain solid fold equity vs the SB/BB and discourage limp-calls, but still a standard, controllable size if I get 3‑bet. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold or re-evaluate; if called I’ll play postflop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 2c, which is a very weak hand. Minni, the chip leader on the button, has raised to 300. Calling out of position with such a weak hand is a losing strategy, especially with the Big Blind still to act. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

J6o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Minni's button raise is likely wide due to position, but my hand has low equity (around 30-35% vs. typical button opening range) and poor post-flop potential OOP. Pot odds are 450:200 (~2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), but implied odds are negative due to dominance risks and difficulty realizing equity. Deep stacks but no history suggesting Minni is overly loose here. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.

Holding K♠3♣ UTG in a 4-handed game is marginal, but the table is short-handed so opening ranges widen. I want to apply pressure to pick up the blinds while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a standard min-open to 200. This achieves steal/fold equity and keeps decisions easy post-flop. If the short-stack big blind (Claude, 800) shoves, I will call — I only have to put up an extra 600 to win a pot of ~1650, so I need ~36% equity to break even, and Kx vs a wide short-stack shove range is in the ballpark. If either deep stack (Flash or Elon) 3-bets big (non-all-in) I’ll fold — K3o is too dominated to continue; I’ll proceed cautiously postflop and c-bet selectively on dry boards, otherwise fold to heavy resistance.

I am on the button with KQo, which is a strong premium hand in a 4-handed game. Minni, who has been active and has the largest stack, opened for 2x from the UTG position. Claude is extremely short-stacked in the Big Blind (8bb). While I could 3-bet to isolate, Minni's large stack and Claude's high likelihood of being forced to go all-in or fold suggest that calling is a solid option to see a flop in position. However, with a short stack in the blinds, a raise to 500-600 would put a lot of pressure on the blinds and potentially isolate Minni. Given Claude's 800-chip stack, any significant raise from me basically commits him. I will 3-bet to 600 to take control of the hand and potentially force Claude to commit his remaining chips with a wider range while testing Minni's opening strength.

T6o is a very weak offsuit hand with no high card strength, straight potential, or suited value. Facing an UTG open from Minni (tight range) and a 3-bet from Flash on the button (likely strong value), entering the pot out of position in the SB multiway with Claude still to act is -EV. Pot odds are insufficient (calling 550 into 950), and future streets will be difficult OOP with this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in the tournament.

Shoving all-in for 800 chips with QJ. At 8BB deep facing a 3-bet, this is the strongest play. QJ has reasonable equity against both opponents, and the all-in move creates significant fold equity - likely winning the pot uncontested. Calling would leave me with only 300 chips in a crippled position, so going all-in applies better pressure while preserving my tournament life with a decent holding.

I opened UTG to 200 with K♠3♣. Flash 3‑bet to 600 and then Claude put it to 900 (all‑in). To call I must add 700 to win a pot of ~1750, so I’d need ≈28.6% equity to break even. My earlier plan was to call a short‑stack shove, but Flash has already shown strength with the 3‑bet and remains to act behind me — this turns the spot into a likely multiway/dominated situation. K3o out of position has poor multiway equity and is a negative‑EV call here. I’ll preserve my big stack and fold.

Claude has gone all-in for 900 chips total. I have already committed 600 chips to the pot, so I only need to call an additional 300 chips. With a pot of 1750, I am getting roughly 6:1 pot odds. KQo is a very strong hand against a short-stack's shoving range, which likely includes any pair, any Ace, and many Broadway or suited connector hands. Given the excellent odds and the strength of my hand, this is an automatic call.

Preflop on the button with J6o, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and domination potential against calling or 3-betting ranges. Position is advantageous, but the hand is below the standard GTO opening range from button vs blinds (typically 40-45%, excluding low offsuit broadways like J6o). Calling 100 into 150 offers poor implied odds due to reverse domination risks and difficulty realizing equity postflop. Raising risks playing out of position if 3-bet and doesn't generate enough fold equity with this holding. Folding preserves stack early in the tournament (hand 11/150) with deep stacks (87BB), focusing on better spots to maximize EV over 150 hands.

I’m in the small blind with A♦3♥ and the button has folded — this is effectively a heads-up spot against the big blind (Flash). A3o is a reasonable raising hand here: it has an ace-blocker, decent showdown value versus a wide defending range, and plays well against a wide BB calling range. I want initiative and to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position postflop, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x size (300 total). That sizing is big enough to discourage too many speculative calls/isolations compared with the min-raise, but still small relative to my 215 BB stack so I can fold to large 3-bets without committing too much. Plan: if BB folds I scoop the pot; if BB calls I’ll play postflop knowing I often have the range advantage (c-bet favorable ace-high or dry boards, proceed cautiously OOP on coordinated boards); if BB 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with T5o. Minni raised to 3BB (300) from the Small Blind. Given the wide range people typically open from the Small Blind, T5o is still too weak to defend profitably against a raise, especially out of position if Elon were still in (though he folded, I'm still closing the action). T5o has poor playability and very low equity against a standard opening range. I will fold and wait for a better spot to conserve my stack.

Three-handed on the button with T♥5♥ (suited, in position). With blinds 50/100 and a pot of 150, this is a spot to open a very wide range — I want to take the initiative and use position. Flash (SB) has been fairly passive and Elon (BB) can 3‑bet but isn’t doing it every hand. A standard min-raise to 200 (2x BB) maximizes fold equity against the blinds while keeping the sizing small enough to comfortably fold to a large 3‑bet. If I get called I can play postflop in position and apply pressure; if I face a big 3‑bet I’ll largely let this hand go. Raise to 200 to steal the blinds and seize initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 7d, which is a decent speculative hand in a short-handed game. The Button (Minni) opened for 200 (a 2BB raise). I have to call 150 more to see the flop, getting roughly 2.3:1 immediate odds. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the stack depths (approx 95BB) allow for some playability with suited connectors/gappers. If the Big Blind (Elon) also calls, the pot odds become even more attractive. I will call and look to hit a strong draw or two-pair+ on the flop.

2c3d is one of the worst possible hands: low, offsuit, poor connectivity. Minni's button raise is wide, but Flash's SB call adds strength and makes it multiway. Calling 100 into 500 gives ~16% implied odds, but equity is far lower (~10-15% vs typical ranges) with minimal playability. No fold equity for a 3-bet bluff post-call. Deep stacks, early in tournament—fold to preserve stack for better spots.

The flop of Td-Ac-Ad is very dry and favors a range that contains many Aces or pocket pairs. I have 7-high with no draw except a backdoor flush. In this position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Minni bets, I have no equity to continue and will fold. If she checks back, I might see a free turn card, but I have no interest in building a pot with this hand.

Flop: Td Ac Ad — I have T♥5♥, so my best hand is two pair (Aces and Tens). This is a strong but vulnerable holding: any Ace (or rare pocket Ten/full house/quads) crushes me. Flash (SB) called preflop and checked the flop, which suggests a wide, often weak/calling range (Kx, Qx, small pairs, some suited connectors) but also includes some A-x and a few pocket pairs. I want to extract value from the many worse hands (Kx/Qx/pairs) while keeping the pot manageable against the combos that beat me. A half‑pot bet (~250 into 500) is the right compromise — it prices in calls from worse hands, preserves fold equity, and leaves room to fold or re-evaluate if Flash raises strongly. Plan: bet 250; if I face a large raise I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold to a very large raise, call or defend vs a small raise and re‑evaluate on the turn).

I have virtually no equity on this board with 7-high. The flop of Td-Ac-Ad is extremely unfavorable for my 5d-7d, as it hits many hands in the pre-flop aggressor's range. Minni's 1/3 pot continuation bet is standard, and without even a gutshot or a strong backdoor draw, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

I am on the button with KJo (King of Spades and Jack of Hearts) in a three-handed game. This is a very strong hand to open for a raise from this position. The blinds are 50/100, and my stack of 9,400 is healthy. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. Both Elon and Minni have shown varying levels of aggression in previous hands, so I want to build a pot with my range advantage.

Facing a standard button raise to 300 (3x) with T2 offsuit from the small blind. This hand has zero equity against a button opening range, terrible postflop playability, no straight or flush potential, and is easily dominated. Folding is standard to preserve our deep stack (86BB) for better opportunities. Minni in BB acts after us, but no reason to speculate with trash.

I’m in the BB with 8♦2♦ facing a button raise to 300. I’ve already posted 100, so calling 200 to win a 450 pot gives me ~30.8% break-even equity (200 / (450+200)). 8-2s has well under that equity vs a standard button opening range and is easily dominated; playing OOP with such a weak hand is unprofitable. A 3-bet bluff here would be thin and unnecessary — I can wait for a better spot with position. Fold and preserve my deep stack for more favorable opportunities.

Pocket sevens (77) is a strong hand in a 3-handed pot from the button with excellent position. The pot is small (150) facing a modest to-call of 100, likely from a limp and check or small raise by the blinds. Raising isolates weaker holdings, builds the pot with value, and leverages fold equity against wide blind ranges. Calling risks a multiway pot without initiative; folding is far too tight. Raise sizing to 400 (4x the to-call amount, or ~4BB effective) is standard for isolation in this spot without overcommitting deep stacks. Minni and Flash have shown aggression but also folds to raises in prior hands.

9♦8♦ is a playable, deep-stack hand in a 3‑player pot — good implied odds and strong postflop playability (straights/flushes). Facing a 4x open from the button and a live big blind behind, I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with a marginal hand and build a big pot; calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves implied odds, and lets me see a flop with equity. I will call the 400 to play a flop and re-evaluate based on texture and reactions (fold to a big 3‑bet, continue aggressively on favorable flops).

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 5s. Elon (Button) raised to 400 and Minni (Small Blind) called. To call the additional 300, I would be getting 3-to-1 odds (300 to win 1200). However, 95 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially out of position against two opponents who have already shown strength or interest in the hand. I have a healthy stack and do not need to defend with such marginal holdings. Folding is the most prudent play here.

Flop: 2♣ J♣ K♣. I have 9♦8♦ — no pair, no club, and no one-card straight draw (only remote runner-runner outs). This is a highly unfavorable, monotone-club board that massively favors the preflop raiser (he can have clubs, Kx, Jx or strong draws). I called preflop to see a flop cheaply; out of position with virtually no equity, I should control the pot size. I will check and avoid bloating the pot OOP — folding to any meaningful c-bet and only considering a call to a very tiny probe bet that offers proper pot odds. If Elon checks back I can reevaluate on the turn.

Pocket 77 is a strong pre-flop hand but faces a disastrous flop of KcJc2c, a highly coordinated board with flush draws, open-enders (QT), gutshots (AT, Q10), and top pairs like Kx or Jx dominating. Minni's SB calling range after my button raise includes many suited connectors, suited broadways, Ax suited clubs, and speculative hands that crush me now. She checked, giving me initiative, but betting risks inducing raises from draws or value, or calls from better hands with little fold equity given how well the board hits her range. My hand has poor showdown value and blocks few strong combinations. Checking preserves stack, controls pot size (900 into 8175 effective), allows realization of any live equity on turn/river if she bets weakly, and exploits position to fold to aggression or check-raise if warranted. Raising pre-flop was correct isolation; now passive play is GTO-optimal on this texture.

Board is 2♣ J♣ K♣ Jh and I hold 9♦8♦ — no pair, no club, and essentially zero realistic equity. Elon was the preflop raiser and his range is very heavy with clubs, Kx and Jx; the monotone-club flop already favored him and the turn pairing the J only improves holdings in his range. I’m out of position and the pot is already 900; bluffing into a likely club-heavy or paired range is a losing line and bloats the pot for no reason. I will check and keep the pot small — folding to any meaningful bet. If Elon checks back I can re-evaluate the river for a very thin bluff or a fold depending on the card and his action.

Pocket 77 remains an underpair on a highly coordinated board (KcJc2cJh) that heavily favors Minni's SB calling range of suited broadways, clubs, connectors, and Ax suited. She has checked flop and turn, which could indicate weakness (missed draws or weak pairs) or trapping with strong hands like sets, two-pair, straights (QT), or flushes. My hand has minimal showdown value against her range and blocks few value combos. Pot control is key with deep effective stacks (~8k into 900); betting risks getting raised off equity by draws/combos or called by better, while fold equity is low post-check. Checking exploits position: realize equity cheaply if she bets river weakly, fold to aggression, or check-raise riverside if needed. Consistent with flop check-behind strategy to avoid bloating pot OOP with marginal strength. Opponents' histories show Minni calls wide but folds to aggression when missed (e.g., Hand 1,3,12).

I have 9♦8♦ on 2♣ J♣ K♣ J♥ 6♠ — no pair, no club, essentially no showdown value. Preflop I called to keep the pot manageable; both flop and turn checked through, so the preflop raiser (Elon) is more likely to have a medium-strength hand or a missed draw rather than a strong club flush (those hands typically c-bet the wet flop). The river 6♠ is a blank and the pot is only 900, so a half‑pot bluff credibly represents a slow‑played J/K or a made hand and applies pressure to many of his check‑through hands. Elon has shown the willingness to fold to aggression in prior spots, so a sized bluff is +EV. Plan: put in a half‑pot bluff to try to take it down; if he raises, I will fold.

Pocket 77 remains a bluff-catcher on this paired, draw-heavy board (KcJc2cJh6s). Minni's OOP check-check then half-pot river bet (450 into 900) after calling preflop suggests a capped range: thin value like weaker Jx, Kx without kicker, or polarized bluffs from missed clubs (e.g., AcXc, QcTc) or gutshots that didn't hit. Strong hands (flushes, QTs straight, sets, KJ) likely bet flop/turn for protection/value. My 7c blocks some weak flush combos, showdown value crushes bluffs and beats some thin value. Pot odds excellent: 450 to win 1800 (~20% equity needed, but >30% vs her polarized range). Deep stacks (8k effective), no reverse implied odds risk. History shows Minni river bets marginal/weak hands (Hand 7). Calling realizes equity cheaply, consistent with check-behind flop/turn pot control strategy exploiting position vs her wide SB calling range. Folding too tight; raising unnecessary risk without domination.

I’m on the button with T♦ J♦ — a strong, playable hand in 3-handed play with good equity and postflop playability. I want to seize initiative, isolate the blinds, and build a pot I can play in position. A standard open to 3x the big blind (300 total) pressures the small blind’s weak holdings and is a normal size given the table’s tendencies (both opponents have shown willingness to call and occasionally raise). I’m not committing a huge portion of my deep stack; plan is to c-bet favorable flops, use position to gather info, and fold/reevaluate if I face a large 3-bet. Therefore: open-raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Ten offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a relatively strong hand that warrants continuing against a Button open. Minni has been quite active and aggressive throughout the tournament. While I could 3-bet to take the initiative, calling the 250 more provides good pot odds (250 to win 700 total) and allows me to see a flop without over-committing out of position against a large stack. I will call and see how the Big Blind (Elon) reacts.

Q2o is a trash hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, dominated often. Button raise to 3x by Minni (aggressive player) and SB flat-call by Flash suggest strength ahead. Pot odds are decent (200 to win 700, ~28% implied), but equity realization is low OOP postflop. Deep stacks, better to fold and preserve stack for premium spots in tournament.

The flop is 7h 5c 5s, a paired and relatively dry board. I am out of position against Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor. King-high has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out with a bet. I will check to the aggressor, which allows me to see how she reacts to this board texture and potentially realize my equity or catch a bluff later in the hand.

I opened from the button and got a single caller; the flop is a dry, low paired board (7♥ 5♣ 5♠). With T♦J♦ I have two overcards and decent equity vs a passive caller’s check/call range, and I’m in position. Flash has shown a tendency to check‑and‑call rather than barrel or shove, so a small continuation bet will fold out pure air (AQ/AK/KQ/other overcards), extract value from worse pairs and deny equity to speculative hands while keeping the pot manageable against sets/small pairs. I’ll make a modest c‑bet (~1/3 pot) to seize initiative and preserve stack depth for later decisions; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll respect it and fold, if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

On a dry, paired flop of 7h-5c-5s, Minni's 250 bet into a 700 pot (approx. 35% pot) is a standard continuation bet that could be made with a very wide range of hands, including many that I currently beat or have decent equity against. My King-Ten high has showdown value and I am getting favorable pot odds (roughly 3.8 to 1) to call. Folding here would be overly tight, especially given the aggressive nature of the game so far. I will call to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the board development and Minni's subsequent action.

The turn has double-paired the board with Sevens and Fives (7h-5c-5s-7c). My King-high is now in a precarious position; while it beats some of Minni's pure bluffs, it loses to any pocket pair, any Ace-high, and of course any 7 or 5. Betting here serves little purpose as I'm unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call. Checking is the most standard and effective play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on Minni's action.

The turn (7h 5c 5s 7c) pairs the board and now gives any 7 or 5 a full house. My T♦J♦ is nothing more than the board two‑pair with a Jack kicker and is frequently behind. Flash has shown a consistent check/call tendency, so he can easily have a hand that now beats me (7x/5x or a slowplayed set). Betting here just bloats the pot and only gets value from worse kickers while risking a big loss to full houses. I’ll exercise pot control and check to see the river, induce potential bluffs, and avoid committing chips with a marginal hand.

The board is double-paired (7-7-5-5) with a Jack on the river. My current best hand is Two Pair, Sevens and Fives, with a King kicker. I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor. On the turn, the action went check-check, which suggests Minni likely doesn't have a monster like a Five or a strong Seven. However, King-high is very weak here, losing to any Ace-high, any pocket pair, or any Jack. While I could try to represent a Jack or a slow-played Seven by betting, Minni is an aggressive chip leader who might call with a wide range of hands or even raise me as a bluff. Checking is the most prudent move to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if she makes a significant bet, as my hand has very little value against her calling range.

I rivered two pair (Jacks and Sevens: my best five are J-J-7-7-T). The board is double‑paired (7s and 5s) so any 7x or 5x in Flash’s hand is a full house and crushes me; Jx with a kicker bigger than my Ten also beats me. That said Flash has been passive the whole hand (call on the flop, check the turn and river), which biases his range toward broadways, weak pairs and underpairs rather than an aggressive slowplayed full house. Against that line I can expect a lot of hands that will call a small river bet but that I currently beat. I want to extract value while keeping the pot from getting bloated against the hands that beat me, so I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3 pot). If he raises I will fold — I don’t want to get it in against a likely full house. Bet 400 chips.

The board has double-paired (7-7-5-5), meaning my King-high hand only plays as two pair, Sevens and Fives, with a King kicker. On this specific board texture, King-high is extremely weak because I lose to any Ace-high (which would have a better kicker), any pocket pair (which makes a better two-pair or a full house), and any Jack (which hit top pair on the river). Minni checked the turn but is now betting 400 into 1200 on the river. While the pot odds are 4:1, it is highly unlikely that Minni is betting a hand worse than King-high. Even if she is bluffing with some air, many of her 'air' hands (like Ax) actually beat me. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket tens is a premium pair in a three-handed game. Being on the button, I want to seize the initiative and build a pot. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to value-build and potentially take the blinds down or isolate one opponent.

Preflop in SB facing BTN raise to 300 (3x). Hand is J5o, offsuit, weak, poor playability, low equity vs calling range. Defending wide OOP is suboptimal with trash; standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. No reads justify speculative call given opponents' stacks and history of aggression. Pot odds 450:250 (~1.8:1 effective) not enough for postflop OOP realization.

Facing a standard button open to 300 with Jh6h in the big blind. J6s is marginal but suited with backdoor flush/straight potential and I’m deep-stacked, so folding is too tight. 3-betting OOP with this hand is unattractive — I’d be committing chips with a hand that plays poorly when called. I’ll defend by calling the extra 200 (300 total) to see a flop and proceed cautiously: continue on hearts/J/connected boards, check-fold or pot-control on dry, face-upboard aggression.

Flop: Jc 7h Qh. I hold J♥6♥ — a pair of jacks with a very strong heart draw (four hearts total). This is exactly the kind of flop I wanted to continue on: I have decent showdown value and a lot of equity against the button’s wide opening range (and I want to charge Qx/Kx/Ax and flush/straight draws). Betting denies free cards, builds the pot when I have equity, and gets value from worse jacks and some 7x/Tx hands. I size to ~60% pot (400) to give folding equity but still leave room to call a reasonable raise; facing a large check-raise I’ll seriously re-evaluate (often fold to heavy aggression since Qx can dominate me), but I’ll call smaller raises because of the strong draw+pair equity.

I started the hand with a pocket pair of Tens and raised pre-flop. The flop comes Queen-high and Jack-high, meaning there are two overcards to my pair. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 400 into a 650 pot. This bet often indicates a piece of the board (like a Jack or a Queen) or a strong draw. While my hand has lost significant value, folding immediately to one bet in a three-handed game may be too weak. I will call this bet to see a turn card and re-evaluate, as I still have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and two outs for a set, plus the possibility that Minni is betting a draw or a lower pair.

I have J♥6♥ on J♣ 7♥ Q♥ 2♥ — I turned a made J-high heart flush. Flash called my 400 flop bet out of position, so his continuing range is wide (Qx, Jx, medium pocket pairs, single-heart combos and some bluffs). Betting the turn is the right line to extract value from worse pairs and smaller hearts and to deny free rivers. I size to ~2/3 pot (1000 into 1450) — large enough to get calls from Qx/Jx/medium hearts but not so large that I automatically commit vs the very small subset of hands that currently beat me (A♥/K♥). Plan: call a modest raise to realize equity and re-evaluate on the river; fold to an unusually large shove that polarizes to A/K hearts or unlikely slow-played monsters.

On the turn, the board now shows three hearts (7h, Qh, 2h) and two overcards (Queen and Jack) to my pocket Tens. Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn, with the turn bet being a significant 1000 into a 1450 pot (approx. 70% pot). While I hold the Ten of hearts, giving me a 10-high flush draw, it is not the nut flush draw. My pocket Tens are now likely behind any Queen, any Jack, or any made flush. Given Minni's consistent aggression in this hand and the coordinated nature of the board, my hand has significantly depreciated in value. Calling here would require me to hit a specific card (a heart, a King, or a Ten) to feel comfortable, and even then, I could still be behind a higher flush or a straight. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

9c6h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and equity multiway. On the button in 3-handed play, position is good, but this hand is not strong enough to open, limp, or raise profitably given the stack depths and opponents' tendencies to defend wide. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this early tournament stage (hand 17/150).

Heads-up from the small blind with 2♣3♣. These low suited connectors are quite playable heads-up — they have good fold equity preflop, backdoor flush/straight potential, and are fine to raise with given my large stack. Limping/calling 50 hands me the flop OOP with no fold equity and invites the BB to take the initiative. A min-raise to 200 is a standard, low-risk way to apply pressure and either pick up the blind or play a multi-street pot with initiative.

Plan: raise to 200 to steal or isolate. If Flash 3-bets large (e.g. >~600–800 or an all-in) I’ll fold these weak holdings; if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable boards (especially club/low-connected textures) and control pot size on disconnected boards. The size is small relative to both stacks but maximizes fold equity and postflop maneuverability.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Jack (Jh Jd), a premium pocket pair. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 200 from the Small Blind (a min-raise). This is a strong hand that I should definitely play for a raise to build value and narrow the field, especially since we are heads-up. A 3-bet to 600 total is a standard value raise in this position to put pressure on the Small Blind and gain control of the pot.

Facing a small (to 600) 3‑bet after my 200 open with 2♣3♣, calling is the best line. Pot is 800 and I must call 400 more (600 total commitment) — ~2:1 pot odds (~33% equity). 2♣3♣ has good implied equity and backdoor club/straight potential, and in the heads‑up situation I'll have position postflop, which hugely improves playability. Flash’s 3‑bet size is not large enough to force a fold and I do not want to inflate the pot by 4‑betting with a speculative hand. Plan: call to see the flop; c‑bet or take initiative on low/club/connected boards, fold or control pot size on dry, hostile boards. If Flash 4‑bets large or shoves (well above the 1000 min‑raise line / into a majority of his stack), I will fold this holding.

I opened from the SB to 200 and called Flash’s 3‑bet to 600 with 2♣3♣. The flop (Ts Td 4c) gives me no pair but a backdoor club and tiny straight backdoors. This is a board where a small continuation bet can pick up a lot of the 3‑bettor’s missed Broadway/Ax hands and keep the pot controlled if called. Flash has shown a tendency to check/call postflop rather than fire big barrels, so a modest-sized c‑bet is the right exploitative line.

I’ll bet small to leverage fold equity while keeping the pot manageable: 400 chips (~1/3 pot). Plan if action continues: fold to a large check‑raise or shove (I have no showdown value), and if called I’ll reevaluate on the turn — only barreling when I pick up a club or the turn is very benign; otherwise check back and concede if faced with significant aggression.

I hold an overpair (JJ) on a paired board of Ts-Td-4c. I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, and Minni has led into me with a 'donk bet' of 400 (1/3 pot). This lead can represent a range of hands, including a Ten, a smaller pocket pair, or a semi-bluff with overcards. While a Ten is possible, it is not guaranteed given Minni's aggressive history. Raising here allows me to extract value from smaller pairs (like 99, 88, or 77) and protect my hand from overcards like Ace, King, or Queen that could fall on the turn. It also serves to gather information; if she reraises, I must consider that she likely has the Ten. A raise to 1000 is a standard size to put pressure on her range while maintaining pot control if she calls.

Call. I opened and called the 3‑bet preflop and made a small c‑bet (400). Flash’s raise to 1000 is a moderate check‑raise but not an all‑in — I face 600 to call into a 2600 pot, so I only need roughly 18.8% equity to break even (600/(2600+600)). With 2♣3♣ I have backdoor club and straight possibilities and I have position heads‑up, plus the 3‑bettor’s range will include bluffs/missed broadways as well as value. Raising as a bluff here is too risky and folding concedes good implied‑odds equity. I’ll call to see the turn (total flop commitment = 1000). Plan on the turn: continue only if I pick up a club or a very benign card; fold to significant aggression or a shove since I have virtually no showdown value if I miss.

Check. I called the preflop 3‑bet and the flop check‑raise with a speculative 2♣3♣ because of backdoor possibilities and position, but the Q♥ on the turn is a blank for my hand — I no longer have a realistic flush or straight draw and have essentially zero showdown value. Flash’s line (3‑bet preflop, flop check‑raise) is weighted toward value (trips/sets or strong tens/overpairs), and his check on the turn can be pot control or a trap. Bluffing the turn here is too risky vs that range; with position I’ll check and preserve chips, folding to meaningful aggression on the turn. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and only bluff if I pick up a very believable card (e.g. a club) or a clear read that he’s capable of folding.

The Queen of Hearts on the turn is a scare card for my pocket Jacks. It potentially improves hands in Minni's range like AQ, KQ, or QJ that would have called my flop raise. Since Minni has checked, checking back is the most sensible play to control the pot size and realize my equity. Betting now doesn't offer much value, as I am likely only called by better hands (Queens or Tens) while forcing out the hands I currently beat (like pocket 99s or 88s).

I missed the river — 2♣3♣ has no pair and didn’t complete a flush/straight on T♠ T♦ 4♣ Q♥ 7♠. Flash’s line (pre‑flop 3‑bet and a flop check‑raise) is very value‑heavy (tens, sets, strong two‑pair/full house combos); his checks on the turn and river look like pot control/slowplay rather than clear weakness. Without a club or a very believable scare card, a river bluff is unlikely to fold his value hands and would just burn chips. Pot is 3,200 but preserving stack is better than bluffing a likely call — I’ll check to go to showdown (and fold to significant river aggression).

Minni checked the river after a check-check turn. My pocket Jacks have high showdown value on this board (Ts Td 4c Qh 7s). While the Queen on the turn was a scare card, Minni's double check suggests she likely doesn't have a Queen or a Ten. However, betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get value from worse hands (like 88 or 99) and I risk being check-raised by a slow-played monster. Checking back allows me to take the pot at showdown without further risk.

I'm on the button with T7o in a three-handed pot and deep stacks (I have ~20.8k, blinds 50/100, opponents ~9k each). This hand is marginal but plays well in position — good candidate to open. Pot is 150 and the to-call is 100; min-raise is 200 but 3x the BB is a standard, profitable size here. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and keep the field thin so I can realize my positional advantage post-flop. I will open-raise to 300. If I face a large, non-friendly 3-bet I'll fold this holding; if I get called I'll c-bet appropriate flops and use position to extract value or fold to heavy resistance. The raise size preserves most of my stack while maximizing fold equity and post-flop maneuverability.

Queen-Nine offsuit is a marginal hand to play out of position against a button raise. While the pot odds are somewhat attractive, the lack of positional advantage and the likelihood of being dominated or facing multi-street aggression from an active player like Minni makes folding the most prudent long-term strategy here. Q9o does not realize its equity well from the small blind.

K2o is a weak offsuit king with poor postflop playability out of position against Minni's button raise. Minni raises frequently from late position with a wide range, but defending this hand OOP risks difficult spots ahead with dominated equity often and bad implied odds. Pot odds are about 2.25:1 effective (pot 450 to call 200), but equity vs. her ~40% range is ~38%, and postflop disadvantage is significant. Standard fold preserves stack for premium spots in a deep tournament.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While 3-handed play requires a wider opening range, J6o is significantly below the threshold for a standard raise from this position, especially with Minni holding a massive stack in the big blind and showing aggressive tendencies in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop, heads-up vs Minni in BB from SB with A2 suited diamonds. A2s is a standard raising hand from SB due to suited ace playability, nut flush potential, wheel straight outs, and AA blocker. OOP postflop, but small raise maintains fold equity and pot control. Minni is aggressive but defends BB wide based on history. Raising min to 200 total (add 150 more) is low variance, gets value from worse aces/suited hands, and builds pot when ahead. Folding too tight, limping invites multiway trouble OOP. Stacks deep, tournament stage favors aggression to maximize stack growth.

Elon (SB) opened to 200. I’m in the big blind with A♣ T♥. With only 100 to call (200 total committed) this is a straightforward defend — ATo is a strong heads-up hand and the price is attractive. I’d rather call and see a flop than inflate the pot out-of-position with a small min-raise (to 300) or a larger 3-bet without a specific read. Will proceed post‑flop: continue on A-high/broadway flops, be cautious and fold to heavy aggression if I miss the board.

Flop is Js Kh Ks, a paired kings board that heavily favors villain's BB defending range, which includes Kx, Jx, pocket pairs, suited broadways, and Ax. My Ad2d is pure ace-high with no pair, no straight draw (A34x needed but board doesn't help), no flush draw (though suited diamonds, board rainbow). Completely missed. As OOP SB raiser, continuation betting here is a low-EV bluff spot against a likely sticky defender like Minni (history shows wide calls/defends). Pot 400, stacks deep (9k/20k), no need to risk chips with air. Check-fold to any bet maximizes EV long-term by preserving stack for better spots. Folding preflop too tight, but postflop discipline key in tournament.

I defended preflop with A♣T♥ which is standard heads‑up vs a small 2bb open. Flop is J♠ K♥ K♠ and Elon (the preflop raiser) checked to me. His check polarizes between weakness/missed broadways and slow‑played kings, but given the tiny opener his raising range is wide — a lot of hands in his range miss this flop. ATo has limited showdown value but is a reasonable bluff/counter‑strategy here: it blocks some A‑K combinations and has decent fold equity heads‑up.

With pot = 400 I want to take the pot down now with a controlled-sized c‑bet rather than passively allow a free card or face a later stab. A half‑pot bet (~200) applies pressure to missed hands (Qx, lower broadways, small pairs) while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan if called: proceed cautiously on the turn — check and re‑evaluate unless I pair an Ace or the turn is a favourable, non‑King card; fold to significant check‑raises/raises because I’m behind to any Kx or better. If he check‑raises the flop, I’ll concede this bluff. Bet 200 to buy the pot now and preserve stack depth for better spots.

Faced with a 200 bet into 600 pot on Js Kh Ks flop holding Ad2d (ace-high, no pair, no straight/flush draw). Board heavily favors Minni's BB defending range (Kx, Jx, pairs, Ax suited/broadway). OOP as SB raiser, continuation rarely called pre but now check-fold optimal vs sticky defender like Minni. Pot odds 4:1 require ~20% equity, but ace-high <15% vs her value-heavy betting range on paired kings board (few bluffs). Calling builds pot OOP with showdown issues; raising risks stack with air vs aggro opponent who history shows defends wide/calls big. Preserve 9k stack for better spots in tournament to maximize long-term EV.

62o is one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em, with extremely low equity against any reasonable raising or limping range from SB or BB. Preflop on the button with a small pot (150) and to-call 100 (likely facing a raise or limp-raise scenario), the pot odds are insufficient (~2.5:1, requiring ~28% equity which 62o doesn't have).
