Checking risks giving free card to her draws or check-raise value; betting denies equity and takes initiative. Deep stacks allow fold equity without overcommitment. Standard exploitative play vs passive BB.

Board: 2♠ 6♣ 2♦. I hold 5♦3♠ — no pair, no real draw (only 6 outs to a lone pair). Elon’s c‑bet of 125 into the pot (now 325) is a moderately sized continuation bet. Calling 125 to see the turn would make the pot 450, so I need ~27.8% equity to break even now, but my chance to make at least a pair by the river is only about 24%. There’s also a good chance I’ll face further aggression on turn/river and my paired hand (if I hit) will often be second best against many parts of his range (he can have 2x, 6x, or overpairs). Raising as a bluff is too risky with essentially no fold equity and no real draw. This is a marginal, negative‑EV spot — consistent with my preflop plan to fold to a sizeable c‑bet with air — so I’ll fold and conserve chips for better situations.

3-handed on the button first to act pre-flop with 94o. Position is excellent, stacks are deep (107BB effective vs BB). In short-handed play, button opening range is very wide, around 60-70% of hands including most offsuit hands like 94o due to fold equity and positional advantage. Blinds will defend wide but I have initiative and post-flop position. 94o has some playability with backdoor potential and live cards. Standard small raise to build pot or take it down pre-flop. Raising to 3x BB (300) balances range and applies pressure on Minni (loose aggressive SB with big stack) and Flash (BB). Folding trash here is too tight; limping is suboptimal.

Holding 9♠2♣ — essentially the worst starting hand. Elon opened to 300 from the button; calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~36% equity to breakeven, and 9-2o has almost no showdown or postflop playability. From the small blind I’d be out of position versus the opener and could easily face a 3-bet or multiway action with a hand that can’t realize equity. With deep stacks I’ll preserve chips for better spots rather than calling or bluff-raising here. Fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The button, Elon, has raised to 300. This is a standard 3x open in a three-handed game. A6o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a wide button opening range. The pot odds are favorable (200 to call into a pot of 450), requiring approximately 31% equity, which A6o comfortably has against a typical button range. I will call to see a flop rather than 3-betting, as A6o plays reasonably well in a single-raised pot but can be difficult to manage in a large pot out of position if I am 4-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. I called a pre-flop raise from the button (Elon). The flop is Seven of Clubs, Jack of Clubs, Queen of Hearts. This board is quite coordinated and likely hits a lot of the button's opening range (Broadways, suited connectors, and pairs). I currently only have Ace-high with a weak kicker and no draw. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they react. Leading out here into a range that likely connects with this board would be inefficient.

Heads-up on the flop after my button raise and Flash's BB call. Board is Qh Jc 7c: draw-heavy with flush draw and straight draw possibilities (e.g., KT, 9T, AT). My 94o has zero equity - no pair, no draws, pure air. However, as pre-flop raiser with position, my range is perceived wide and includes value like Qx, Jx, 87s, sets, etc. Flash checks, which is standard with a wide defending range but often weak. Continuation betting maintains initiative, denies equity to draws/improvers, and exploits fold equity. Deep stacks (100+BB effective vs Flash's 88BB), but small bet sizes work well in position post-flop. Betting ~45% pot (300 into 650) balances value/bluff frequencies, targets Flash's marginal hands/draws that called pre but fold to pressure. Checking folds out too much equity and signals weakness, allowing Flash to realize equity cheaply or bluff turn. Standard c-bet spot in short-handed play.

The flop (Qh Jc 7c) connects heavily with a button raising range, including many Broadway hands and suited connectors. My hand (Ah 6s) has no pair and no real draw on this board. While I have Ace-high, calling a bet here out of position is a losing play long-term because I have very little equity against Elon's continuation betting range and will likely face further aggression on the turn. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for better spots.

Holding K♥T♠ on the button in a 3-handed game is a clear open-raise spot. KTs (king-ten offsuit) plays well postflop and dominates a lot of blind-defense ranges; with two players to act I want fold equity and to take the initiative. Both opponents are deep-stacked, but a standard 3xBB sizing is appropriate to balance value and pressure while keeping pot manageable if I get resistance. I will raise to 300 to isolate/steal the blinds and play position postflop. Plan: c-bet favourable flops (especially K/Q/T highs, dry boards) and be prepared to give up to large 3-bets — fold to heavy pressure unless I flop very strong. The 300 raise is small relative to my 20k stack and maximizes long-term EV here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. The Button (Minni) has opened with a raise to 300. Queen-Seven offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against an aggressive chip leader. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and raising is too risky with this hand strength. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

82o is one of the worst possible hands with no playability, straight potential, or suited value. Facing a button raise to 3x from Minni, who opens wide but still, this hand has poor equity (~25-30% at best) against her range and is easily dominated. Pot odds are 200 to call into 450 (2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), but folding preserves stack in BB defense spot where position is poor postflop. No reason to speculate with trash; prioritize strong hands to maximize stack long-term.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo) is a strong hand to open from the button. It has good playability and is ahead of the random range of hands the blinds will be holding. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately, or play a post-flop game with position.

9sTs is a strong suited connector with excellent playability against a button raise, which is likely a wide range including many speculative hands. Direct pot odds are favorable: calling 250 more into a 450 pot (effective pot lay ~1.8:1), and with deep stacks and good implied odds if we hit draws or pairs. The hand realizes equity well multiway, and Minni in BB may check or call light. Folding is too tight from SB vs button; 3-betting risks playing OOP with a hand that prefers multiway pots and flops. Standard defend by calling to see flop.

Three-handed: Button (Flash) opened to 300 and the SB (Elon) came along. I’m in the big blind with 5♥6♥ — a suited connector with good flop-playability but not a hand I want to inflate the pot with by 3-betting out of position. Calling 200 more (300 total) gives ~22% pot odds and lets me realize equity against a fairly wide button opening range while keeping pot manageable OOP against two players. Plan: see the flop, continue aggressively if I hit a pair/strong draw or check-fold/deny equity if the board is hostile and opponents show strength.

Flop of 8c-2s-6c gives me an open-ended straight draw (6-8-9-T, needing a 7 or J for the nuts or near-nuts) with 8 outs (~32% equity if called), plus backdoor spade flush draw for additional equity. Multiway pot (900) OOP vs button raiser (wide range: Ax, broadways, suited connectors, pairs) and BB defender (even wider). Strong draw warrants aggression to build pot, deny equity to opponents' flush draws (two clubs out), wheel draws, or weak pairs/overs. Checking risks giving free cards to Flash's bluffs/A-high or Minni's speculative hands, or check-check-check. Half-pot bet (500) gets value from worse (pairs, gutshots), fold equity from air, and sets up post-turn flexibility with SPR ~15 post-bet/call. Deep effective stacks support realizing equity.

Flop: 8♣ 2♠ 6♣ — I have 5♥6♥ for middle pair (6s) with a weak kicker. Elon (SB) leads for 500 into a 1,400 pot (~35% pot). Calling 500 is the best play here: it gives ~20–22% pot odds and my pair + backdoor possibilities have reasonable equity versus a wide SB c‑bet range (many missed overcards, club draws, small pairs). Raising to the minimum (1,000) would bloat a multiway pot out of position and invite 3‑bets from the button; folding a made hand here is too nitty. I’ll call 500 to control the pot, keep worse hands and draws in, and reassess on the turn — fold to large, polarized aggression unless I improve (5/6/heart/trips/two‑pair) and extract value when appropriate.

I raised pre-flop with JTo and both blinds called. The flop is 8c-2s-6c. The Small Blind (Elon) has led out for 500 into a 900 pot, and the Big Blind (Minni) has called. This 'donk bet' and subsequent call indicate that both players likely have a piece of this board (pairs or flush draws). I only have two overcards and no significant draw. Calling 500 into a pot where I am currently third-best with little chance of a clean improvement is not profitable. Folding is the most disciplined choice here.

Turn 7d completes the nut straight with 6-7-8-9-Ts. This is the nuts on 2s-6c-7d-8c board—no higher straight possible (next would require J for 7-8-9-T-J). Minni's BB calling range pre and flop includes pairs like 66/77/88/22 (sets or overpairs), flush draws (clubs), straight draws (e.g., 4-5, 9-x for gutshot), or weaker pairs/overs that float. Acting first OOP with deep effective stacks (~10k), a value bet extracts from her entire calling range: sets, top pair (unlikely but possible floats), draws chasing flush/str. Checking risks her checking back or giving free river card to draws. Bet ~2/3 pot (1300 into 1900) builds pot while keeping SPR reasonable (~4-5 post-call) for river jam if needed, balances vs her draws/pairs. Avoids overbetting to maximize fold equity from air while getting called by value hands.

I have 5♥6♥ on 8♣ 2♠ 6♣ 7♦ — a single middle pair with a weak kicker. Facing Elon’s 1,300 into a 3,200 pot requires ~1300/(3200+1300) ≈ 28.9% equity to break even. My clean outs to improve are: 3 fives (two pair), 2 sixes (trips) and the 4 fours + 4 nines for a straight = 13 outs → 13/46 ≈ 28.3%, which is slightly below the pot‑odds threshold. On top of that, Elon’s line (call pre, c‑bet flop, a larger turn barrel) is consistent with a range that often already beats a single six (turned 7x, 6x, sets, or strong made hands) or will not pay off many of my river improvements. Raising as a semi‑bluff (min to 2,600) would bloat a pot out of position with a marginal holding and risks a shove or call where I’m behind. I’ll preserve my big stack and fold this marginal pair rather than chase a slightly negative‑EV river or turn a marginal bluff into a larger mistake — consistent with my earlier plan to fold to large, polarized aggression unless I’d improved.

Preflop on the button in a 3-handed pot with 6h3c, one of the weakest possible hands (offsuit, disconnected low cards). Pot 150, to call 100 into approximately 250 (28% pot odds needed), but 63o has poor equity against any reasonable SB limp or BB range (~20-25% at best) and terrible postflop playability multiway. Raising to steal (min 200) risks commitment with a hand that flops nothing well and can't stand a 3bet or resistance from deep-stacked Minni (194BB) or shorter Flash (83BB). Deep effective stacks (my 121BB), no need for marginal bluffs. Standard fold preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

Elon folded so I'm heads-up with Flash in the big blind. J♦9♠ is a playable hand in a 3-handed game and I want the initiative rather than seeing a cheap flop out of position. Opening from the small blind applies pressure, gains fold equity, and lets me continuation-bet many flops. I'll make a standard-sized open to ~3x the big blind (raise to 300 total) — large enough to discourage light defenses but small relative to effective stacks so I don't overcommit with a marginal holding. If Flash 3-bets big I'll fold; if he calls I'll continue with c-bets on favorable textures and re-evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d2h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely difficult to play post-flop and rarely wins at showdown. It's better to fold and wait for a more playable hand than to bleed chips out of position with garbage.

Button with K♦5♦: a marginal but suited hand. In 3-handed play the button is very profitable to open — I want to use my positional advantage to steal the blinds rather than limp and invite multiway action. Elon (BB) has shown preflop aggression in this game, but a standard 3x open (300) puts pressure on both blinds and reduces the frequency of profitable 3-bets. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand (K5s is often dominated), but if called I can play postflop aggressively with position and take advantage of backdoor diamond equity. Raise to 300 to maximize fold equity while keeping the sizing conventional for this table.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit (J7o). Minni, the chip leader, has opened from the Button for 3x the big blind. J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position and is generally a fold in this spot, especially with the Big Blind (Elon) yet to act. Preserving my stack of 83 big blinds is more important than defending with such a marginal hand.

Qh5h is a suited queen-high hand with some playability for flush draws and straight potential. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive and raises frequently based on hand history. In the big blind, the pot odds are favorable: calling 200 more into a 450 pot (2.25:1 direct odds), and with deep stacks (12k effective for me vs 19k), there are strong implied odds if I hit. Modern BB defense vs button open is wide, and Q5s suited fits the calling range over folding, especially heads-up post-flop where I can realize equity despite acting first. Folding weak hands preserves stack for better spots, but this has enough potential to speculate profitably long-term in a tournament setting to maximize stack growth.

On the flop of As-2h-Qc, I have paired my Qh for top pair (queens) with a weak 5h kicker, plus a backdoor heart flush draw (two hearts in hand + one on board). The board is relatively dry: Ace-high with no flush draw completed except backdoors, and straight draws are weak (A-Q gutshot unlikely). Heads-up OOP vs Minni's button opening range, which is wide (per history: frequent small raises preflop, value bets flops). Pot 650, acting first. Pair of queens blocks some of her Qx value but vulnerable to Ax (her range hits ~25-30% aces), better Qx like QJ/QT, or sets. Equity ~45-55% vs her range (beats bluffs/pairs/draws, loses to Ax/overpairs). Standard OOP play with medium-strength hand (TPWK): check to pot control, induce bluffs from her wide range, realize equity post-flop. Betting risks check-raise from Ax/sets and folds out worse; checking allows cheap showdown or traps her floats. Deep stacks (11.8k effective), tournament stage favors patience to maximize stack over 150 hands—don't bloat pot OOP without nuts. If she bets small, call; larger bet, evaluate fold equity/call based on size.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called heads‑up. The flop A♠ 2♥ Q♣ misses K♦5♦ — I have no pair and only backdoor diamond equity. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation‑bet a good portion of the time to take down the pot now or charge floats and medium pairs. Because the Ace/Queen are on board I don't want to overcommit with a weak hand, so I choose a medium half‑pot c‑bet (≈325 into 650). This size gives me fold equity, keeps the pot manageable versus an opponent who often check/calls, and leaves flexibility to fold to a large check‑raise or to continue on a favourable turn (diamond or King).

Facing a 325 bet into 975 pot (~3:1 odds, requiring ~25% equity), Qh5h gives top pair queens with weak kicker and backdoor heart flush draw on As2hQc board. Minni's button range is wide per history (frequent small preflop raises, value bets flops), including Ax (~25-30% hits), Qx, pairs, draws, bluffs. My equity is strong (~45-60% vs polarized range: beats bluffs/pairs/draws KQo+/worse Qx, loses to Ax/sets). OOP but excellent pot odds and deep stacks (11.8k effective) justify calling to realize equity, induce bluffs on later streets, and chase implied odds if I improve. Raising risks bloating pot vs Ax/sets or folding out worse; folding too tight for TPTK. Standard call preserves stack for tournament longevity while exploiting her aggression.

On the turn, the board is As-2h-Qc-Kc. I hold Qh5h for top pair queens with a weak five kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw. Preflop, I defended wide OOP as BB vs button open from aggressive Minni. Called flop c-bet OOP with TPTK on a dry board (As2hQc rainbow-ish), getting good odds (~3:1). The Kc turn is a disaster card for my hand: it improves villain's wide button range significantly (KJ/KT/KQ now top pair or two pair, AK/AQ now two pair or top pair kings, clubs for flush draws completed some like AcXc). Straight draws improve (e.g., JT). My hand is now likely 2nd pair effectively, with poor equity (~30-40% vs her polarized range: loses to Kx/Ax/sets/club flushes, beats pure bluffs/worse Qx/pairs below Q). OOP first to act, pot 1300, deep stacks (~11k effective). Betting risks raising from better hands, folding out bluffs, and bloating pot OOP OOP. Standard play: check to pot control, realize equity cheaply if she checks back weak hands/draws, or face a bet where I can fold profitably to aggression (saves stack for better spots). Tournament mid-stage (hand 45/150), preserve stack to exploit edges long-term vs her aggression. No strong reason to bet; checking maximizes EV by avoiding mistakes post-turn.

I improved to top pair on the turn (K♦5♦ with K on the board) but have a very weak kicker and there's an Ace on board that dominates me. Elon has shown a tendency to call down (he called my flop c‑bet), so his range likely contains Qx, medium pocket pairs, and some Ax. Betting for value here is the correct line: I want to extract from worse kings/queens/pocket pairs and charge any club‑or‑broadway hands that can still improve, while avoiding bloating the pot too much when I'm behind to an Ace. A medium, half‑pot bet (≈650 into 1300) maximizes calls from worse hands, protects against giveaway runouts, and keeps the pot controllable if I face resistance. If I face a large raise I’ll be prepared to fold given my poor kicker; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn board of As-2h-Qc-Kc, holding Qh5h for second pair (queens) with a weak five kicker and minimal backdoor draws. Preflop defended wide OOP in BB vs Minni's button open. Flop As-2h-Qc gave TPTK, which I check-called her 325 into 650 pot at good odds (~3:1). Turn Kc is a terrible card: completes club flush draws (many in button range like AcXc, KcXc), pairs kings (KJ/KT/KQ/AK/AQ), and opens straights (JT). Minni's range, aggressive per history (frequent preflop raises, flop/turn bets), now heavily favors value: AK/AQ (two pair/straights), Kx (top pair), sets, flushes over bluffs or weak Qx. My equity drops to ~25-35% vs her polarized continuing range (beats air/bluffs/worse pairs, crushed by value). Pot odds ~3.3:1 (650 to 2600) require ~23% equity, marginal OOP where I face river aggression on scary board (possible river A/K/club/brick). Deep effective stacks (~11k), mid-tournament (45/150 hands), folding marginal made hands preserves stack for premium spots vs her aggression. Calling risks bloating pot OOP without showdown value or improvement outs (no strong draws). Raising suicidal vs value range. Standard fold exploits her wide range long-term by not paying off.

92o is a very weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game on the button, it is not profitable to open or call with this hand against active opponents like Elon and Minni. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

AKo is a premium hand, one of the best starting hands. In small blind vs big blind with button folded, this is a clear raising spot to build the pot for value, take initiative, and potentially isolate or steal if Minni folds. Minni has shown aggression but defends wide in BB. Standard raise size of 3x the BB (300 total) balances value and fold equity while keeping pot manageable out of position.

Facing a small‑blind open to 300, I’m in the big blind with T♣4♠. The extra 200 to call into a 400 pot only gives ~2:1 pot odds (need ≈33% equity), and T4o has very poor equity vs a standard SB opening range and will be hard to play out of position. A raise would be a thin, speculative play and risks bloating the pot with a dominated hand. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot with position or a stronger holding.

3-handed on the button with J8o, a speculative hand with straight potential and good playability postflop. Position is excellent, stacks are deep (mine 116BB, opponents deeper/shorter). Pot is 150 with 100 to call (likely unraised blinds or limp), offering good implied odds but limping is passive. Raising wide is standard here to steal blinds or play in position. Min raise 200 is small, but sizing to 300 (3x BB) balances stealing and value. History shows opponents fold to button raises often (e.g., hand 46). Folding too tight, calling too passive.

Three-handed I want to be proactive with JcTc suited — a strong, playable hand that fares well as a 3-bet to isolate the Button and take initiative. The pot is 450 and a flat call costs 250, which keeps me OOP and lets Elon realize a wide opening range. I prefer a standard 3-bet sizing (~3x the open) to apply pressure on Elon and discourage the big blind from coming along cheaply. A raise to 900 is a small fraction of my deep stack but gives good fold equity; if called I can continue on favorable flops or fold to heavy 4-bets depending on sizing. Execute a 3-bet to isolate and play the flop with initiative.

I am holding King-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button opened for 3x, and the Small Blind has put in a 3-bet to 900. K7o is significantly below the range required to continue in a 3-bet pot, especially when playing out of position against an aggressive 3-better like Minni. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 (600 to call into pot of 1300) with J8o offsuit from the button. While position postflop is advantageous and the hand has straight potential, it lacks suitedness, blockers, or showdown value against Minni's likely 3-bet range from SB (QQ+, AKo/AJs+, mid pairs, some suited broadways/bluffs). Pot odds are roughly 2.17:1 direct (~3.17:1 effective after call), but reverse implied odds are high as I'm often dominated by better broadways or drawing dead to sets. 4-betting bluff has fold equity but risks stacks with a marginal hand; her deep stack (192BB) and history of aggression (e.g., frequent raises/3-bets) suggest she continues wide. Deep tournament stage (hand 47/150, 112BB), fold preserves stack for premium spots. Standard fold.

8♦3♠ is a very weak hand, but in 3‑handed play the button should be opening a very wide range. With deep stacks (≈205bb) I want to seize initiative and generate fold equity rather than limp. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) is my choice — it’s big enough to deny good odds to callers and avoid inviting free or cheap flops that a min‑raise would create. Plan: take the lead — c‑bet small on dry, favorable flops; proceed cautiously on coordinated boards; fold to a large 3‑bet with this garbage holding. Raise to 300 to try and steal the blinds / play position postflop if called.

I am holding 3h 5s in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. The player on the Button (Minni) has raised to 300. There is no strategic justification for calling or raising with this hand out of position, especially with a healthy stack of over 80 big blinds. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

92o is a very weak offsuit hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Defending the BB OOP against a button raise from an aggressive player like Minni is not profitable. Hand ranks poorly in preflop charts (bottom ~60% of hands). Preserve stack for better opportunities; folding is standard and +EV.

I have been dealt a premium pocket pair (Jack of Clubs and Jack of Spades) on the button. With 81.5 big blinds, this is a strong hand to open the action. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand from position, while pricing out weaker holdings from the blinds.

Facing a button raise to 300 from Flash, who tends to open wide from late position based on hand history. My hand Qh Td is offsuit queen-ten, which is marginal at best out of position in the small blind. Calling 250 OOP risks playing multiway against BB and gives up position. Equity against a wide button range is decent but playing postflop OOP with this hand is tough, especially with Minni likely to defend wide in BB. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deepstack tournament phase. No strong reason to 3-bet as it's not in my premium shoving or value range.

Call — I will call 200 more (300 total). J♦8♣ is marginal but defensible from the big blind vs a single-button raise when stacks are deep. The pot odds are reasonable (200 into ~450), and the button's opening range is likely wide enough that J8o has playable equity and post‑flop options. I don't want to 3‑bet with this offsuit hand and build a big OOP pot where I'm often dominated. Plan: play cautiously on the flop — fold to heavy aggression on dry boards if I miss, but continue/raise when I hit a pair, two pair, or strong draws and extract thin value when appropriate.

Check. I defended the big blind with J♦8♣ preflop but on Ad Ah 6s I have no pair, no draw and I’m out of position vs the button raiser. Flash’s opening range contains lots of A‑x and strong hands that now have trips or good showdown equity, so bluffing OOP is low EV and I don’t want to bloat the pot. I’ll check to keep the pot small, fold to large/committed aggression, but will call small c‑bets (rough threshold ~200 chips, ≈1/3 pot) because I have ~6 outs (~24% to pair by river) and the pot odds justify a light call. If Flash checks back I’ll see a free turn and re‑evaluate — if I pair or pick up real equity I’ll become more aggressive, otherwise continue to protect my stack.

I have a strong pocket pair with Jacks, but the flop comes with two Aces. On a paired board like AA6, my hand has high showdown value but is vulnerable to any Ace in the Big Blind's range. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow Minni to potentially bluff on later streets with a wider range of hands that I currently beat. It also protects me from a check-raise that would force me to fold the best hand. I will use this street to realize my equity and evaluate the turn card.

Check. I have J♦8♣ on Ad Ah 6s 5s — no pair, no meaningful draw and only ~6 outs to pair on the river (≈13% equity). Flash checked the flop, so his range is likely a mix of broadways, Ax and some pocket pairs; many of those beat any single pair I might make. With the pot at 650 a call would need to be ≤≈97 chips to be +EV given my equity, and the minimum bet here is 100, so I should not call a bet. Betting myself OOP with virtually no equity is a low‑EV bluff that can be raised off my hand. Plan: check to see the river for a free card; fold to any meaningful turn bet (≥100). If the river pairs me (J/8) I can reevaluate for thin value, otherwise keep the pot small and preserve stack.

I am holding pocket Jacks on a board of AA65. After checking back the flop for pot control, Minni has checked again on the turn. While my hand is likely the best, the presence of two Aces makes it a 'way ahead or way behind' situation. If Minni has an Ace, she is unlikely to fold to any bet, and if she doesn't, she has very few outs against me. Checking back the turn continues my strategy of pot control and allows me to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate a river bet. It also avoids a potential check-raise that would force me off a high-equity hand.

Check. On Ad Ah 6s 5s 7h with J♦8♣ I have no pair, no backdoor draw and the river didn’t help. Flash checked both flop and turn after opening preflop, which is consistent with either a slow‑played Ace, a cautious Ace‑x, or a medium pair — all of which either beat me or will call a small river bluff frequently. The pot is only 650 and my bet would need significant fold equity to be +EV; given his line that fold equity is low. I preserve a healthy stack and avoid a low‑EV bluff that will often be called or get raised. If he now checks back we go to showdown; if he leads out I can fold without further damage. Action: check (0).

I have pocket Jacks on a board of Ad-Ah-6s-5s-7h. After raising pre-flop and checking the flop and turn for pot control, Minni has checked for a third time on the river. My Jacks represent high showdown value, but the presence of two Aces on the board makes this a classic 'way ahead or way behind' scenario. While I could potentially bet for thin value against a mid-pair like 88-TT or a 7, those hands might not always call, and a bet could provoke a bluff check-raise or run into a slow-played Ace. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and realize the current pot equity without further risk.

Preflop on the button with J♥5♣ offsuit, a speculative trash hand with poor playability, low equity against any calling range, and no strong blockers or suited/connectors to justify a call or raise. Pot is 150, to call 100 offers ~2.5:1 odds, but implied odds are minimal postflop with this hand due to domination risks and difficulty making strong hands. Opponents have shown aggression in blinds, but folding preserves stack for better spots in a long tournament (50/150 hands). Standard fold to avoid negative EV play.

3-handed, I'm in the small blind with Q♠9♥. Against a single big blind opponent this is a reasonable open — I want to seize the initiative, apply fold equity, and prevent the BB from seeing flops cheaply. Deep stacks allow postflop play, but calling 50 would give me a marginal hand out of position; a standard 2x open to 200 isolates and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: raise to 200; c‑bet good flops, proceed cautiously vs aggression and fold to a large 3‑bet unless I pick up favorable odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5s. Elon has folded from the button, and Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more to see the flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. In this Small Blind vs. Big Blind dynamic, I will have position post-flop. While 85o is a weak hand, the price and the advantage of being in position make it a reasonable call against a wide small blind range.

Check. The flop A♣ K♠ 4♦ completely misses Q♠9♥ — no pair, only a remote backdoor spade. Flash’s preflop check‑call from the big blind represents a wide calling range that includes a lot of A‑x and K‑x combos which dominate me, so a continuation bet out of position has poor fold equity and risks bloating a pot I’m likely behind in. With deep stacks and three players, I prefer pot control: check and see how the BB reacts. Plan: fold to significant aggression, call small probe bets to see a turn cheaply and re‑evaluate (look to bluff or fold catch on very favorable turn runouts), and only turn aggression into a shove if I pick up real equity or a clear read of weakness.

I am in position in a blind-vs-blind battle on an Ace-King-Four board. My hand, 8d 5s, has zero showdown value and no drawing potential. Minni, the pre-flop raiser, has checked. This board texture is typically very good for a pre-flop raiser's range, so Minni's check could be a trap or a total miss. Since I have no equity, betting would be a pure bluff. However, on such a dry board with two high cards, it is difficult to represent a hand that Minni wouldn't expect me to have called with pre-flop. I will check back to take a free turn card and see how the action develops, rather than risking chips on a low-percentage bluff.

Check. Q♠9♥ completely missed A♣ K♠ 4♦ 8♠ — no pair, no realistic draw (a 4th spade on the river still won’t make a five‑card flush), so I have essentially zero showdown equity. Flash called preflop and has checked flop and turn, which is consistent with a wide but mostly dominating range (A‑x, K‑x, medium pairs) or a passive/slow‑played made hand. Betting out of position here will rarely fold enough better hands and will only bloat a pot I’m likely behind in, so I prefer pot control. Plan: check and concede the initiative — fold to significant turn aggression; call very small probes (≈≤200, about half the pot) to see the river since those are cheap, but fold to pot‑sized or larger bets. On the river I’ll re‑evaluate: consider a thin value/bluff only if a favorable card (Q/10 or other runout that credibly improves me) appears or if Flash gives clear signs of weakness.

On the turn, the 8s gives me middle/bottom pair. Minni has checked for the second time on a board that should favor her pre-flop opening range (Ace-King high). This suggests she is likely weak or potentially trapping with a monster, though the latter is less probable given the dry texture. With my pair of eights, I now have some showdown value. Betting here doesn't necessarily accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold, and I'm ahead of most of her air. I will check back to realize my equity and try to reach a cheap showdown.

I check. On A♣ K♠ 4♦ 8♠ 6♦ my Q♠9♥ never improved — no pair and no realistic showdown equity. Flash’s line (check‑call preflop, check flop and turn) is consistent with a lot of A‑x, K‑x and medium pairs that dominate me and will rarely fold to a thin river bluff. The pot is small (400) relative to stacks, and betting here is a negative‑EV bluff against a passive caller. Plan: check and fold to any meaningful bet; only consider calling an extremely tiny probing bet (unlikely given his line).

On the river, the board is Ac-Ks-4d-8s-6d. I have a pair of eights. Minni, who raised pre-flop from the small blind, has checked every street (flop, turn, and now river). While the board contains two overcards to my eights, Minni's passive line suggests she does not have a strong hand like an Ace or a King, as those would typically bet for value on such a dry board. By checking back, I can realize my showdown value against her missed draws or lower pairs (like 6x or 4x) without risking a check-raise or losing chips to a slow-played monster.

I raise to 300. With 8♦6♦ on the button in a three-handed game I should be opening a wide range — suited connectors are strong in position and gain a lot of value when I can take the initiative. I'm very deep (~200 BB), so I want fold equity and the chance to play postflop in position; a 3x-ish open (300 total) is a standard sizing that pressures the blinds but keeps postflop play manageable if called. Plan: if both blinds fold I take down the pot; if I'm called I'll c-bet favorable textures (especially diamond/connected boards) and pot-control or check behind on high, dry boards. If I face a large, polarized 3-bet (much larger than a standard 3-bet, e.g. ~3x my raise), I'll fold 8-6s; I can consider calling smaller, non-polarized 3-bets to see a flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs Th, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Minni opened for 300 from the Button, a standard raise that likely represents a wide range of hands. While QTo is somewhat vulnerable out of position, it is too strong to fold. I have the option to 3-bet, but given Minni's deep stack and aggressive playstyle, I prefer to call and see a flop to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the board texture before committing more chips.

Qd2c is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity multiway, and no position after the flop. Facing a button raise from Minni (likely wide range) and a small blind call from Flash, folding the big blind defense is standard to preserve stack for premium spots. Pot odds of ~3.5:1 aren't sufficient given the low equity (~20-30% vs ranges) and future playability issues in a 3-way pot.

I have missed the flop with Queen-high. This board (9-4-6) is relatively neutral but does not connect with my hand. As the caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play. I will evaluate my next move based on Minni's action if they choose to bet.

I have 8♦6♦ on 9♣ 4♥ 6♠ — middle pair (6s + 6d) with backdoor diamond potential, and I'm in position after opening preflop. Flash checked, so a continuation bet is standard: this is a fairly dry board and he called preflop with a wide range (overcards, small pairs, draws). A small c-bet extracts value from worse pairs/weak made hands and charges floats while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance. I size to ~1/3–1/2 pot to deny free cards to overcards but avoid bloating the pot with a vulnerable hand. If I face a large, polarized raise I'll fold; if I'm called I'll re-evaluate on the turn and pot-control or continue barreling on favorable runouts (diamonds/low cards).

I have completely missed this flop with Queen-high and possess no meaningful draws on a dry board texture of 9-6-4. While I have two overcards, calling a continuation bet out of position against an aggressive opponent like Minni is generally unprofitable, as I will likely face another bet on the turn. Given the lack of equity and poor positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Q5o. While a 3-handed game allows for a wider opening range, Q5o is quite weak, especially against Minni, who has a massive stack and has shown a willingness to defend her big blind or even 3-bet. Given the current tournament situation and the need to preserve my stack for more favorable hands, folding this hand is the most disciplined play.

3-handed, button (Flash) folds, facing BB (Minni). Posted SB (50), pot 150, calling 50 more gives 3:1 odds. T3s suited has playability, straight/flush potential, decent equity vs BB defending range. OOP postflop but deep stacks (110BB effective vs Minni's 200BB). Completing wide is standard here; folding too tight, raising weak. Will see flop cheap and play postflop based on board.

I have J♦5♠ in the big blind heads-up after SB (Elon) completed to 50. This is a weak, unsuited hand that is frequently dominated and I’ll be out of position postflop (he’s the button). A minimum raise to 200 to try a blind-steal is unnecessary and too risky against a caller-heavy opponent with deep stacks — I don’t want to build a pot OOP with marginal equity. I’ll check to control the pot and see a flop; then fold to significant aggression if I completely miss, or continue/call small bets if I pick up equity or get good odds.

Flop board is 2h-8d-Td, giving me top pair (Tens) with a terrible 3 kicker and only backdoor heart flush draw. OOP first to act into a checked BB with wide range. Board is draw-heavy (diamond flush draw, straight draws like J9, 96). Betting thin top pair weak kicker risks getting raised off by better hands (TT+, 8x, draws) or check-raised. Checking allows pot control, induces bluffs from Minni's wide range (Ax, low pairs, pure draws), and lets me realize equity cheaply if checked back or facing small bet. Folding to large bet later is fine given vulnerability. Standard check here to play postflop fit-or-fold.

Check. I have J♦5♠ on 2♥ 8♦ T♦ — essentially one overcard and a very weak backdoor diamond possibility; no pair, no clean one-card draw, and I’m out of position. Elon checking the flop shows weakness or a trap, but I don’t want to build a pot OOP with marginal equity. Pot is small (200) and there’s no price to see the turn, so I’ll control the pot and take a free card. Plan: fold to sizable aggression on the flop/turn, call small probes only if the price and equity justify it, and re-evaluate if checked to on the turn.

Turn board brings the 6h, completing my nut flush draw (four hearts: 2h, 3h, 6h, Th) while keeping my pair of tens. This gives me ~35-40% equity vs most of Minni's checking range (worse pairs like 77-99, Ax, bluffs, weaker draws). OOP first to act into checked pot, betting accomplishes multiple goals: gets value from weaker hands/draws that will call (e.g., 8x, low pairs, diamond draws, gutshots), charges diamond flush draw/straight draws (J9, 97, 75 etc.), protects against runner-runner outs, builds pot for when I hit flush/river value, and denies equity to her floats/bluffs. Checking risks giving free card to her draws or allowing free showdown with worse. Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (130ish) balances value and semi-bluff from my SB limping range (suited connectors, low pairs that hit pair/draw). Deep stacks allow fold equity if raised. Strong spot to bet.

I hold J♦5♠ on 2♥-8♦-T♦-6♥ — no pair and no realistic draw (the turn being a heart means a diamond flush is now impossible). I have only 6 outs (three J’s and three 5’s) ≈ 13% to improve on the river. Elon bet 125 into a pot that was ~200 (pot now 325), so a call of 125 is offering ~2.6:1 pot odds (~27% required equity). My ~13% equity is well below the threshold, and even if I hit I’m often second-best to tens/eights/other pairs. I’m out of position and earlier plan was to fold to significant turn aggression. This is a clear negative-EV spot to call or bluff-raise, so I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with Qs5c offsuit. This is a standard raising hand in a short-handed game as button open ranges are very wide (50%+ of hands), including all queen-x offsuit. Excellent position, blinds are 50/100, pot 150, raising to steal or play postflop profitably. Deep effective stacks, but low variance play early in tournament. Opponents defend blinds sometimes but fold frequently to button opens based on history (e.g., many folds to raises). Raise to 3x BB standard size for initiative and fold equity. Postflop, can c-bet or fold to resistance.

Facing a button open to 300 with 3♦6♠ in the small blind. This hand is very weak — unpaired, poorly connected and with no meaningful blockers — and will be dominated a large portion of the time by the button’s opening range. I must call 250 more to make the pot ~700, so my break-even equity on a call is roughly 250/700 ≈ 36%. 3♦6♠ does not have the equity or post‑flop playability to meet that threshold comfortably, and I’ll be out of position postflop versus the opener (with the big blind still to act behind me), making postflop lines difficult. A light 3‑bet is possible in some spots, but with no blockers and a competent opponent I’m unlikely to get enough fold equity to make it profitable. With deep stacks I can wait for much better spots. Fold and conserve chips for a stronger situation.

I have Jc Tc in the Big Blind. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni folded her Small Blind. Jc Tc is a strong, playable hand with good post-flop potential, especially in a head-up situation. The cost to call is 200 chips into a current pot of 450, providing me with favorable pot odds (better than 2:1). Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing pre-flop against a deep-stacked opponent who has been active.

On this dry, disconnected flop of 3s-9h-4d, I have two overcards (Jack and Ten). As the defender in the Big Blind against a button raise, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I will see if Elon decides to continuation bet. If he checks back, my overcards might still be the best hand or I can potentially realize equity on the turn. Leading into the raiser on this board doesn't provide much strategic benefit.

3-handed, button vs BB postflop after my preflop raise. Flash checked flop. Board 3s-9h-4d is rainbow, low paired-ish potential but mostly draws and weak hands. Qs5c has double gutshot straight draw (2 or 6, 8 clean outs ~32% to hit by river, ~17% turn), backdoor spade flush draw, and queen-high. Equity solid (~30-40% vs BB calling range). Deep stacks (108BB eff with Flash), position, initiative: standard c-bet to deny equity, take pot (~50% fold equity vs BB check), or build if called. History shows Flash folds frequently to postflop aggression (e.g., Hands 13,25,36,37). Bet ~45% pot (300 into 650) for value from Ax/KQ/etc. worse, bluffs well with equity. Check back risks giving free card to overcards/draws. Avoids overbetting early tournament.

On this dry 3s-9h-4d flop, my Jc Tc represents two overcards. Elon's 300 continuation bet into a 650 pot is roughly half-pot. While I don't have a made hand or a direct draw, I have 1.5:1 pot odds and my overcards (J, T) may be live if Elon is betting with air or a low pocket pair. Folding here would be too tight against a Button raiser on such a disconnected board, but raising is unnecessary as I want to keep his bluffs in. I will call to see a turn card, hoping to hit a Jack, Ten, or a backdoor straight/flush opportunity.

The turn King of Hearts is a card that significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Having called a flop continuation bet with Jack-high and no direct draw, my position has weakened. I will check to see how Elon reacts. If he bets, I will likely have to fold, as I have no showdown value and very little equity on this board.

Heads-up on turn vs Flash (BB) after my button open-raise got one caller, flop c-bet called. Board 3s-9h-4d-Kh: king brings possible AxK, Kx pairs, or blank. My Qs5c is queen-high with backdoor spade flush draw and negligible straight outs now (river 2/6 unlikely to be clean). No pair, no strong draw (~10-15% equity vs calling range heavy on 9x, 44+, Kx, sets, draws that missed). Flash check-check suggests possible weakness (missed draw, middle pair, ace-high), but BB defense range is wide and king hits enough to check strength. Deep effective stacks (~78BB), position: checking preserves stack, induces bluffs or controls pot when behind. Betting thin value risks calling station call or getting raised; pure bluff poor with low fold equity post-call and scary turn. History shows Flash passive/check-calls but folds to aggression sometimes (e.g., Hands 16,28); showdown equity decent if weak (beats Jx, lower pairs). Check back standard to realize equity or see free river card cheaply (pot 1250 into 10k+ stacks). Avoid spew early-mid tournament.

The river is the 6d, completing a board of 3s-9h-4d-Kh-6d. I have Jack-high, which has virtually no showdown value. Elon checked back on the turn after c-betting the flop, which typically suggests he has a hand with some showdown value (like a pair of 9s, 4s, or a middle pocket pair) or he has given up on the hand. While I could attempt a bluff here to push him off Ace-high or a weak pair, Elon has shown himself to be a capable and active player, and a single bet may not be enough to induce a fold from the hands that currently beat me. Checking is the most prudent play. If he checks back, I get to see the showdown for free; if he bets, I will fold as my hand cannot beat any value bets.

River board 3s-9h-4d-Kh-6d completes some straight draws (e.g., 5x for 2-6 wheel, or gutshots to 5-7 etc.) and adds a third diamond but unlikely flush. My Qs5c is pure queen-high no pair, no straight (3-4-5-6 unfilled without fifth card), no flush. Flop check-call by Flash (BB defending wide) suggests capped range: 9x, 44, 33, Ax (missed K turn), weaker Kx, missed draws now checking down. Turn check-check mutual, river check by Flash again indicates weakness unwilling to bet (no strong Kx/value). Deep effective stacks (74BB with Flash), pot 1250 small relative. Queen-high has good showdown value vs polarized check range (beats Jx/Tx/A-high without K, lower pairs/draws). Betting thin value (e.g., 500-700) risks calls from 9x/sets/Kx or folds from worse; low fold equity post multi-street passive play. Bluff worse with no blockers. Checking realizes equity safely, induces if trapping (unlikely), preserves stack in mid-tournament grind vs passive opponent who check-calls light but showdowns (history: Hands 2,8,14,17,24,27,38,49,50,52). Consistent with turn check to control pot/see showdown.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed pot with Jc6h offsuit. With deep stacks (~204 BB) and only two opponents (SB and BB), I should be opening fairly wide — position is a big advantage post-flop. Limping/calling 100 surrenders initiative and invites multiway action where J6o is harder to play. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total) applies pressure to the blinds, reduces callers compared with the min-raise, and preserves fold equity. If I face a sizable 3-bet from either opponent I’ll fold this marginal hand; if I get callers I’ll play post-flop in position looking for top pair, a jack, or favorable runouts. Therefore I raise to 300 to take the initiative and steal the blinds or play in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit (Td 6c). Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser. While the pot odds are roughly 2:1 to call, the hand has poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Pocket sevens is a premium hand heads-up against a button raise from Minni, who has a wide opening range. We have excellent equity against their range (~75-80%+). Facing a fold from SB Flash, this is an ideal spot to 3-bet for value, build the pot, and apply pressure while denying equity to dominated hands like Ax, Kx, Qx. Out of position but deep stacks allow postflop playability if called. Standard sizing is 3x the raise to 900. Folding or calling is too weak; we want to charge draws and worse pairs.

I opened to 300 on the button with Jc6h to take the initiative and play a hand in position, but Elon 3-bet to 900. The pot is 1250 and I must call 600 more, which gives me ~32% pot-equity required to justify a call. J6o is a marginal holding that is heavily dominated by a typical BB 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, Ax) and rarely has sufficient equity or good playability deep-stacked. A min 4‑bet to 1500 as a bluff is possible in some spots, but with poor blockers and deep stacks it’s an overly risky line here. I’ll preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot in position — fold.

I am on the button with Qh 6c, which is a marginal hand even in a three-handed game. With both Elon and Minni showing significant aggression in recent hands and having larger stacks than me, I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips rather than opening a pot and potentially facing a 3-bet or playing a weak hand post-flop against active opponents.

Flash folded on the button, leaving heads-up versus Minni in the big blind. K4o is a marginal hand but provides excellent pot odds: 50 to call into a 150 pot (3:1). Against a typical BB defending range, K4o has around 55-60% equity heads-up. While out of position postflop is a disadvantage, the low cost of completing minimizes risk, and the king blocker adds value against king-heavy ranges. Deep stacks (mine 12k, Minni 20k) offer implied odds if I connect. Folding squanders the odds; raising risks variance OOP with a hand prone to domination. Limping/ calling is balanced and exploits Minni's history of wide defense and passive play in spots.

We’re heads-up vs the small blind after the button folded. I hold pocket fives — a medium pocket pair that wants to either take the pot now or play a controlled pot (set-mining) vs a wide calling range. Elon completed the small blind, so his range is wide; raising gives me fold equity and builds value vs worse pairs and broadways. I’ll raise to 300 total (i.e. add 200 to my posted BB) — a standard, not tiny sizing that still keeps SPR manageable OOP. If he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and check back dry boards to protect my set-mining plans.

Facing a standard BB raise after SB limp. Pot odds are excellent: risking 200 to win 400 (pot will be 600 after call, ~3:1 odds, breakeven ~25% equity). K4o offsuit holds ~40-45% equity against Minni's probable wide raising range (pairs, suited connectors, Ax, broadways) based on her history of frequent preflop aggression after limps but passive postflop check-calls. King blocker reduces her KK/AK heavily. Deep effective stacks (~120BB) provide implied odds if king hits or we hit draws. Position is a disadvantage OOP, but low cost minimizes risk and allows playing postflop fittingly (bet strong hands, check-fold weak floats). Folding squanders equity and odds; 3-betting increases variance with a marginal hand prone to domination OOP. Calling balances range, exploits Minni's tendencies (e.g., Hands 1,3,5,9+ where she raises but often gets called/folded to lightly). Goal: maximize stack long-term by realizing equity in favorable spots.

First to act OOP on a flop of 2d 3d Jh with Kd4c (king-high, no pair, no draws, wrong suit for flush draw). Board is draw-heavy with diamond flush draw and potential gutshots (e.g., 45 for wheel). Preflop, I defended wide OOP due to excellent odds and deep stacks for implied odds. Postflop, king-high has limited showdown value (beats bluffs/unimproved hands but loses to Jx, pairs, sets) and blocks Minni's strong Ax/Kx value. Her BB raise range vs SB limp is polarized wide: value (AJ+, pairs), bluffs/semi-bluffs (suited connectors, Ax suited, wheel blockers). Betting risks folding out better equity realizations and bloating pot OOP with marginal hand; vulnerable to check-raise from Jx/draws. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs/checks from weak range, allows free card if I improve (backdoor KD or runner-runner), and sets up cheap fold to bet from strong range. Exploits Minni's history of passive check-calls postflop (e.g., Hands 8,17,22) while realizing equity long-term. Deep stacks (118BB) favor seeing turns cheaply. Goal: avoid variance, maximize EV over 150 hands by pot control in tough spot.

I opened preflop and got called by the small blind; heads-up the 2♦-3♦-J♥ flop is fairly dry with two diamonds. I hold 5♠5♥ — a medium pair that beats most of his unpaired calling range (A/K/Qx, many single-card broadways) but loses to Jx, sets (22/33) and higher pairs. As the preflop aggressor I should c-bet to take the pot down now and charge worse hands/draws. A ~½-pot bet (300 into 600) applies pressure, folds out overcards, and keeps weaker pairs and diamond draws priced to call while preserving SPR. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises large I’ll likely fold given the vulnerability of a middle pair.

Facing a half-pot bet of 300 into 900 OOP on a draw-heavy flop (2d3dJh) with Kd4c (king-high + weak gutshot to the 5 for bottom straight, backdoor KD). Pot odds require ~25% equity. My hand has limited equity (~20-30% vs Minni's BB betting range post-check: Jx, sets, two-pair, strong diamond draws like AdXd/KdXd/45d suited, pairs; bluffs less frequent given her history of aggressive preflop but selective postflop betting into checks). Kd blocks some value, but vulnerable to most of range, and low-end gutshot pays poorly if hit (beats bluffs but loses to Jx/flushes). OOP multi-street play risky: pot bloats to 1200, turn OOP likely check-fold to further aggression. Deep stacks don't help much with poor implied odds (rarely stack off profitably). History shows Minni bets for value/protection (e.g., Hands 3,4,12,16); check-calling her light previously exploited passivity, but calling here builds tough river spots. Folding realizes fold equity spent preflop cheaply, preserves stack for better spots in 95+ hands left, avoids variance OOP with marginal equity.

Preflop on the button with KTo offsuit, a strong hand in position against a likely limped pot (pot 150, to call 100 suggests SB completed or small raise, BB checked). Raising is standard to take initiative, build the pot with position, and thin the field. KTo plays well postflop with straight potential and king blocker. Raising to 300 (3x BB size) is a good balanced size, denying equity to worse hands while keeping deep stack playability. Calling risks multiway pot OOP for opponents; folding too tight on button.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with K♠J♥. Pot is 450 and I need 250 more to call (300 total). KJ is a playable hand heads-up but is marginal to 3-bet out of position — against a button open I prefer pot control and to see a flop rather than inflate the pot OOP with a hand that can be dominated by better broadways. With deep stacks and a wide button opening range, calling keeps weaker hands in, preserves fold equity on favorable flops, and lets me realize equity on K/J-high flops or strong draws. If Flash folds and it’s heads-up, Elon will be in position — that’s fine; if Flash defends or Elon 4-bets, I’ll re-evaluate (and likely fold to heavy 4-bet aggression). Decision: call 250 more (300 total committed).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven of spades (As7s). Elon (Button) raised to 300 and Minni (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to close the action. Ace-Seven suited is a strong hand that plays well in multi-way pots, particularly because of its flush and top-pair potential. While a 3-bet is an option to thin the field, calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and realize my hand's equity without inflating the pot while out of position against two active opponents.

I have K♠J♥ and the flop is 2h–5d–Jc: top pair with a very strong kicker. This is a multiway pot (Elon open from BTN, Flash defended), so I want to both extract value from worse jacks and medium pairs and deny equity to the few straight/runner draws (3-4 type combos) that can outdraw me. The board is relatively dry, so a value/protection bet is appropriate rather than checking and giving free cards. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (450) — big enough to charge worse Jx and small pairs and to make speculative draws pay, but not so big that I fold out too much thin value. Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and continue to value-bet thinly; if face a large check-raise I’ll give credit to a very strong hand and likely fold given two opponents and multiway dynamics.

I am currently holding Ace-high on a Jack-high board. Minni has led out with a 450 bet into two players, including the pre-flop raiser (Elon). This 'donk' bet often indicates a made hand like a Jack or a medium pocket pair. I only have one overcard and very weak backdoor draws. Furthermore, Elon is still to act behind me, creating a risk of being squeezed or facing further aggression. Calling with Ace-high in this sandwich position is not profitable in the long run. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack.

Flop of 2h-5d-Jc completely misses my K♦T♣ range. No pair, no straight or flush draw, just king-high with poor showdown value. Minni's 450 donk-bet into 900 pot from SB after calling my button raise suggests they have equity: likely Jx, sets (JJ/55/22 unlikely but possible), or draws like 43o/s. Pot odds ~3:1 but implied odds poor heads-up deep with no hand. Raising as bluff has fold equity but Minni aggressive and calls wide per history; better spots exist OOP for villain. Position helps but can't realize equity profitably. Fold preserves deep stack (115BB) for 94 hands left.

On the button with 2♣8♠ this is a weak hand, but button position and deep stacks make it profitable to open a wide stealing range. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 100 to call, or I can raise to 200 (a 2xBB open) to apply pressure and pick up the blinds. I prefer a small standard open-raise: it gives fold equity versus the SB (Flash) who has shown a tendency to fold preflop, and even if the BB (Elon) defends I play postflop in position. Risk is limited (only +100 invested), and I will fold to a sizable 3-bet or proceed cautiously if called (c-bet small on favorable flops, check/control or fold to heavy aggression on others). Raise to 200 to try to take the pot or play a favorable positional postflop game.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah7d. Minni, the chip leader, has opened with a 2x raise from the button. Ace-Seven offsuit is a solid hand to defend in this spot, especially given the favorable pot odds (calling 150 to play for a 500 pot). While 3-betting is an option to take the initiative, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the board develops, as I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

Preflop in the big blind with 5c6h offsuit, a marginal speculative hand at best. Facing a button raise to 200 from Minni (who has been aggressive) and a call from Flash in the small blind, this will likely go multiway. My hand has poor playability out of position, low equity against likely calling ranges, and no strong implied odds. Pot odds are about 5:1 (100 to call into 500), but postflop with two opponents OOP, I expect to lose money on average. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament with 150 hands remaining.

On this low, disconnected flop (6d-4h-2s), I have Ace-high with an overcard. As the caller out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most prudent play. This allows me to gauge Minni's strength and potentially see a turn card cheaply if they check back, or decide whether to peel a bet depending on the sizing.

The flop is very dry (6♦ 4♥ 2♠) and I’m the preflop aggressor with a small pair (2♣) in position. A small continuation bet ~1/3 pot (150) is the best play: it leverages fold equity against the SB’s wide calling range, denies free cards to overcards, and extracts thin value from worse hands (A‑high, Kx, Tx, some 8x). If Flash folds I take the pot; if called I have showdown value but no strong redraws so I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check/reevaluate or fold to significant aggression). I won’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so keep the sizing small to control risk and maximize immediate EV.

On this low, disconnected board of 6d-4h-2s, my Ace-high with a Seven kicker remains a relatively strong hand against a wide button opening range. Minni's bet of 150 into a 500 pot is a small continuation bet, likely intended to test my resolve. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 800) and the fact that I have two overcards that could improve my hand on the turn, folding is not an option. Raising is unnecessary and would bloat the pot while I am out of position. Calling is the most balanced play, allowing me to see a turn card and evaluate Minni's subsequent actions.

The turn Ten of Hearts is a relatively neutral card that doesn't significantly change the board's texture, although it does complete some unlikely gutshots. As the out-of-position defender with Ace-high, checking is the standard play to see how the pre-flop aggressor responds. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a river card for free if Minni checks back, or evaluate the strength of my hand against another bet.

I have 2♣8♠ and paired the deuce on the board (6♦ 4♥ 2♠ T♥) — a weak/bottom pair with no real redraws. I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop (was called), so opponent’s range is wide and includes lots of overcards, weak pairs and some small pockets. The turn Ten is a dangerous overcard that improves many hands in his calling range, and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Check for pot control and showdown value: if Flash bets small (reasonable bluff or a thin value bet) I’ll call and re-evaluate on the river; if he fires a large bet or raises I’ll fold my marginal pair. Checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position to make a disciplined decision on the river.

I have Ace-high (Ah7d) on a board of 6d-4h-2s-Th-9d. After calling a small flop bet and seeing the turn check through, the river 9d is relatively neutral. Ace-high has some showdown value against missed draws or lower non-paired hands in the button's wide range. There is no merit in betting here, as I likely won't get called by worse or fold out better. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown or evaluate based on the size of a potential bet from Minni.

Check. I have a weak bottom pair (2♣) with no redraws after the T♥ and 9♦. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop (was called), but the turn checked through and the opponent has been very passive since — a line consistent with missed overcards, weak pairs, or showdown control. A river bet will get value from very few worse hands and will often be called by hands that already beat me; it also risks folding out some marginal hands that would check‑back while opening me up to raises from better hands. Pot control and guaranteed showdown is the higher EV line here: check and see the showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, the button opening range is typically wide, but Ten-Seven offsuit is at the bottom of that range and often a fold against aggressive blinds. Both Elon and Minni have shown willingness to defend or three-bet. With a stack of 7,100 (71 BBs), I don't need to force a marginal play with a weak hand from the button. Folding is a safe and standard decision here to wait for a better opening opportunity.

T3o is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, mediocre high card with poor kicker and low playability post-flop. In SB vs BB with deep stacks (75+ BB effective), limping invites aggression from Minni who frequently raises/3-bets. Raising has poor equity realization. Pot odds to complete are decent (3:1), but hand too weak multiway or vs raise. From history, Minni defends/raises wide; better to fold and preserve stack for superior spots in tournament where survival and stack growth matter over 150 hands.

3-handed on the button facing a limp from SB (Minni) and check from BB (Flash), with QJo offsuit. QJo is a strong hand in this spot: decent equity multiway, blockers to strong hands, and excellent playability from position. Raising is standard to take initiative, deny equity to worse hands, and build pot when ahead. Minni limped, suggesting weakness; Flash checked BB. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is balanced size for tournament, considering stacks (Flash ~70BB, others deeper). Folding or limping exploitable.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I have 8♥4♦ — one of the worst offsuit combinations with almost no playability or equity, and I’ll be out of position postflop. Calling 250 to play a 3‑handed pot OOP is a clear -EV; a 3‑bet bluff is unnecessary here with the BB still to act and stacks deep but no fold equity worth risking. Preserve chips and wait for a much better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four suited. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni has folded. While J4s has some playability in a 3-handed game and I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against Elon, who has been relatively active. J4s is at the very bottom of a defending range and often struggles to realize its equity post-flop without a very specific board. Given my stack size and the current stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than bleed chips in a marginal out-of-position spot.

On the button with 5♦2♠ — essentially junk with almost no showdown equity. In 3-handed play my positional advantage is huge and opens are wide; stealing the blinds is high-EV. Pot is 150 and the to-call is 100, so a standard 3x BB open to 300 (total) applies pressure, preserves my image, and often wins the pot immediately. If I face a sizable 3-bet (e.g. ~900+ from Elon) I’ll fold — this hand is a clear fold versus strong aggression. If called, I’ll play very cautiously postflop, c-betting small on favorable texture or checking and folding to significant resistance on dry boards. Raise to 300 to attempt the steal while protecting my deep stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 4c. Minni has raised from the Button to 300. 8-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against an aggressive opponent with a large stack. The pot odds do not justify a call, and a reraise would be an unnecessary risk with this holding. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB) from Minni after SB fold. Pot 450, need to call 200 more (total 300 committed). Hand Js9h is a decent speculative hand with straight potential, broadway blocker, and plays reasonably postflop. Minni raises frequently from button as seen in history (e.g., Hands 1,5,9,11,18,20,21,27,30,33,42,44,48,51), suggesting a wide range. OOP but pot odds are good (~2.25:1 direct, plus implied), and equity vs. wide button open (~35-40%) justifies a call. Folding is too tight multiway potential gone but heads-up fine; 3bet not ideal as J9o is more of a call/call-merge hand. Plan: call, see flop OOP and c/r strong or check-fold weak/marginal. Deep stacks allow realization.

Flop of Ks Ts 6d hits my Js9h perfectly with an open-ended straight draw (8 or Q for the nuts straight 8-9-T-J-K or 9-T-J-Q-K), giving me 8 clean outs (~17% to river, ~32% by showdown) plus backdoor spade flush draw. No pair or made hand, pure semi-bluff draw. OOP heads-up vs Minni's wide button opening range (frequent button raises in history: hands 1,5,9,11,12,15,16,18,20,21,24,27,28,30,33,36,39,42,44,45,48,51,54, etc.), which includes many Ax suited (esp spades), Kx, Tx, suited connectors like J9s-QTs-76s-87s that hit this board or have draws. Board is draw-heavy (spade flush draw, OESDs like Q9,97,87), favoring her range somewhat but my blocker to Js and specific draw realization is strong. Pot 650 (~6.5BB), deep stacks (112BB eff). Preflop plan was check-raise strong flops, and this qualifies: check to induce c-bet from her high c-bet freq (e.g., hands 1,3,12,16,21,28,31,37,41,45,51,55), then check-raise to ~1/2-3/4 pot or overbet semi-bluff, building pot with ~35%+ equity vs her range (GTO sims suggest check-raise OESD profitable OOP). Donk betting thin OOP risks folding out worse; pure check-fold too weak. Folding absurd with odds/depth. Check sets up optimal line: deny equity, get value from worse draws/pairs if she calls, fold out pure air.

I opened wide preflop to steal; Elon called with a fairly wide range. The flop K♠ T♠ 6♦ misses my 5♦2♠ (only a lone spade/backdoor). However Elon’s calling range contains many unpaired broadways and small pairs that will fold to pressure — this is a good spot for a small continuation bet to seize the pot or deny equity to spade and straight draws. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (225) to represent K/T and apply fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a substantive raise (he will usually have a very strong hand if he raises), and if called proceed very cautiously on the turn (check or tempo back depending on the card).

Flop Ks Ts 6d is a perfect realization for Js9h: open-ended straight draw with 8 nut outs (32% equity by river), Js blocker to broadway, backdoor flush. Minni's button opening range is wide (frequent raises: hands 1,5,9,11,12,15+), hits this drawy board with Kx/Tx/pairs/draws like QJs/J9s/87s/Q9s. Her small 225 bet (~1/3 pot) into 650 smells like weak protection/value/thin air, high fold equity for semi-bluff raise. OOP check-raise per preflop plan: denies equity to her worse draws/spades/air, extracts value from pairs/weaker Tx/Kx if calls, maximizes pressure deepstacked (112BB eff). Pot 875, raise to 675 (~3x her bet, ~2/3 pot post-call) balances value/semi-bluff, good vs her calling range. Call too passive (realizes equity poorly OOP, lets her draw free); fold insane. History: she cbets light (hands 1,3,12,16+), folds to aggression (e.g., hand 34). +EV line to build stack long-term.

Elon’s raise to 675 over my 225 flop bet is a substantial, value-leaning response on K♠ T♠ 6♦. The pot is 1,550 and calling 450 would require ~22.5% equity to break even. I hold 5♦2♠ — no pair, only a backdoor spade — and have virtually no showdown equity against a raising range that likely contains Kx, Tx, sets or overpairs and only occasional semi-bluffs. A call is a losing long-term play and a bluff-raise here would be too spewy. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold to this substantive raise.

I am on the button with 4h 6c, which is a weak starting hand. The blinds are 50/100, and it costs me 100 to call from the button, but even in late position, this hand has very poor playability and realization of equity. Minni and Elon have both been active and have significantly larger stacks than I do. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

Preflop in the small blind with Kc8d after button folds. K8o is a playable hand with king-high equity and some straight potential. Facing only the big blind with deep stacks (mine 12k, Minni 20k+), completing the SB for 50 more into a 150 pot is standard and +EV. Folding is too tight from SB; raising OOP with offsuit marginal king risks playing a bloated pot out of position against Minni's likely wide defending range, based on history of frequent calls and aggression. Prefer seeing a cheap flop to realize equity postflop.

I have J♦2♠ in the big blind facing only a limp from the small blind. This is a very weak, offsuit hand and I’m out of position; with deep stacks I don’t want to inflate the pot or iso-raise light. Standard, low-variance play is to check and see a flop for free, then fold to significant aggression unless I pair or pick up a realistic draw. If Elon checks behind we’ll see a cheap flop; if he leads, I’ll usually fold to a sizeable c-bet and only continue with a clear improvement or a very cheap probe.

Flop of 7d Th 3s completely misses my Kc8d – no pair, no draws, just king-high with a mediocre kicker. Rainbow, uncoordinated board favors checking OOP to control pot size and realize any thin equity on later streets. Minni's preflop check indicates a wide range including many weak hands that I beat, but betting air here risks bloating the pot OOP against her potential calling range or check-raises. Preflop completion was for cheap multiway potential, but now heads-up with nothing, checking exploits her likely weakness while preserving stack. Will fold to reasonable bets, check back showdown if she checks.

Board is 7♦ T♥ 3♠ and I hold J♦2♠ — no pair, no real draw (only a backdoor diamond draw requiring both turn+river). Pot is tiny (200) and Elon checked to me after limping. With deep stacks and being out of position, a bluff c-bet here is high-variance and unlikely to fold out a lot of his calling range. Standard pot-control / low-variance line is to check back, see a free turn, and only continue if I improve or get a very cheap spot to bluff later. I will check and preserve my stack rather than turn this into a bloated pot with air.

On the turn with board 7d Th 3s Qd, my Kc8d remains pure air: no pair, no draws, just king-high. The board has two diamonds (flush draw completed), a gutshot for some (e.g., J9), but nothing for me. OOP vs Minni's wide checking range from BB (which includes many weak hands and missed draws), checking preserves pot control, realizes my thin king-high showdown equity cheaply against her likely floats or weak holdings, and avoids bloating the pot with a bluff when she frequently calls stations per history. Betting risks calling stations or her check-raises; fold equity is low OOP on turn with deep stacks (12k effective). Standard check to river.

I have J♦2♠ on a 7♦ T♥ 3♠ Q♦ board — no pair, no realistic draw (with only one card to come I cannot make a flush), and I’m out of position against a limp/caller who has checked the turn. Pot is tiny (200) and betting here with air is high-variance and unlikely to fold many better hands (pairs, Qx, Tx, any 7, or even float/call hands). Standard, low-variance play is to check back, control the pot, and see a cheap river — only bluff or invest if I pick up a clear improvement or get a very favorable river spot. I’ll check and preserve my stack.

River 6h on a 7d Th 3s Qd 6h board. My Kc8d is still pure king-high air with no improvement, no pairs, no draws. The board is somewhat drawy with diamond flush possible, gutshots (e.g., 45 for straight, J9 for gutshot), but the 6h mainly completes weak straight draws like 456, 45x. Minni's range from BB check-back preflop is wide and weak: low pairs, ace-highs, missed draws, junk. She's checked three streets, indicating no strong hands or missed value opportunities. OOP on river in a tiny 200 pot with 12k stacks, checking realizes my thin king-high showdown equity against her frequent weak holdings (per history of calling stations and check-backs). Bluffing risks her calling with worse (e.g., Qx, Tx, Ax, low pairs) or even better hands; fold equity low vs her passive lines. No value since few hands she calls without beating me. Pot control achieved all hand; check for potential win at showdown without risk.

I hold J♦2♠ on 7♦ T♥ 3♠ Q♦ 6♥ — no pair and no realistic draw. SB limp‑called preflop and has checked flop and turn, representing a very wide range that includes many hands that beat me (Qx, Tx, 7x, small pairs) or weak floats. The pot is tiny (~200) so betting as a bluff has poor fold equity and just introduces unnecessary variance. Standard, low‑variance play is to check back and preserve my stack; I will fold to a river bet unless it’s an extremely small probe I have a good read on.

Preflop on the button with T5s suited in an unopened pot (blinds posted, pot 150, to-call 100, likely SB folded or checked through). This is an excellent spot 3-handed. T5ss is a profitable open-raise from the button: wide range appropriate here (suited connectors play well multiway or heads-up in position, potential for straights/flushes). Raising achieves fold equity against blinds (esp. Flash shallower at ~69BB), builds pot with initiative, and allows control postflop. Limp/call is too passive; fold too tight. Size to 3x BB (300 total), standard for tournament preflop open, balances with value hands. Minni deep, Flash defendy from history but still EV+. Maximizes stack growth via steals and playable pots.

Fold. 4♠10♣ is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability out of position. Elon opened from the button in 3‑handed action; to call I must put in 250 to win a 450 pot (break‑even equity ≈ 35.7%). 10‑4o will frequently be dominated by the button’s opening range and has low realization OOP, and I can also face a squeeze from the big blind. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the higher EV play.

I am in the Big Blind holding A3o. Elon raised to 3x from the button, which is a standard opening range. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450), A3o is a mathematically sound defend against a button open. I have roughly 54% equity against a wide button opening range.
