I will call to see a flop and realize my equity without over-inflating the pot out of position.

On a paired board of 8s-6d-8h, my Ace-high holds significant showdown value. I defended my big blind with Ac3s against a button raise, and this texture generally misses a lot of the aggressor's range as well as mine. Leading out (donk betting) here serves little purpose as it would likely only be called by better hands or cause worse hands to fold their equity. Checking is the standard play here to see how Elon reacts. If he bets, I have a relatively easy call given the dry board and the possibility that he is continuation betting with air or overcards that I currently beat.

Heads-up on a paired 886 rainbow flop after Flash checks OOP. My button raise range is wide, including many overcards, sets, and two-pair combos that crush Flash's capping range. T5ss completely misses but has backdoor spade FD and two overcards, making it a fine bluff candidate with fold equity. Flash's history shows passive play post-flop (frequent check-calls, folds to aggression like in H16, H28), especially shallower at ~66BB. C-betting ~1/3 pot continues initiative, denies equity to his medium-strength hands (77-JJ, Ax, flush draws), and builds pot when called by worse. Checking risks giving free cards and missing steals in position. Polarized small bet maximizes EV long-term.

On a paired board of 8-6-8, my Ace-high has significant showdown value against a button opening range. Elon's continuation bet of 225 into 650 (about 1/3 pot) is a standard sizing often used with both value hands and air. I am getting excellent pot odds (225 to win 875) and only need about 20% equity to continue. Since Ace-high is ahead of many of his overcard bluffs and drawing hands, I will call to see a turn. Folding would be too exploitable on this dry board, and raising would turn a hand with showdown value into a bluff.

On the turn card 2s, the board remains very dry (8s-6d-8h-2s). My Ace-high (Ac3s) still holds reasonable showdown value against Elon's button opening range, which includes many overcard combinations like KQ, KJ, and JT. Since I called his flop continuation bet, my range is capped but contains several hands that can beat his bluffs. Betting here doesn't serve much of a purpose as I likely won't get called by worse hands and won't fold out better ones. Checking is the most appropriate action to maintain the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or induce further bluffs from Elon's air.

Heads-up vs Flash on turn board 8s6d8h2s (paired eights, now spade flush possible). Holding T5s spades gives me the nut flush draw (four spades: T-8-5-2), backdoor straight potential minimal, live T overcard. Preflop button raise wide, flop c-bet polarized bluff/value mix called OOP by passive Flash (history: frequent check-calls, folds to aggression e.g., H16,H28,H43). Pot 1100 (~11BB), Flash ~64BB effective, checked again. Board low/completed draws but paired limits scary rivers; my range perceived as strong (sets, two-pair, overpairs). Semi-bluff bet exploits: fold equity vs his medium pairs (77,99? crushed, A-high, missed draws), value from weaker draws/pairs. Position allows control if called (30% river equity to nuts). Check risks free river or check-raise; pot-sized bet too big, half-pot ~550 denies equity, builds for river shove if called. Bet 550 maximizes EV in 3-handed tournament spot.

Elon's 550 bet into a 1100 pot on the turn is a half-pot sizing, which can be polarizing on such a dry, paired board (8s-6d-8h-2s). My Ace-high (Ac3s) is still ahead of many of his semi-bluffs or 'air' continuation bets, such as overcards (KQ, KJ, JT) or missed straight draws (75s). Given the pot odds (calling 550 to win a total pot of 2200 requires 25% equity), and the fact that this board texture is difficult for him to have hit significantly outside of a few 8x combinations or pocket pairs, I have a profitable call to see the river. Folding here would be over-folding on a board that largely misses a button opening range, especially since Ace-high retains significant showdown value.

On the river Jack of Hearts, the board is 8-6-8-2-J. My Ace-high (Ac3s) remains a hand with decent showdown value against a button range that includes many missed overcard combinations like KQ, KT, or QT. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands and wouldn't reliably fold out pairs or a Jack. Therefore, checking is the most strategic move to reach a showdown or see if Elon continues his aggression. If he checks back, I often have the best hand. If he bets, I will have to decide if he's bluffing a third barrel.

River board 8862J with paired 8s, spade flush possible (missed my nut FD), Jack brings gutshots/pair Js. Holding T5s: complete air, T-high no pair/draw. OOP Flash check-called flop small c-bet (paired board), turn half-pot (spade draw hits, he calls again OOP showing strength/medium pair likely: 77-99, 66/86 two-pair, A-high SDV, unlikely sets given passivity). History: Flash defendy preflop BB but folds to postflop aggression (H16,H28,H37,H43), check-calls weak pots but vulnerable to river pressure. My button raise range polarized strong (sets,88,86,OTPs,Jx,Ax,flushes); line (cbet flop/turn) reps trips/flush/Jx heavily. Pot 2200, Flash eff ~59BB (5875), checked OOP again (weakness/cap). Pure bluff exploits: denies SDV, ~35-40% fold equity vs calling range (pairs <JJ crushed by my image), position lets fold to raise. Check risks chopping/losing SDV showdown (he likely ahead). Half-pot bet 1100 consistent sizing, offers poor odds (~3:1 pot odds on 3300 pot for 1100 call into 4400 center), max EV steal in 3-handed tourney spot w/ deep stacks ahead. Builds stack via initiative/range advantage vs passive opp.

Elon has fired three barrels on a very dry, paired board. While Ace-high (Ac3s) had reasonable showdown value on the turn, the river Jack of Hearts is a problematic card. It connects with many of the overcard combinations in a Button opening range that might have been bluffing the previous streets, such as AJ, KJ, QJ, and JT. By betting 1100 into 2200, Elon is putting significant pressure on my capped range. Given that the Jack improves a significant portion of his potential bluffing range to hands that now beat my Ace-high, and a triple barrel line generally represents a strong value range on this texture (trips or overpairs), calling with Ace-high is too risky. I will fold and preserve my remaining stack.

3♣2 off is a trash hand, but the price to see a flop is small (100 into a 150 pot) and I’m on the button — last to act post‑flop. I don’t want to bloat the pot with junk by raising; I’ll call cheaply and use position to fold to aggression on later streets unless I flop something or get a favorable board. If either blind re‑raises preflop I’ll fold to avoid playing a big pot out of my hand. Call to see the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with J7o. Minni has limped from the button for 100. This hand is marginally weak, but with only 50 chips more to call into a pot of 200, I am getting 4:1 odds to see a flop. Given the pot odds and the fact that Elon in the Big Blind might check through, calling is a standard defensive play to see if I can hit the board. Raising is unnecessary with such a mediocre hand against a button limper who likely has a wide range.

K8o is a strong enough hand in the BB facing two limpers in an unraised pot. Limpers from Minni (button) and Flash (SB) have wide ranges based on hand history, often calling or limping weak hands. Raising isolates, applies fold equity, and builds the pot with top pair/top kicker potential. K blocks KK, good postflop playability despite offsuit. Min raise to 200 is too small; pot-sized raise to ~400 risks too much OOP. Raising to 300 (3x BB, standard iso size) balances value vs worse (KQ, KJ, Axs) and bluffs. My deep stack (132 BB) vs Flash's 58 BB supports aggression. Checking multiway risks domination or getting outdrawn cheaply, but raise maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

Elon raised to 300 and I hold 3♥2♣ on the button — a very weak hand. The price to see a flop is tiny (I only need to add 200 more chips; total commitment 300) and I have position. With a deep stack (20,700) and only one active raiser, calling to realize equity and use position post‑flop is the best line. I won’t inflate the pot by raising with junk and I’ll fold to a 3‑bet to avoid playing a big pot out of hand. Postflop plan: proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression unless I pair a 2/3, pick up strong draws, or find a clear bluffing opportunity on a favorable board. Call to see the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Elon (Big Blind) has raised to 300 after Minni and I limped. Minni has already called the raise. While the pot odds are roughly 3.5:1 to call the additional 200, J7o is a very weak hand to play out of position against two opponents, one of whom has shown strength by raising into multiple limpers. My stack is the shortest at the table, and preserving chips for better spots is more important than calling with marginal holdings in a multi-way pot. I will fold.

Preflop raiser OOP vs Minni's button limp-call, which is a wide range including suited broadways, Ax suited, pocket pairs, and speculative hands. Flop 4d Js 2s is dryish with two spades, hitting my range decently (KJ, AJ, JTs, sets) while my Ks blocks some Ax spades and KJs. K8o is pure air but has backdoor spade draw. C-bet sizing ~1/3 pot to deny equity to her floats/draws (spade draws, gutshots like 53), apply fold equity, and maintain range balance. Her history shows calling stations tendencies but folds to aggression postflop often (e.g., Hand 1, 3). Large pot built pre (700), deep stacks allow bluffing. Check risks giving free card to OESD/spade draws or check-raise. Bet denies, reps strength.

I have bottom pair (2s) with a very weak kicker (3). Elon opened preflop and is making a small flop bet (250 into a 950 pot) — a size consistent with a wide continuation bet heads‑up. The price to see the turn is cheap (call 250, breakeven ≈21%), I have position, and my hand has some showdown value plus two outs to trips. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and folding is too tight given the pot odds, so I’ll call to realize equity and use position to fold to heavy turn aggression or to extract value if I improve.

On the turn 9c, the board is 4d Js 2s 9c. My K8o missed completely: no pair, no draws (Ks blocks some flush draws but I have no spade equity left; 8d gives negligible backdoor diamond). Pot is 1200, heads-up OOP vs Minni's button limp-call range, which includes Jx, suited broadways, Ax spades, pairs, suited connectors that hit (e.g., JTs, 53s for str8 draw, sets). She called my flop c-bet 250 into 700, indicating strength: likely Jx, two-pair, sets, or strong draws (spade flush draws now have 9 outs). As preflop raiser and flop bettor, my range is polarized (value like AJ/KJ/sets or bluffs), but betting turn into her capping range risks getting called by better or raised off air. Checking realizes equity (if she has draw, gives free card but OOP we control pot), induces bluffs/checks from weak Jx, and allows cheap river showdown or fold to bet. Deep stacks (100+ BB), but no fold equity here as she floated flop. History shows Minni calls stations but folds postflop aggression sometimes; check preserves stack, avoids bloating pot with nothing. If she bets, easy fold as range too strong.

I have bottom pair (2s) with a weak 3 kicker on 4d Js 2s 9c, pot is 1,200 and Elon (the preflop raiser / flop bettor) just checked the turn. In position I want to extract value from missed overcards and deny a free river card to hands that c‑bet the flop and then gave up on the turn. A small protection/value stab is the highest EV line here — it folds out a lot of air and overcards while keeping the pot manageable if called. Bet 400 (~1/3 pot). This sizing risks 400 to win 1,200 (break‑even fold frequency ≈25%), which is easily met against a wide flop‑bet/turn‑check range. Plan: if Elon folds I take the pot; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the river for thin value or folding to significant aggression; if he check‑raises the turn I’ll fold (I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal pair).

Facing a 400 bet into 1600 on the turn with K8o, complete air (king-high, no pair, no draws after turn 9c bricks everything). Board 4d Js 2s 9c has possible straights (TQT, but unlikely), Jx, sets, two-pair from her wide limp-call range (Jx, suited connectors like JTs, 89s for str8 draw now, pairs that hit). She called flop c-bet (repping strength) and now bets after my check, polarized to value (Jx+, sets) or bluffs (missed spades, pure air less likely from her). Pot odds 400 to win 2000 (~3:1) require ~17% equity, but K8 has <10% vs her calling range (dominated by any J/A/K, fold equity realization poor OOP). Calling bloats pot OOP with nothing, risks larger river bet/call-down OOP. Folding preserves deep stack (127BB), avoids spew, consistent with turn check to pot control/realize equity (now zero). Tournament phase favors discipline over heroics; exploit her later with better spots. History shows she values bets post-flop when strong.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, which is a premium starting hand. I have a stack of approximately 58 big blinds, making me the short stack, but still with plenty of room to play. Standard strategy here is to open for a raise to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately. I will raise to 300, which has been a consistent opening size in this game.

Preflop from SB OOP vs button raise to 300 (facing 250 to call into 450 pot). A7o is a marginal ace offsuit with poor postflop playability out of position. Button ranges are wide but include many Ax, KQ, suited connectors that dominate A7o. Calling risks difficult spots postflop; 3-betting as a bluff is suboptimal without better blockers or fold equity vs a potentially sticky opponent. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a multi-hand tournament, especially with deep effective stacks.

I’m in the big blind with J♣6♠ facing a Button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I must put in 200 more (total 300) — calling gives me ~30.8% break-even equity (200 to win ~650). J6o is a weak, oft-dominated hand and a poor 3-bet candidate, especially out of position postflop, so I don’t want to build a big pot with it. Against a reasonably wide button open my hand has marginal equity and some showdown/oncidental improvement potential, so I’ll take a flop cheaply and re-evaluate. Plan: call to see flop, exercise pot control OOP, fold to significant turn/river aggression if I miss, and continue only with top pair, good draws, or favorable runouts.

I have Jc6s on Jd-5c-Qh — top pair but a very weak kicker. BTN's open is wide and contains lots of Qx/KQ/AQ and better Jx combinations that beat me, so I'm often second-best. I'm out of position and the pot is already meaningful (650) relative to the stacks; I want to keep the pot under control rather than bloating it with a marginal hand. Checking preserves my ability to call reasonable bets and fold to heavy aggression, lets Button bluff or value-bet worse, and gives me a free turn to improve. Plan: check now; call small-to-medium c-bets (roughly up to ~1/3–1/2 pot) but fold to large pressure on turn/river unless I improve to two pair/trips or get a clear read to continue.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker on a Queen-high board. In a three-handed game, this is a relatively strong hand. Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise, which is standard. I will place a continuation bet to get value from weaker Jacks, pocket pairs, or draws (like K-10 or 10-9), and to protect my hand against overcards like a King. A bet of 325 (half pot) is a standard sizing to maintain initiative.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (J♣6♠ on J♦5♣Q♥). Button (Flash) opened wide pre and now bets 325 into ~650 (pot now 975) — a standard ~1/2-pot c-bet. His range contains hands that beat me (Qx, KQ, AQ, better Jx/overpairs) but also worse Jx and bluffs. Calling 325 to win ~1300 gives me ~25% immediate pot odds and preserves pot control OOP. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal kicker; folding surrenders too much value. I’ll call 325 to see the turn and re-evaluate: continue only with two pair/trips, a clear improvement, or a strong read; fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (Jc6s) on Jd-5c-Qh-2h, out of position to a wide-button opener who c-bet the flop and I called. The turn 2h doesn't change much — villain's range still includes lots of Qx, KQ/AQ, better Jx and some bluffs, so I'm often second-best. Betting OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and make it easy to get raised/fold; checking keeps the pot under control, lets villain bluff worse hands, and allows me to fold to heavy aggression if I don't improve. Plan: check and see the river — call small/medium river bets for showdown value or fold to large pressure; only start building the pot if I pair up or get a very clear read.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a Queen-high board. After betting the flop and being called, checking the turn is a standard pot-control move. It allows me to see a river for free, protects me from a potential check-raise by a hand that beats me (like a Queen or a set), and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal made hand. I can then evaluate the river based on Minni's next action.

Check. I have J♣6♠ on J♦-5♣-Q♥-2♥-Q♦: two pair (Qs & Js) but it’s a quite vulnerable two‑pair out of position — many hands in Button’s line (preflop open, flop c‑bet, then checking the turn) are Qx (now trips/full houses), better Jx (KJ/AJ) or pocket pairs that crush me. Betting out of position bloats the pot and invites value‑raises from the exact hands that dominate me; it also prices in calls from hands I can’t beat. Checking keeps the pot under control and lets the button bluff missed draws or thin‑value bet worse Jx/overcards that I beat. Plan after a check: call small‑to‑medium river bets for showdown, fold to large raises. Given stack sizes and table dynamics, avoid building a pot OOP with a second‑best, kicker‑weak two pair — check 0.

On the river, the board has paired the Queen, giving me two pair (Jacks and Queens) with an Ace kicker. I have significant showdown value. Minni checked the flop, turn, and now the river. While it's possible she is trapping with a Queen, it is more likely she has a weaker Jack or a missed draw. However, if I bet, it's unlikely she calls with a hand I beat (like a small pocket pair or a missed draw), and she will only call or raise with a Queen or a better hand. Since the pot is already decent and I am guaranteed to see the showdown by checking, I will check back to realize my equity and avoid a potentially difficult decision if she were to check-raise.

Qh4d is a marginal offsuit hand preflop, with queen-high and poor connectivity or suitedness. In 3-handed play on the button, the position is advantageous for wide ranges, but facing a 100-to-call into a 150 pot gives pot odds of about 2.5:1 (requiring ~28% equity, wait no: 150 pot +100 call =250 final pot, risk 100/250=40% breakeven). Vs a typical blind minraise range (loose 3-handed, ~40-50% hands), Q4o has roughly 38-42% equity, borderline. However, postflop playability is poor: many dominated by better Qx, awkward floats, reverse implied odds. Opponents like Minni are aggressive (frequent preflop raises from history), so better to fold trash and preserve stack for premium spots or steals when first in. Raising over risks multiway or vs strong range, calling leads to difficult spots. Optimal: fold to wait for better dynamics.

We're heads-up after the button folded, so this is effectively a SB vs BB spot and I'll have position postflop. T♥9♦ is a playable hand in position with good playability and equity. I want to seize initiative, apply pressure and isolate the big blind rather than limp; a standard 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) gives fold equity and lets me play postflop with position. If called I'll c-bet on favorable flops and control the pot when I miss; if Flash 3-bets large I'll frequently fold T9o given it's often dominated.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The Small Blind, Minni, has made a small raise to 200. Given the excellent pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 350) and the fact that I will have position throughout the rest of the hand, this is a clear call. A8o is well ahead of a typical wide opening range from the Small Blind in a short-handed game. I prefer calling over raising here to keep the pot size manageable and to avoid being forced to fold a strong hand to a potential 4-bet from a big stack.

I opened preflop and have position; the flop (Jh 6c 2s) is pretty dry with a single overcard (J). T9o has no pair and only backdoor outs, so this is a standard spot to c-bet as the preflop aggressor to fold out missed hands and gain information. I’ll choose a half-pot c-bet (200 into 400) — large enough to pick up the pot often, small enough to keep the pot controllable if called. Plan: if Flash raises big I will usually fold this marginal hand; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue with a meaningful improvement (Q/8 or strong equity).

I have Ace-high (Ad 8c) on a dry flop of 6c-2s-Jh. Minni (Small Blind) has made a standard continuation bet of 200 into the 400-chip pot. While Ace-high may occasionally be the best hand against a wide Small Blind range, I have no equity to improve aside from hitting an Ace or an Eight, and I have no straight or flush draws. Continuing here would essentially be a 'float' with the intention of taking the pot away on a later street, but against a large stack like Minni, this is high-risk. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities where I have more equity or a stronger made hand.

Button vs two blinds, holding J6 offsuit: marginal but absolutely open in position heads-up at this table. Pot is 150, to-call 100; with deep stacks (I ~210bb, SB ~62bb, BB ~126bb) I want to take the initiative and leverage position and fold equity. I’ll open-raise to 300 (3x the BB / a standard BTN sizing here) — big enough to generate folds and respect my table image but small enough to keep maneuverability post-flop. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (Elon has shown frequent aggression), continue with a c-bet on favourable flops if called, and play straightforwardly post-flop (pot control or folding marginal turns) — calling 100 is too passive and folding is too tight for button opening range.

I am holding 7s 2s in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, and I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand if I called. Given the low strength of my hand and my position, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Facing a button raise to 300 (3BB) from Minni, who is aggressive from late position based on hand history. My hand is T3o, offsuit, disconnected, and weak with poor playability out of position in the BB. Pot odds are 450:200 (~2.25:1, ~31% equity needed), but against a wide button range, while I might have ~35-40% raw equity, OOP post-flop dynamics with this hand are poor—hard to realize equity, vulnerable to continuation bets. No strong reason to call or raise (no fold equity with this holding). Stacks are deep (126BB), early in tournament (hand 66/150), better to fold trash and wait for premium spots to build stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. With 62 BBs, I have a healthy stack but am the shortest at the table. I should be playing aggressively to build my stack. Since it's folded to me on the button, I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and build a pot with my strong hand.

Preflop from SB with T3o, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability. Facing a button raise from Flash, who may have a wide range, but T3o is easily dominated by better tens or any pair, and has minimal equity multiway if Minni calls from BB. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (pot 450 to call 250), but implied odds are poor due to hand strength. Standard fold to preserve stack in a tournament setting with 150 hands goal, avoiding speculative calls that bleed chips.

I’m on the big blind with 9♥2♥—a very weak, easily‑dominated hand and out of position. Flash opened to 300 from the button (pot 450); calling 200 would make the pot 650 so I’d need roughly ~23–24% equity to break even. 9‑2 suited doesn’t have that equity versus a standard button raising range, and postflop play OOP will be difficult and costly. A 3‑bet to 500 is just spewing chips with a marginal hand. With a healthy 210bb stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots and fold rather than invest another 200+ out of position.

Preflop on the button with 6d3s, a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low cards, and minimal playability postflop. The pot is 150 with a 100 to call, implying a raise from one of the blinds (likely a small raise given the min raise to 200). Pot odds are 150:100 or 1.5:1, requiring about 40% equity, but 63o has poor equity against typical blind raising ranges (often 20-30% at best). No position advantage compensates for the hand strength, and as a deep-stacked player (126BB), preserving chips for better spots is optimal in a tournament with 82 hands remaining. Opponent stacks: Minni very deep (210BB), Flash medium (62BB). Folding avoids spewing chips with trash.

Open-raise to seize the initiative. Heads-up from the small blind I should be opening a wide range; 6-7 offsuit is marginal but has straight potential and is perfectly playable heads-up. I’ll raise to 300 (3BB) — a standard size that gives fold equity and lets me play as the aggressor rather than limp out of position. If Flash insta‑3bets big I’ll fold this hand (not worth fighting for a big pot out of position), but if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dangerous boards. Overall this maximizes my EV by stealing the BB often and avoiding marginal, costly post‑flop spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 5d. Minni, who is the chip leader and has been playing very aggressively, raised to 3x from the Small Blind. While I will have position post-flop, 85o is a very weak hand that does not play well in a raised pot. Even getting 2:1 odds on a call, the risk of being dominated or forced to fold on most flops makes this a clear fold. I prefer to wait for stronger hands to challenge Minni's wide opening range.

3-handed, button — open a very wide range. 7♦6♣ is a playable, connected hand with good post‑flop playability and lots of implied odds in position. I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and deny free cards, so I’ll open‑raise rather than limp. Standard sizing in these logs is ~3x the BB; raising to 300 gives good fold equity against the small blind and keeps the pot manageable if the big blind calls. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet, call smaller 3‑bets rarely depending on sizing/reads, and play aggressively in position on favorable flops (c‑bet on wet boards when it folds to me, control size on dry boards and pursue straights/2‑pair/sets).

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 5s. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. 9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. Calling would put me out of position against the chip leader (Minni) with a subpar hand, and I would still have the Big Blind (Elon) left to act behind me. Given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot). Held 9s5d, offsuit gappers with poor playability OOP. While 3-handed button opens wide (~50-60% range) and pot odds are ~3:1 (needing ~23% equity), this hand has low equity (~25% vs wide range) but terrible postflop prospects: few strong draws, dominated often, hard to realize equity OOP vs aggressive Minni who cbets frequently (e.g., Hands 1,3,12,16,21). Preserve stack for better spots; standard fold at deep stacks.

I am on the button with King-Six suited (Kh 6h) in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to play from the button to put pressure on the blinds. A 3x raise is a standard opening size to attempt to take the pot pre-flop or to play in position post-flop with a hand that has decent equity and flush potential.

J5o is a trash hand offsuit with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Facing a button raise from Flash to 300 (standard open), calling 250 OOP from SB offers poor pot odds (pot will be 700, ~2.8:1 but multiway possible with BB behind) and horrible postflop playability. Dominated by any Jx, Tx, etc. No reason to defend this wide against button range. Raising has no fold equity with this hand and commits too much stack (12k deep). Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 80 hands left.

I’m in the big blind with A♠ Q♥ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must add 200 to call). AQs is a strong hand and a standard 3-bet candidate even OOP — I want to isolate the opener, take the initiative, and fold out weaker aces/Kx hands. Flash has ~59bb, I’m deep, so I’ll make a constructive 3-bet to ~3x the open. Raising to 900 gives good fold equity and keeps post‑flop play with a high-equity hand manageable. Plan: if he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll play the flop OOP for value and protection; if he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to a polar shove, calling smaller, depending on sizing).

I am on the button with Kh 6h and faced with a 3-bet to 900 from Minni in the Big Blind after my initial open to 300. While K6s has some playability in position, Minni is the table chip leader and has demonstrated a high level of aggression throughout the tournament, frequently using her stack to pressure opponents. Calling here would commit 15% of my remaining stack to a pot where I am likely behind or dominated, and I would be vulnerable to a continuation bet on most flops. Given that I am the shortest stack at the table with 59 big blinds, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable spot rather than getting involved in a bloated pot with a marginal hand against an aggressive leader.

3-handed on the button (acting first pre-flop). QTs offsuit is a strong enough hand to open raise in this spot with wide UTG ranges. Pot has 150 dead money from blinds. Raising to 3x BB (300) builds the pot, takes initiative, and leverages position post-flop. Minni and Flash have shown aggression but also folding tendencies to raises. My deep stack allows flexibility, Flash shorter but still can pressure.

Three-handed, facing a BTN open to 300 with K♠Q♣ in the small blind. KQo is too strong to just limp/call here — calling keeps me out of position and invites the big blind to come along. Elon opens wide from the button, so I want to 3-bet for value and fold equity to isolate him. Stack depths (me 21.6k, Elon 12.1k, Flash 5.8k) comfortably support a standard 3-bet; I’ll size to ~3× the open to apply pressure without overcommitting. Raise to 900 total gives good fold equity vs weaker opens and extracts value from worse Kx/Qx and broadway combos. Plan: if I face a polarized 4‑bet shove I’ll fold KQo; call smaller jams or flat 4‑bets only with a concrete read. If called heads‑up I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and control the pot OOP.

I am in the Big Blind holding 2h 5h. Elon raised from the button to 300, and Minni 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind. Although 2h 5h is suited, it is a very weak hand to play in a 3-bet pot, especially when I would be out of position against two active players for the rest of the hand. Committing 900 chips (roughly 15% of my stack) with such a marginal hand is not a winning strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni in the SB after my button open with QTo offsuit. Excellent position post-flop as button. Pot odds are strong: 600 to call into a pot of 1300 (effective ~24% equity required). QTo has good playability with broadway cards blocking QQ/TT/JJ, straight potential, and two overcards likely. Minni's history shows aggression including light 3-bets (e.g., Hand 35), but she folds to further pressure sometimes. Deep stacks (mine 12k, hers 20k) allow post-flop flexibility without committing too much. Folding is too nitty in position vs SB range which includes bluffs and suited broadways; 4-betting offsuit QTo is too thin without nut blockers. Call to realize equity and control pot size in position.

I 3‑bet preflop to 900 and got called, so on the flop (Jh 4c Td) I'm the preflop aggressor OOP. I hold K♠ Q♣ — two overcards and a backdoor Broadway (need an A), but no realistic flush; this hand has decent equity vs a wide calling range and works well as a continuation‑bet bluff. I want fold equity while keeping the pot controlled OOP, so I’ll make a relatively small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) to fold out weaker A‑highs and small pairs and to get value from worse broadways. Plan: if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check most blanks, barrel only when I improve or see clear weakness); fold to large raises since KQo is not a strong made hand here.

On the flop of J♥4♣T♦, I have middle pair (Tens) with a Queen overcard and backdoor heart draw holding Q♥T♠. This is a strong enough hand in position against Minni's SB 3-bet range, which includes overpairs, sets, two-pair combos, Jx, Tx, Ax, flush draws, straight draws, and bluffs. Her 650 bet (~1/3 pot) into a 1900 pot pre-bet is standard continuation sizing. Pot odds are excellent: calling 650 into 2550 (effective pot ~3200 post-call) requires only ~20% equity, and my hand has solid showdown value, blocks QT/JT straight draws, and can improve to top pair or Broadway. Raising is too thin as it risks folding out her worse hands (Ax, bluffs, weaker Jx/Tx) while getting called by better; folding is far too tight in position with deep stacks (my 11.5k effective). Calling realizes equity, keeps pot controlled, and leverages position to extract value or bluff-catch on later streets. Minni's history shows aggression but folding to post-flop pressure at times (e.g., Hand 1, 3), but here we float profitably.

Turn is a blank for K♠Q♣ on Jh 4c Td 8s. I was the preflop 3-bettor and c‑bet the flop; Elon just called. I have two overcards and good equity to improve (K/Q pairs, plus A or 9 give straights) but no made hand and I'm out of position. Barrel OOP again with just overcards is unnecessary and risks bloating the pot when I'm behind. I want pot control and to realize my equity, so I will check the turn. If Elon checks back we see a cheap river; if he bets small (~≤1/3 pot, ~≤1k) I’ll call for my ~30%+ chance to improve and to keep weaker hands honest. If he fires a larger sizing (>~1/2 pot, ~≈1.6k+) I’ll fold most of the time — he’s representing a pair or better and I don’t want to commit OOP with KQo. Default line: check, call small bets, fold to large pressure, re-evaluate on the river if I pair or complete a straight.

On the turn 8♠ board (J♥4♣T♦8♠), holding Q♥T♠ for middle pair (Tens) with Queen kicker and backdoor heart draw. Pot is 3200, Minni checked after her flop c-bet which I called in position. Her SB 3-bet preflop range is strong but polarized: overpairs (QQ+/AA), sets (JJ/TT/44), strong Jx/Tx (AJs/KJs/ATo), combo draws, and bluffs (suited Ax, broadway suited connectors). The 8♠ is a blankish card for most of her value range but completes some straight draws like 97 or Q9 (though Q9 would have flatted or 4-bet pre less likely). She checked, which could represent weakness (missed Ax/draws/bluffs that checked back), trapping with monsters, or medium strength planning to call/raise. In position with deep effective stacks (~10k for me, 20k her), my hand has strong showdown value, blocks QT/JT/Tx straights, and beats her worse pairs (weaker Jx/Tx/8x/4x), floats, and Ax high. Pot odds favor thin value: betting 50% pot (1600) gets called by worse often enough while folding out pure air and protecting against river draws (e.g., 9♠ for straight/flush scare). Checking back realizes equity safely but misses value from her ~25-35% calling range (Ax, weak pairs, stubborn draws). Her history shows aggression pre/flop but checking weakness post-flop (e.g., Hands 7,14,22); she folds to pressure sometimes but calls stations on others. Betting exploits her checking range, builds pot for river value if ahead, and denies equity to draws. No raise needed as no bet to raise. Sizing 1600 (1/2 pot) balances value/protection without overbetting into monsters.

I checked the turn to control the pot and realize equity. Elon bets 1,600 into a 4,800 pot (~1/3). I hold K♠Q♣ — two overcards and a Q that pairs or combines with a 9 to make a straight. Clean outs are roughly 3 Ks + 3 Qs + 4 nines = ~10 outs (~21–22% to improve by the river). Calling 1,600 to win 6,400 (~25% required) is borderline by pure math but justified because Elon’s line and sizing include many hands I can still beat when I hit (weaker Jx/Tx, floats, worse broadways). Raising as a bluff OOP is too risky with no pair; folding is too tight versus this small sizing and my decent equity. I’ll call, see the river, and fold to large river aggression if I miss; if I pair or complete the straight I’ll look to extract value.

River pairs my queen: I hold K♠Q♣ on J♥ 4♣ T♦ 8♠ Q♦ — top pair with a very strong kicker. Elon’s line (call 3‑bet pre, call my flop c‑bet, small bet on the turn, then check the river) looks like a medium/weak calling range (Jx, Tx, some pocket pairs, floated broadways, missed draws). Very few hands beat me here (two‑pair combos like QJ/QT or a 9 for the 8‑9 straight), and if he had those he often would have sized differently or shown more aggression. With the pot at 6,400 I want a small value bet to extract from worse J/T pairs and floaters while charging any marginal draws. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot to keep worse hands in and avoid bloating the pot out of position. If he raises big I’ll fold (I’m reluctant to call a large raise with a single pair); if he calls I expect to be ahead and win a decent pot. Betting 2,200 balances extraction and risk.

River Q♦ pairs my Q♥ and pairs the board's T♦ with my T♠, giving me top two pair (Queens and Tens), a very strong hand on J♥4♣T♦8♠Q♦. This beats most of Minni's SB 3-bet range including weaker Qx (like QJ for Queens+Jacks? Wait QJ beats me slightly? No, QJ is Q+J pair Queens+Jacks > QT. But overall strong: beats AJ (Jacks), AT (Tens), Ax high, 9x draws that missed, many Tx/Jx. Worse: QJ, Q8, but also blocks QT. Beats 89 straight? 89 is straight which beats two pair. Straights: 89 for 8-9-T-J-Q. Possible bluffs or thin value. Her line: 3-bet pre, c-bet flop, check-call turn bet, now OOP river lead 2200 (~1/3 pot into 6400). Checking turn suggests not super strong (monsters might raise), calling my value bet, river lead polarized: value like AQ (A♠Q♠? Aces+Queens), KQ (Kings+Queens), QJ (Queens+Jacks), trips (unlikely QQ/TT/JJ less likely check turn), straights 89s, or bluffs (missed draws like 95,75, A9, floats). Pot odds for call: 2200 into 10800 (~17% equity) very favorable even if behind 30% time. But in position, deep stacks (my 9k, her 16k), raising extracts max value from her worse pairs (Qx weaker, Tx, Jx), Ax, stubborn draws that she turns into bluffs or thin calls OOP. Her history: aggressive pre/flop but check-calls and folds to pressure sometimes (Hands 1,3,21,55), stations others (14,22). Raising ~2.5x her bet to 5500 (total pot commitment) sizes well: gets called by top of her range (AQ/KQ/QJ/89) enough vs folds bluffs, builds pot ~17k if called, leaving room for shove if re-raised unlikely. Calling realizes equity but caps value; folding insane. Raise balances value/protection.

Board: J♥ 4♣ T♦ 8♠ Q♦ — I hold K♠ Q♣ (top pair, good kicker). I bet a small value on the river (2,200) to get called by worse J/T pairs and floats, but Elon’s quick raise to 5,500 is large and polarizing. Pot is 14,100 and I must call 3,300 to win ~17,400, so I need ≈19% equity. Given his line (call 3‑bet pre, call flop, bet turn, then big raise over my river small bet) he is more often showing value: QJ/QT (two pair), QQ (trips), TT (set) and 9x straights all beat me. He may have a few river bluffs, but with his short stack and the sizing the raise is heavily value‑weighted. With a single pair OOP and my earlier plan to fold to a large raise, calling here would be spewy — raising is also too thin. I preserve chips and fold.

Button vs just the blinds with T6s — a suited, playable hand with position. In 3-handed play I should be opening a wide range to take the initiative and collect dead money; calling 100 is too passive. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300) to generate fold equity, isolate the SB/BB, and give myself post‑flop advantage. Both opponents are deep enough to play post‑flop; plan is to c‑bet favorable flops, fold to large, committed 3‑bets, and realize equity if checked to. This sizing is big enough to discourage too many multiway calls but small enough to keep maneuverability with these stacks.

I have pocket fives in the small blind. In a three-handed game, pocket pairs are significantly more valuable than in a full-ring game. Minni has been opening very frequently from the button, suggesting a wide range. While I could call to set-mine, being out of position for the rest of the hand is disadvantageous. I previously successfully 3-bet Minni in Hand #36 and took the pot down. By raising to 1000, I can take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or define the range of my opponents more clearly. If Minni calls, I still have a decent hand that can navigate a flop or hit a set.

82o is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Facing a button min-raise followed by a 3-bet from the small blind (shorter stack but still aggressive), the range is very strong. Pot odds are poor (900 to call into 1400, ~39% equity needed), and this hand has negligible equity against their ranges. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities in 3-handed play.

I opened to 300 with T6s and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000. With position and a suited, playable hand I want to keep the pot manageable and realize equity post‑flop rather than folding a decent hand or over‑committing with a marginal 4‑bet. Calling 700 into a 1400 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity) — T6s has workable equity vs a mixed SB 3‑bet range and plays well in position (can barrel favorable flops, pot‑control on bad boards, fold to heavy action). A minimum 4‑bet to 1700 is too small to generate reliable folds and a big shove is over‑committing against his value-heavy callers. I’ll call to 1000 total, see the flop, and re‑evaluate with position and the board texture.

I am first to act after 3-betting pre-flop. The flop comes Ten-high (7h-2s-Th) with a heart draw. My pocket fives are now an underpair to the Ten and the Seven. Minni's calling range for a 3-bet includes many hands that hit this board (like ATs, KTs, QTs, JTs, T9s) or medium pairs (88, 99, JJ) that have me beat. With an SPR of roughly 2.3, I want to be cautious. Leading out here often results in getting called by any piece of the board or better pairs. By checking, I can control the size of the pot and see how Minni responds. If she checks behind, I get a free turn card. If she bets, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but I'm likely not looking to commit my whole stack with just an underpair.

I have T♠6♠ — top pair with a weak kicker, in position versus the SB who 3‑bet preflop and now checked the flop. The board (7♥ 2♠ T♥) is relatively dry and my hand wants protection from overcards and to extract value from worse pairs / weaker tens and medium pockets. Flash's check can be both denial or an attempt to pot‑control; betting while in position lets me charge floats and realize equity. I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal kicker, so I’ll make a standard value/protection bet of about half the pot to get called by worse and keep the pot manageable if he check‑raises. Bet ~1/2 pot (≈1050). If he flats I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check‑raises big it will usually be a strong range and I’ll re‑evaluate (often folding to very large aggression).

Pre-flop, I 3-bet with pocket fives to take initiative against Minni's frequent button opens. Minni called, and the flop came 7h-2s-Th. I checked to control the pot, as this board contains many cards that connect with a 3-bet calling range (like T-x or mid-pairs). Minni has now bet 1050 into a 2100 pot. With only 3800 chips remaining, calling this bet would commit nearly half my starting stack to a pot where I only have an underpair and no real draw. Minni is an active player, but her range here either has me crushed (TT, 77, AT, KT, QT, JJ) or has significant equity against me (heart draws, overcards). Given the low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio), calling essentially commits me to the hand. I prefer to fold here and preserve my 38 big blinds rather than risk a large portion of my stack on a marginal underpair in a bloated pot.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit (K9o) in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand. Given the current blinds of 50/100 and my position, I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and aim to take control of the pot. Although I am the short stack with 4,800 chips, I still have 48 big blinds, which allows for normal play without going all-in yet.

Jd4h is a complete trash hand offsuit with no high card value, poor playability, and facing a button raise from Flash who has been active in late position historically. As SB, I'm out of position post-flop against potentially two opponents (Minni yet to act). Pot odds are about 1.8:1 but the hand has no equity realization OOP. Clear fold to preserve deep stack (177BB) for premium spots in tournament with 77 hands left.

I’m in the big blind with A♠ K♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 200 to call). This is a premium hand — too strong to just fold or routinely flat-call out of position. Flash’s button open is likely a fairly wide range, so I want to isolate, seize the initiative, and get value from worse broadways and medium pocket pairs. A 3-bet generates fold equity and keeps control of the hand while leaving comfortable post‑flop play if called. I’ll raise to 1,000 (a ~3.3x  open): large enough to apply pressure and discourage marginal calls, but not so large that I overcommit vs a potential 4-bet. If Flash shoves 4-bet I’ll seriously consider/call depending on sizing — AK has good equity vs a typical 4-bet shove range. Overall, 3-betting maximizes EV here.

I am on the button with K9o and opened to 300. Minni, who has been playing very aggressively in recent hands, 3-bet to 1,000 from the big blind. While the game is three-handed and ranges are wider, K9o is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, even with position. Calling 700 more would leave me with a 1.7 SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) on the flop, making it very difficult to play post-flop without committing my entire stack on marginal holdings. Given that Minni has shown a willingness to play large pots and apply pressure, I will fold and preserve my 45 big blind stack for a more favorable situation.

Preflop on the button with 5s 3h offsuit, one of the worst possible hands. Poor connectivity, offsuit, low cards with minimal potential. Even in position facing what appears to be an unraised or minimally raised pot (to call 100 into 150 pot), this hand has terrible equity against calling ranges and is unplayable postflop. No steal worth attempting here given the hand's lack of playability, reverse implied odds if it hits, and opponents' stacks (Flash shorter at 44BB may defend wide, Minni deep and aggressive from history). Folding preserves stack for better spots in this long tournament.

3♣7♥ is one of the worst starting hands — very poor playability and frequently dominated. I’m out of position to the big blind and only risking 50 to see a flop, but the postflop situation will be difficult and low EV against a BB who defends reasonably often. Raising to try to steal is spewy with this holding (and risks a shove or 3-bet), while completing to see a flop still leaves me OOP with garbage. With a deep stack and many hands left, preserving chips and folding is the best long-term play.

3-handed on the button with A♦9♦ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand (suited ace, decent kicker). Pot is 150 and 100 to call; I want the initiative and to isolate the blinds, so I’ll open-raise. A standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) is appropriate: it denies good odds to the small blind, is in line with table dynamics, and keeps postflop play manageable with deep stacks. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable textures (A, diamonds, dry high cards), use position to realize equity, and respect large 3-bets (fold or re-evaluate vs very large 3-bets; call smaller 3-bets to play in position). Raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with T6o. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. With a stack of 4500 chips, I am the short stack at the table. T6o is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially against a Button raise when the Big Blind (Elon) is also a deep stack and still to act. There is no reason to risk chips with such a marginal hand. Folding is the correct play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB) from Minni, who has been aggressive in previous hands with frequent raises and steals. My holding of 4s9c is offsuit, unconnected, and weak with poor playability postflop. Equity against a typical button opening range is insufficient to justify calling 200 into a 450 pot (needing ~30% equity, but we have far less). Folding preserves stack for better spots in a deep-stack tournament where survival and selective defense maximize EV over 150 hands.

JTo is a strong starting hand on the Button in a 3-handed game. With a 45 BB stack, I have plenty of room to play post-flop, but I want to take the initiative and potentially take down the blinds or define the hand early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

A8o is a standard defending hand from the SB versus a Button open-raise, which is typically wide (40-50% range). Equity realization is decent with Ace blocker, straight potential, and high card strength. Pot odds are favorable (250 to call into 450 pot, ~2.8:1). Folding is too tight preflop OOP; 3-betting is suboptimal as it's not polarized enough and vulnerable to 4-bets/shoves from Flash's 42BB stack. Calling keeps the pot multi-way potentially, allowing postflop play with good playability. Opponents' histories show Flash opens frequently from late position, supporting wide defense.

I have pocket Aces (As Ah) in the big blind facing a button open to 300 with the small blind calling. Flash (button) is short (~4.2k) and likely opening a wide range; Elon (SB) is deep and capable of 3-bets. With the best possible preflop hand my priority is to isolate the short-stacked opener and get as much of his stack in as possible (or build a sizable pot if SB wants to join). Raising small or just calling gives a cheap multiway flop and lets more hands with reasonable equity see a flop; a large 3-bet that forces the button all‑in is the highest EV line here. Raising to 4,500 total exactly puts Flash all‑in for his remaining stack (or folds him), while not committing more of my stack than necessary — if Elon engages I’m willing to get it in with AA. Action: 3-bet to 4,500 to isolate/extract value.

I opened for 300 with JTo, a standard play from the Button. Elon called from the small blind, but Minni then 3-bet to 4500 from the big blind, which effectively puts me all-in. While Minni has shown significant aggression in recent hands, JTo is a marginal hand that is significantly behind her likely value range (pairs, strong Aces). Calling here risks my entire stack of 42 BB on a hand that is likely dominated or at best a coin flip against lower pairs. Since I still have a healthy stack to play with, I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity to commit my chips.

Facing a massive BB squeeze raise to 4500 (15x the initial open) after Flash's BTN raise to 300 and my SB call. This oversized 3-bet sizing strongly indicates a premium range: QQ+, AK, possibly JJ/TT for value or thin value. A8o has only ~30-35% equity against this range (blocks AA/AK but 8h is live minimally), far short of the ~45% needed (4200 to call into 9300 pot). OOP as SB, postflop playability is poor with offsuit Ace-high in a bloated pot. Calling commits ~25% of stack with marginal hand; 4-betting risks cooler or fold equity loss vs likely jam. History shows Minni capable of big aggressive moves when strong (e.g., Hand 6, 71). Preserve stack for superior spots in mid-tournament (76/150 hands). Fold is optimal GTO/exploitative play.

3-handed on the button with QJo offsuit, an excellent position to open wide. QJ has good equity, playability postflop, and can make strong hands. Standard to raise for value and to steal blinds. Min raise is 200, but opening to 3x BB (300) is a standard size to build pot or take it down preflop. Flash is shorter (41BB) and may defend wide, but position allows me to play profitably postflop. Minni deep but button acts last. Folding too tight long-term.

Facing a 3bb open from the button with A♦5♦ in the small blind, the straightforward, +EV line here is to call. I only have to put in 250 more (300 total committed) into a 450 pot — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize the equity of a suited ace out of position against Elon’s wide opening range. A 3‑bet is a viable exploit at times, but with deep effective stacks (Elon ~170bb) and a medium BB (Flash ~41bb) who can still get involved, a larger 3‑bet often creates a multiway or bloated pot where A5s is frequently dominated. Calling preserves stack flexibility, gives me removal/blocker value to the nut ace, and lets me see a flop to evaluate continuation: I can c-bet, check/fold, or extract on favourable boards. If I face a shove from Flash or a large 4‑bet from Elon, I’ll re-evaluate then (likely fold to a large cold 4‑bet, call/shove only vs committed shove sizing with correct pot odds). For now: call 300 total to see the flop and proceed postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five suited, which is a strong hand to defend against a Button raise and a Small Blind call. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700, needing ~22% equity). Given my stack size of 4,100 (41 BBs), I have enough depth to play post-flop without being forced all-in immediately. Calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially hit a nut flush draw or an Ace while keeping the pot manageable against the two much larger stacks.

I have Ad5d on Qs‑8c‑Ah: top pair but a weak 5 kicker. Pot is 900 and it checked to me. This is a relatively dry flop with few draws and a lot of marginal hands (Qx, 8x, medium pairs) in both opponents’ ranges. Betting for value and protection is the best line — I want to charge worse queens and weaker aces, deny free cards, and keep the pot controlled out of position. I’ll size small-to-medium (~1/3 pot = 300) to induce calls from worse hands while not bloating the pot vs Elon’s deep stack. If I face a large, polarized raise or a shove (especially from the short‑stacked BB), I will fold — my 5 kicker makes continuing vs heavy aggression unprofitable without a strong read. Versus a modest single raise I can call and re‑evaluate on the turn. Therefore: bet 300.

I have Top Pair (Aces) with a 5 kicker on an Ace-high board. Minni has led out with a small bet (300 into 900), which could indicate a marginal hand like a Queen, a lower Ace, or a draw. I am getting 4:1 odds on a call. Raising here would likely only get called by better Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and would put too much of my 3,900 chip stack at risk with a weak kicker. Folding is out of the question given I have top pair. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see how Elon, the original pre-flop raiser, reacts behind me.

Button with top pair good kicker (QH JH pairing the Qs) on a dry A-Q-8 rainbow board. Strong value hand that blocks QQ and some AQ. Minni's donk lead could be Ax, weak Qx, or bluffs/draws; Flash's flat call likely weak Ax/draws or floats given short stack. Raising builds pot for value, charges draws (e.g., JT, KT, 97o), denies equity, and leverages position. Pot 1500, facing 300 into ~20% pot. Raising to 1200 (~4x the bet) is balanced size: gets value from worse (Qx, Ax no kicker), pressures short stack Flash (3600 behind). Calling is too passive, risks multiway free cards. Folding trash. Deep stacks but exploiting loose history from opponents who call stations frequently.

I bet 300 on Qs‑8c‑Ah with Ad5d (top pair, weak kicker). Flash (BB) called my small c‑bet and Elon (BTN) shoved pressure by raising to 1,200 total — I must call 900 more into a ~4,500 pot (call would make it ~5,400). Elon’s raise size is very large and is either value‑heavy (AK, AQ, A9/AJ types with better kickers) or a polarised shove; in either case I’m often dominated or face further aggression. There are no backdoor draws to justify gambling a large sum OOP, and the short BB (3,600) can still shove over a call and put me to a tough decision. A 3‑bet is too thin and expensive given my kicker and lack of fold equity. Better to preserve a deep stack for cleaner spots — fold and wait for a better situation.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (5) is extremely weak. Elon, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has made a significant raise to 1,200 on the flop after both the Small Blind led out and I called. This type of raise into two players on an Ace-high board typically represents a very strong range, likely AK, AQ, AJ, or AT, all of which dominate my hand. Even though I am getting roughly 3:1 pot odds to call the additional 900, doing so would commit nearly half of my remaining stack and leave me out of position against a much larger, aggressive stack. In a tournament setting, preserving my 36 BB stack is more important than calling down with a marginal top-pair hand in a spot where I am frequently dominated or facing heavy future pressure.

I open-raise to 300 (3x the BB) with 4♥6♥ from the button. In a three-handed pot I should be playing a wide, speculative range — suited connectors/gappers gain a lot of post‑flop value in position. A 300 sizing is the table standard here, gives good fold equity, helps isolate the short SB (Flash) and keeps the pot manageable if called. If Flash or Elon 3-bet large (or Flash jams), I’ll fold this marginal holding; if I get called I’ll use position to realize equity on heart/connected/paired flops and apply pressure when appropriate.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c7h. Minni, who is the chip leader, raised to 3x from the button. My stack is relatively short at 35.5 big blinds. 97 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against a dominant stack, especially with Elon in the Big Blind still to act. Calling would commit nearly 10% of my remaining stack to a hand that rarely flops well enough to continue aggressively, and 3-betting is too risky given the stack sizes and the strength Minni has shown recently. The most prudent play to preserve my tournament life is to fold.

KJo is a strong offsuit broadway hand from the big blind facing a button raise in 3-handed play. Button opening ranges are wide, especially given Minni's history of frequent aggression and steals. Pot odds are excellent: 450 in pot, 200 to call (roughly 2.25:1), requiring ~31% equity, which KJo exceeds (around 40-45% vs. typical button range). Calling keeps the pot small OOP while realizing equity. Folding is too tight for this spot; 3-betting adds unnecessary risk OOP with a hand that plays better multiway or postflop when improved, and stacks are deep (18k+ effective). Will proceed cautiously postflop, value betting strong hands and bluffing selectively.

TPTK (Ks on Kd) is a very strong hand on this extremely dry rainbow flop (2c Kd 4s) with no obvious draws. OOP as the preflop caller vs. Minni's wide button opening range, which includes many bluffs, Ax, broadway, pockets that miss. Standard play is to check first: pot control OOP with a hand that doesn't want to bloat the pot unnecessarily, induce bluffs/value bets from thinner pairs/draws, and set up check-raises for value/protection. Donk betting OOP is non-standard and risks folding out worse while getting raised by better. Pot odds irrelevant since facing no bet yet. Will check-raise medium if she bets small/medium, call larger, proceed cautiously on turns that connect.

I have middle pair (4h) on a dry K♦-2♣-4♠ rainbow flop, acting last versus a Big Blind caller. This is a textbook spot to bet for thin value and to deny free cards to overcards — my hand is good versus Ace/Queen/Jack-high type floats that call preflop but loses to Kx or sets. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (325) to extract from worse hands and keep the pot controllable against stronger holdings. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check or small barreI depending on the card and action); if he check-raises I will give up most of the time since my pair is too vulnerable to continue facing strong aggression.

Facing a half-pot bet of 325 into 650 on a dry rainbow flop (2c Kd 4s) with TPTK (KsJh). Minni's button range is wide in 3-handed play, including bluffs, weaker Ax/Kx, missed broadways/pockets. TPTK crushes her bluffing range and has great equity even vs value (beats KQ/KT/K9 worse kickers, AJ+, pairs below). Pot odds excellent (~3:1), but check-raising for thin value/protection is optimal OOP: extracts from worse (Kx, Tx, Ax turning pair), denies equity to gutshots/spades (~6% of range), and folds out pure bluffs while keeping initiative. Calling risks giving free cards or multiway pots; flat too passive. Size to pot (975) balances value from calls and fold equity from bluffs/draws. Stacks deep, so proceed cautiously postflop. Minni aggressive historically, likely to hero with worse.

I bet the dry K♦‑2♣‑4♠ flop for thin value with middle pair (4♥) and was met with a check‑raise to 975. A check‑raise of this size on a rainbow, low‑texture board polarizes Elon's range heavily toward strong value hands (Kx, sets like 22/44, occasionally two pair or a very rare big trap) — bluffs are possible but not common here given the board and that he cold‑called preflop. Calling 650 into the ~2600 pot (needs ~25% equity) against a polarized value‑heavy range is a poor long‑term play; raising as a bluff or protection would commit a lot of chips with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. I planned to give up to a check‑raise in this exact spot, so I fold to preserve my deep stack and wait for a better, less dominated spot in position.

Holding Queen-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a strong opening candidate. With a stack of 35.5 big blinds, I am the short stack compared to the blinds, but I still have enough leverage to play. Opening for 3x the big blind is a standard play here to take control of the pot and utilize positional advantage on later streets.

5h9c is a trash hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the small blind. Flash's button raise to 300 is standard and his range is wide but still dominated by playable hands like pairs, suited connectors, Ax, or broadway cards. Calling 250 into 450 offers insufficient odds given the positional disadvantage and high likelihood of being behind post-flop. Raising or shoving is -EV with this holding against Flash's range and Minni behind. Best to fold and wait for better spots with 71 hands remaining and a deep stack.

Call 300 total. Facing a button open to 300 from the Button with A♥7♠ in the Big Blind, I want to defend rather than fold or bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet. A7o has an ace-blocker and decent postflop playability; calling 200 more gets good pot odds (~200 to win ~650) and keeps the pot manageable out of position. A min-raise to 500 has limited fold equity here and risks creating a bigger OOP pot with a weak kicker, so I prefer to call and evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

I defended the BB preflop with A♥7♠ and the flop is a very dry, low, rainbow 3♥-2♠-5♦. I have only Ace-high (with an Ace blocker to some A3/A5 combos) and I'm out of position. Betting OOP here unnecessarily bloats the pot and hands me a decision I don’t want (a short-stacked Button can shove and force a big, marginal call). Pot control is the priority: checking keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize showdown value for free if he checks back, and lets me call a reasonable c-bet without committing too much. If Flash bets small I will call and re-evaluate; if he jams I’ll use pot odds and reads to decide, but I’m not going to inflate the pot proactively with a marginal hand.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage on this relatively dry flop, even if my specific hand (Queen-Ten) didn't connect. Minni's check is expected, and a standard continuation bet of about half the pot (300 chips) is a solid play to take down the pot now or define the strength of her hand. If she has a small pair or a draw like A4 or 46, she will likely call, but a bet forces her to fold her 'air' hands that still have some equity against my Queen-high.

Call 300. This is a very dry, low, rainbow flop (3h-2s-5d) and Flash’s bet is small (300 into a 950 pot) — it gives me ~19% break-even equity to continue, which is well within reason versus a wide button c-bet range. I have the Ace blocker to a few A3/A5 two‑pair combos and decent showdown value vs many of his unpaired overcards and bluffs. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin raise or fold away too much equity; calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me see a turn, and lets me re-evaluate based on his turn sizing. If he jams a large amount on the turn I’ll use pot odds and his line to decide (likely fold to an all-in unless I’m getting correct odds), but for now a call is the lowest-variance, +EV line.

Check. I only have Ace-high (A♥7♠) on K♥-3♥-2♠-5♦ — no made pair and only four river outs (the four 4s) to make the wheel, so my equity is low. The pot is small (1,250) and Flash is short (≈2,950). Betting out OOP would needlessly bloat the pot and expose me to a check-raise or shove that I should fold to with only Ace-high (calling a shove here requires ~41% equity). Flash checked the turn, so his range includes slow-played Kx, some pocket pairs blocking the river, and a lot of missed hands — checking preserves pot control and lets me realize my showdown equity for cheap. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call small/medium bets to realize equity, fold to large shoves that don’t give me correct odds.

The King on the turn is an excellent scare card that significantly favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. After Minni check-called the flop, her range likely consists of mid-pocket pairs (66-TT), some Ace-high hands, or small pieces of the board like a pair of fives. By betting the turn, I am continuing the narrative of a strong hand (representing AK, KQ, or KJ) which puts immense pressure on her holding. A bet of roughly half the pot is large enough to force folds from her 'marginal' hands while keeping my stack depth manageable for a potential river decision.

Call 625. Flash (short-stacked Button) has shown aggression (open preflop, small flop c-bet, medium turn barrel) but his range is still wide on such a dry board — it contains bluffs/overcards and some Kx/value hands. I only have Ace-high (A♥7♠) and my clean outs are the three remaining Aces (~3 outs, ~6.5% to hit the river), so versus a pure Kx range I’m behind — but versus a continuing double-barrel range that includes many bluffs I have reasonable equity. Calling 625 into a 1,875 pot requires ~25% equity; calling keeps the pot controlled out of position and lets me fold to a large river shove if he polarizes. Raising would bloat the pot OOP and can force an awkward all‑in decision with only Ace-high, while folding concedes too much against his missed hands. Plan: call now, reevaluate the river (call small/medium river bets to realize equity, fold to large shoves that aren’t priced correctly).

Check. I have only Ace-high (A♥7♠) on a dry, low board (3♥-2♠-5♦-K♥-6♦) — no pair and very little showdown improvement. Flash has been aggressive (BTN open, flop c-bet, turn barrel) and is short (~2.3k), so betting OOP here either folds out worse hands (no value) or induces a shove I should fold to. Checking preserves pot control, lets him bluff or value-shove, and allows me to call small river bets to realize equity. If he jams his remaining stack into the ~2.5k pot I’d need roughly ~32% equity to call, which A-high does not have versus his likely value-heavy line, so I would fold a shove. Check now and reassess if he bets — call small/medium bets, fold large shoves.

I attempted to represent a King or better by firing a second barrel on the turn after my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. However, Minni's decision to check-call both the flop and the turn on a relatively low, connected board (3-2-5-K) indicates she likely has a made hand, such as a mid-pocket pair, a pair of fives, or even a King herself. With my stack currently at roughly 23 big blinds, a third-barrel bluff on the river would essentially be an all-in move. Given that Minni has shown resistance on two streets and is a large stack who may be inclined to call down a shorter stack, checking behind and conceding the pot is the more disciplined play. I need to preserve my remaining chips to find a better spot to double up rather than risking my tournament life on a high-variance bluff against a range that has shown strength.

Preflop on the button with 8h2d, a trash hand with no connectivity, low cards, and offsuit. Assuming SB folded (pot 150 suggests dead SB blind) and BB checked, leaving an unraised pot. While button can widen range for steals, 82o has terrible postflop playability, makes few strong hands, and is easily dominated. BB has 22BB and might defend wide, but folding preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacked tournament, no need to play marginal hands this early in hand.

I have pocket tens (TT) in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind (Flash, ~2,225 chips). This is a strong preflop hand and with the button folded I want to build the pot and isolate the shorter-stacked BB rather than limp and give them a cheap flop. Min raise is 200, but I size modestly to 300 total to extract value from worse pairs/broadways and keep some fold equity. If Flash shoves I will call — TT is ahead of a typical shove range and calling won’t put my deep stack at undue risk. Raise to 300 to take the initiative and maximize EV here.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine of clubs (Qc 9c), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game, particularly when facing a raise from the Small Blind. I have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my position to realize equity without committing too much of my 22 BB stack pre-flop. Folding would be too tight given the strength of my hand and the pot odds (2:1), while raising (3-betting) might unnecessarily polarize my range or force me into a difficult decision if Minni shoves.

Flop: K♦ 6♣ 3♦. I hold T♦T♥. Heads-up vs a short-stacked BB who just flat-called my preflop raise. Tens are behind any K but ahead of the bulk of his defending range (we beat most pocket pairs below TT, many Ax without a K, and most broadways/suited connectors). The board is relatively dry aside from two diamonds (so some one-card flush draws exist if he holds two diamonds). Plan: continuation-bet to extract value from worse pairs/floats and to charge draws while keeping fold equity vs garbage hands. I’ll size ~1/2 pot (300 into 600) — big enough to fold out air and get value, but not so large that I’m overcommitted if I face strong resistance. If Flash jams with his short stack I’ll call — TT is often ahead of a shove-range from a short-stacked caller. If he just calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and pot-control or apply further pressure depending on the card.

On a King-high board (Kd 6c 3d), my Queen-high hand (Qc 9c) has very little direct equity. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor from the Small Blind, is likely to have a King or at least high-card superiority in her range. While she C-bets frequently, my stack size (approximately 20 BBs) doesn't allow for much maneuverability to float or semi-bluff on such a dry board without a significant draw. Folding here preserves my stack to look for a better opportunity to double up or exert pressure in a more favorable situation.

I'm on the button with T♦2♦ — a weak, but suited, hand. In 3‑handed play position is extremely valuable and open‑raising is the +EV line versus limping or folding. Limping hands like this often get squeezed or deny initiative; folding is too tight given deep stacks and the opportunity to steal. I'll open to a standard 3xBB sizing (total 300) — big enough to take the pot down against the blinds and to avoid cheap multiway flops, but not so large it commits me unnecessarily. Flash (SB) is short (~20BB) and will often fold or shove; I will fold to an SB shove or a large, committed 3‑bet from Elon with this hand, but if called I can play postflop in position with some flush and ten‑pair equity. Raise to 300 to apply pressure and isolate/fold out marginal blind defense.

I am holding Ten-Deuce offsuit in the small blind, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from the button, and although I am the short stack with roughly 20 big blinds, this is not a hand worth defending or shoving with. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining chips.

In the big blind facing a button open-raise to 300 after SB folds, my KQo is a strong hand that plays well against a wide button range, which Minni often employs based on history of frequent raises. KQ offsuit dominates many of her calling hands like Axo, broadway, suited connectors. Position is OOP, but deep stacks (190BB effective) favor 3-betting for value, initiative, and fold equity. Flash is short at 20BB and folded, reducing multiway risk. Pot odds are good but better to 3-bet to ~3x the raise (standard sizing observed in prior hands like 300 to 900). Raising to 900 builds pot with equity advantage and protects against floats/set mines.

Elon 3‑bet to 900 after my button open to 300. I have T♦2♦ — weak but suited and in position in a 3‑handed pot, so I want to realize equity and keep the pot manageable. Pot is 1250 and I must call 600 more (900 total), so I need ~600/(1250+600) ≈ 32.4% equity to break even. That’s reasonable to pursue in position against a likely mixed 3‑bet range; 4‑betting to 1500 is too thin and would risk building a pot I’m not prepared to commit to with this holding. I’ll call 900 total, see a flop, and fold to large further aggression or proceed aggressively only if I hit meaningful diamonds/ten/top-pair or a favorable board texture.

Pre-flop 3-bet with KQo was correct for value and initiative against Minni's button calling range, which includes broadway, Ax, suited connectors that we dominate. Flop 3s4s7d is fairly dry but with spade flush draw and wheel straight draws (5x open-ender). No pair or draw, but KQ are live overcards blocking top of her range (KQ, AK). As OOP aggressor post-flop, continue with a standard c-bet ~2/3 pot (1200 into 1850) to deny equity to her draws/floats, build pot if called with equity, and maintain fold equity against missed hands like Axo, lower pairs. Her history shows calling but folding to post-flop aggression (e.g., Hand 1,21,55). Deep stacks favor pressure; check risks giving free card to draws. Flash out, heads-up.

Board is 3♠-4♠-7♦; I hold T♦-2♦ (no pair, only a backdoor diamond draw). Elon 3‑bet preflop and now bets 1,200 into the pot (pot is 3,050). Calling 1,200 requires about 1,200/(3,050+1,200) ≈ 28.2% equity to break even. My clean outs are limited: 3 tens + 3 twos to pair (~24% to pair by river) and a small (~4%) chance to make the backdoor diamond flush by river — and many of those pair/flush outcomes will still lose to Elon's likely 3‑bet/c‑bet range (overpairs, top pairs, A/K, spade draws). With realistic equity below the required threshold and deep stacks/position available for better spots, this is a clear fold — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am currently the short stack with roughly 20 big blinds, while my two opponents have significant chip leads. King-Four offsuit is a marginal hand even in a three-handed game. Given that both Elon and Minni have been playing very aggressively and frequently 3-betting or applying pressure to short stacks in previous hands, opening with K4o on the button is risky. If I raise and one of the big stacks shoves, I would be forced to fold and lose a significant portion of my stack or call with a dominated hand. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit my chips.

Preflop in SB vs BB heads-up after BTN fold. Ts7s is a speculative suited connector with good playability, straight and flush potential on deep stacks (~200BB). SB should raise a wide range (~40-50%) for initiative and fold equity. BB defends wide based on history of frequent calls and postflop check/calls. Min-raise to 200 total or small raise to 250/300 builds pot without overcommitting. Choosing raise to 250 (2.5x BB) for balanced sizing, good vs calling range, and position advantage postflop.

I have Q♣? (Q♦J♠) in the big blind facing Elon's open to 250 (pot 350). Calling costs me 150 more (total committed 250) for good pot odds and QJ offsuit is a strong defending hand heads‑up against a likely wide SB-opening range. A minimum raise to 400 is too small to fold him out or to credibly polarize my range — and a larger 3‑bet would be a commitment out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll call, keep the pot controlled, see a flop, and proceed based on texture: continue with top pair/strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression with missed boards.

Preflop raiser OOP with initiative after BTN folds. Flop 9s6d2h is fairly dry (rainbow, disconnected low cards), favoring my perceived range which includes Ax, broadway, pocket pairs, and suited connectors that hit pairs or draws. Ts7s blocks some straight draws (T9, 87s, 76s) and has a gutshot straight draw (8x for T9876), backdoor spade flush draw (one spade on board), and T-overcard blocker. Minni's BB calling range is wide (pairs, suited broadways, Ax) but often weak; history shows frequent calls but folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 81). C-bet small ~40% pot exploits fold equity, denies equity to her floats/draws, builds pot with equity, and balances value. Checking risks giving free card to her range or facing bet OOP. Deep stacks allow flexibility postflop.

Elon (SB) made a small c‑bet (200 into a 500 pre‑bet pot → pot is 700). I hold Q♦J♠ — no pair but two overcards and small backdoor equity. Calling 200 to win 700 gives ~22% pot‑odds, and versus a likely wide SB c‑bet range my equity is in that ballpark. Raising out of position with a marginal hand is unnecessary and folding gives up too much equity.
