This is a textbook spot to open-raise for position and fold equity — the hand has suitedness and playability postflop and I can pressure the short-stacked small blind (≈22BB) and the big blind. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB): big enough to apply pressure/isolate the SB but small enough to keep the pot manageable with this marginal hand. If Flash (SB) shoves I’ll call or evaluate based on shove sizing and ranges (he’s likely wide); if Elon 3-bets large I’ll usually fold T7d out of position. If called and heads-up I’ll play aggressively in position; if it becomes multiway I’ll proceed cautiously and avoid bloating the pot on unfavorable boards.

I am the short stack with approximately 22 big blinds in a 3-handed game. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the button, which is a high-frequency play for a wide range of hands. While K7o has some equity against a button range, playing it out of position against a big stack is difficult. With my current stack size, I should avoid marginal calls that lead to difficult post-flop decisions. I will fold and look for a more advantageous spot to commit my chips, such as a hand with better playability or a stronger 'push or fold' candidate.

9c2d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no playability, straight potential, or suited value. Facing a standard button raise from Minni, who is aggressive but this range still crushes 92o. Pot odds require ~23% equity to call 200 into 650, but 92o has far less vs any reasonable opening range (top ~40-50%). Out of position postflop makes it even worse. Deep stacked with 45 hands left; fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

As the short stack on the button in a three-handed game, I need to be proactive in picking up blinds. Q6s is a reasonable hand to open for a steal or to play in position. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing that allows me to represent strength and potentially take the pot down pre-flop, while still giving me the flexibility to fold to a significant 3-bet from the much larger stacks in the blinds.

Preflop from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). Qd5d is a suited queen-high hand with some flush and straight potential, but it's weak overall with a low kicker and poor high-card strength. Button ranges are wide, but calling out of position post-flop is problematic due to difficult playability multiway (BB Minni with deep stack likely defends wide). Pot odds are about 1.8:1, but realized equity OOP is low. Folding preserves my deep stack (15525) for better spots late in the tournament (hand 106/150) where position and stronger hands matter more for stack maximization.

I’m on the big blind with pocket 7s facing a 3bb button open from Flash who is short (~19bb). Pot is 450 and it’s 200 more to call. This is a great spot to 3-bet: 77 is a strong hand versus a wide button open and I have a large stack advantage, so I want fold equity/isolation rather than passively seeing a flop out of position. I’ll raise to ~3x the open (900 total) — it gives good immediate fold equity (win the 450 now if he folds), keeps the price reasonable if he calls, and preserves maneuverability postflop. If Flash jams I have decent equity and can call; folding is too passive and a single flat call concedes too much initiative/position.

I opened on the button with Q6s, which is a standard steal attempt, but Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 900. My remaining stack is 1,912 chips, meaning a call of 600 more would commit nearly 1/3 of my stack. With a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of roughly 0.5 on the flop if I call, I would be effectively pot-committed with a marginal hand that is likely dominated by Minni's 3-betting range. Shoving is also too risky with this specific hand against a chip leader who has shown high aggression. Folding allows me to preserve my 16 BB stack and look for a better opportunity to double up.

Preflop on the button facing what appears to be an unraised pot (SB likely limped/completed for 100 total, BB checked), with K2o offsuit. This is a marginal spot for a steal raise in 3-handed play due to good position, but K2o has very poor post-flop playability: weak kicker, offsuit, no straight potential, and dominated by any calling Kx. Minni (SB) is deep and aggressive, likely to defend wide; Flash (BB) is short ~18BB and may shove wide over a raise. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor multiway, and limp-calling is -EV. Raising min to 200 risks 200 to win ~150 with shaky fold equity against these players based on history of frequent calls/defenses. Folding preserves stack for premium spots with ~44 hands remaining; this hand is bottom-of-range trash not worth risking chips.

7♣2♠ is essentially the worst starting hand — not worth building a bigger pot with. We're heads-up (Elon folded) against Flash in the big blind who is relatively short (~18BB). The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 more to complete (total commitment 100), giving ~4:1 pot odds to see a flop. Raising to 200 risks inducing a shove from the short stack and needlessly risking chips with a garbage hand. I'll complete the blind (call) cheaply to see a flop and fold to strong preflop shove or heavy postflop aggression if I don't improve.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-5 offsuit. The Button (Elon) has folded, and the Small Blind (Minni) has just called the blind. With 1812 chips, I am the short stack at the table. Raising here with a weak hand like 7-5 offsuit against a massive stack like Minni is unnecessarily risky, as she has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and potentially realize equity if I hit a favorable board. Therefore, I will check my option.

Flop: 9♠ 7♦ 3♥ — I have 7♣2♠: one pair (sevens) with a terrible kicker. Pot is 200 and it’s checked to me. Flash (BB) is short (~18BB) and will defend wide, so my pair is ahead of a lot of his checking/calling range (overcards, weak broadways, many bluffs). The board is pretty dry (no real flush, minimal straight draw), so betting now gets value from worse pairs and folds out unpaired overcards while denying free cards.

Plan: take the initiative with a half‑pot c-bet (100). That size gets reasonable fold equity against a short, non‑committal BB yet doesn’t needlessly commit a big portion of my deep stack if he jams. If he simply calls I can re-evaluate on the turn; if he jams I’ll re‑evaluate based on his shove range but I’m prepared to fold to clear strength (e.g. a line that strongly represents 9x/pocket pairs) and call only when his shove looks light enough that my pair has sufficient equity.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a dry board of 9-7-3. In a limped pot, Minni (the small blind) has led out for 100 into a 200 pot. This is a standard continuation/probing bet. While my kicker is weak, folding middle pair to a single half-pot bet in this situation is too conservative. I will call the 100 to see a turn card and evaluate further. Raising would be unwise as it bloats the pot with a marginal hand and puts my tournament life at risk unnecessarily against the chip leader.

I have 7♣2♠ — middle pair with a very weak kicker. I c‑bet the flop and Flash (short, ~17BB) called, so his continuing range contains lots of hands that the A♣ on the turn now either pairs or otherwise improves (A‑x, A9/A3, 9x, sets) and also hands that can profitably shove. Betting the turn would build a pot where I’m often getting value only from hands that already beat me and where a shove from the short stack would put me to a very difficult decision with a marginal holding. Checking controls the pot, preserves fold equity against a shove, and lets me call small/medium bets for pot odds or see a cheap river if he checks back. Plan: check and re‑evaluate — call small turn bets if reasonable, fold to a committed shove unless it looks light.

The turn brings an Ace, which is a significant scare card. Since I have middle pair (Sevens) with a weak kicker (5), my hand has moderate showdown value but is unlikely to get multiple streets of value. By checking behind, I keep the pot small and avoid the risk of being check-raised by the chip leader, Minni. This allows me to see the river for free and evaluate whether to call a small bet or check it down to showdown.

The river (J♦) leaves me with second pair (7♣) and a terrible kicker (2♠). Flash is a short stack (~17BB) who called my flop c‑bet and then played very passively (checked the turn and river so far). A river bet would rarely fold better hands (9x, sets, A9/A3, Jx) and only extract thin value from the small fraction of his range I actually beat (treys, tiny pairs). Betting also risks inducing a shove that I would have to fold or call into negative EV. With pot control and stack preservation in mind, I check to take the showdown for free — if he wants to bluff he can show it; if he shows strength by betting I can fold to committed action.

The board has completed with a Jack of Diamonds. My pair of Sevens has decent showdown value in a limped pot, especially after Minni checked both the turn and the river. Betting here wouldn't accomplish much, as better hands like a Nine, Jack, or Ace are unlikely to fold, and worse hands are unlikely to call. Checking is the most prudent play to get to showdown and preserve my stack.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed pot so position is a big advantage and my opening range should be wide. 5♥8♦ is a weak, marginal holding but is open-raiseable here to pick up the blinds and play post‑flop in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — big enough to put pressure on the short-stacked SB (Flash, ~1,862) and discourage loose calls, but small relative to my 22,563 stack if Elon decides to 3‑bet. Plan: if Elon 3-bets to a large size I’ll fold this hand; if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet thin on dry, favorable flops and play cautiously on wet/connected boards to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Minni has raised from the Button, which is a wide opening range. With a stack of 1862 (approximately 18.6 big blinds), I am in a prime position to re-shove (3-bet all-in). This maximizes my fold equity against the Button's opening range while ensuring I realize the full equity of my hand. Calling would put me out of position with a very low stack-to-pot ratio on the flop, making it difficult to play optimally. Given my stack size, an all-in is the most effective play to either take down the pot pre-flop or get my money in as a favorite.

KQo is a strong hand in the BB facing a button open-raise from Minni (wide range) and a large 3bet from short-stacked Flash in the SB (now with only 50 chips left after committing 1862, effectively all-in). Pot odds are excellent: calling 1762 into a 2262 pot offers ~1.28:1 immediate odds (requiring ~44% equity), but multiway potential and dead money improve it further. Vs Flash's polarized shoving range (premiums or bluffs exploiting dead money), KQo has solid equity (~40-50% vs typical 3bet shove range) and plays well in a low-SPR spot (~0.2 SPR post-call heads-up) even OOP. Minni acts last and may fold weaker hands, isolating vs Flash or allowing postflop decisions. Folding surrenders too much EV in a tournament spot with 42 hands left and deep effective stacks (my 154BB). Calling maximizes stack growth by realizing equity against short stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with a marginal hand (5♥8♦) to leverage position, but Flash’s large 3‑bet to 1862 and Elon’s call has turned this into a multiway pot against strong, polarized ranges. Calling 1,562 into ~4,024 (pot odds ~2.5–2.8:1) looks tempting given the price, but my hand has poor equity and playability multiway and I’ll frequently be dominated or forced to fold to continuation aggression on many flops. A 4‑bet shove with 5‑8 offsuit would be unnecessarily risky and not +EV here. Preserve the deep stack for better spots — fold.

I have only 50 chips remaining in a pot of 4024. With Ace-Jack on a Queen-high board, I have two overcards and must realize my equity. Betting my final 50 chips puts me all-in, ensuring I see the turn and river to potentially improve and win this significant pot. Given the pot odds, there is no scenario where folding or checking is superior to committing the last of my stack.

Facing a tiny all-in bet of 50 into a 4074 pot (~82:1 odds), this is essentially free money. KQs gives top pair top kicker on 6s7sQd, a strong holding that blocks some of Flash's flush draws (holds Qs spade blocker) and has excellent equity vs. their short-stack polarized preflop shoving range (bluffs, lower pairs, gutshots, flush/straight draws). No fold or raise possible/needed as opponent is all-in; calling maximizes EV with minimal risk in late tournament stages (hand 108/150), preserving stack growth potential.

In a 3-handed game, 9-8 offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button to exert pressure on the blinds and take advantage of position. With approximately 41 big blinds, I am the short stack at the table, but I have enough chips to play post-flop. I will raise to 300, which is a standard 3x opening. If either of the larger stacks 3-bets me significantly, I can comfortably fold without compromising my stack, but I want to maintain an aggressive button strategy.

Hand 6h3c is extremely weak: offsuit, low cards, poor connectivity, and no high card value. Facing a button raise from Flash who has been aggressive in steals (evident from history like hands 13,37,70), defending SB light is unnecessary here. My stack is healthy at ~136BB, pot odds to call 250 into 450 (~36% implied but hand has zero equity/showdown value). Standard fold to preserve chips for premium spots in late tournament with 41 hands remaining and ICM pressure minimal given stack sizes. Minni still to act but irrelevant for decision.

I’m in the big blind with A♥2♦ facing a button open to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (total 300 committed including my BB). Calling gives ~30.8% pot odds (200 to win 650) and A2o has comfortable equity versus a typical button opening range, so this is +EV. I prefer to call rather than 3-bet: a 3-bet risks committing chips and can induce a shove from the 38bb opponent, forcing marginal all-in decisions with a weak kicker. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and play postflop (look for an ace or good runout to extract value or fold to heavy aggression).

I have A♥2♦ on T♥ 2♠ Q♣ — a marginal made hand: a single deuce with an Ace kicker. The button checked to me, which signals a lot of missed hands or pot-control rather than a strong value line. The flop is relatively dry (no real flush draws and limited straight possibilities), so there’s little need to bet for protection. Betting here would risk getting check‑raised by Qx/TX/sets and bloating the pot with only middle pair. I want to keep the pot under control and realize equity against his checking range — check back and evaluate the turn. If he bets a small amount on a safe turn I can call for value; if he shows heavy aggression I can fold and preserve a large stack for better spots.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of Queen-Ten-Deuce is quite favorable for a button opening range, which includes many high cards and pairs. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot. This exerts pressure on her likely wide defending range and allows me to take the pot down now or potentially improve on the turn.

I have A♥2♦ on T♥-2♠-Q♣: a vulnerable but real pair of deuces with the Ace kicker. Flash (BTN) has made a small, standard c-bet (300) into the pot — a sizing consistent with a wide continuation range or a thin value bet. This hand has decent showdown equity versus his likely opening/c-bet range (we beat a lot of air and some Ax/low pairs), plus a few clean outs (two remaining deuces and three aces) to improve. Raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and can induce a shove or a check‑raise by T/Q/sets where we’re behind; folding is too nitty given our equity and the price. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and reevaluate on the turn (extract value from worse hands or fold to heavy turn aggression).

Board: T♥‑2♠‑Q♣‑5♣. I hold A♥2♦ — a single deuce with the best possible kicker against many bluffs, but still a very vulnerable pair versus Top Pair/sets. Flash (BTN) opened wide preflop and made a modest flop c‑bet (300) that looks like a wide continuation range; I called the flop to realize equity and keep the pot controlled. The 5♣ on the turn is essentially a blank — it doesn’t complete dangerous draws and it doesn’t improve our hand relative to likely value hands (T/Q/sets). Betting or raising here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and will often get value/pressure from hands that beat us or fold out worse hands we want to see at showdown. Checking preserves my stack, keeps Flash’s range wide (can induce bluffs or thin value bets on the river), and lets me call reasonable river bets with decent showdown equity. Plan: pot control now, call small/medium river bets for value but fold to large, polarized aggression. Action: check.

The 5c on the turn is largely a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Having been called on the flop, it's likely Minni has a marginal hand like a Ten or a Queen, or potentially a straight draw like J-9 or K-J. Since I only have 9-high with a gutshot straight draw, checking back is a prudent move to realize my equity for free and avoid inflating the pot further while I am the short stack. This allows me to see the river and decide my final action based on whether I hit my Jack or if Minni shows further weakness.

I hold A♥2♦ on Th‑2s‑Qc‑5c‑Qd — that’s two pair (Qs and 2s) with an Ace kicker. Line summary: Flash (BTN) opened wide preflop, c‑bet the flop (300) and I called, then both of us checked the turn. The river paired the board (Qd), which both (1) gives a lot of his previous flop-check range trips/full houses if he held a Q or specific pair combos, and (2) reduces the value of a thin river bet because many worse hands will fold while many better hands will now either call or trap.

Hands that now beat me: any Qx (trips/full houses), any Tx (Q+T two‑pair beats my Q+2), and pocket pairs that make full houses (TT, 22, 55) or combinations that produce a full house. Hands I still beat are missed overcards and some weaker pairs, but many of those are unlikely to call a sizeable river bet after he checked the turn. Flash’s line (wide open, flop c‑bet, turn check) contains a healthy mix of weak c‑bets and controllable value hands — which means a river bet is unlikely to extract much value but will get me in trouble vs the portion of his range that improved. There’s limited fold equity and significant downside if I bet and he check‑raises or jams with trips/full house.

Given those factors I prefer pot control and showdown: check and take it to a showdown. Betting here would often fold out the hands I beat and get called/raised by the hands that beat me, so checking is the +EV, lower‑variance line.

Action: check.

On this board of Th-2s-Qc-5c-Qd, I have 9-high with no showdown value. I was the pre-flop aggressor and fired a continuation bet on the flop, which Minni called. After we both checked the turn, Minni has checked the river as well. While her double check might signal weakness (like a missed draw or a weak Ten), my turn check significantly weakened the story that I have a strong Queen. Given that I am the short stack and Minni has a massive chip lead, she is likely to call a river bluff with any Ten or even some Ace-high hands just to keep me honest. Since I have no chance of winning at showdown, but I also have a low probability of successfully bluffing a large stack on this board texture after checking the turn, I will check behind and concede the pot to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

79o from the button in a 3-handed pot facing a small bet of 100 into 150 (pot odds ~2.5:1 after call). Position is excellent, and the hand has straight potential with two broadway cards. Equity vs wide SB/BB opening ranges is sufficient (~35-40%), and implied odds are good with deep effective stacks (Flash shorter at 34BB but Minni deep). History shows opponents often check/call passively postflop, allowing value if we hit. Raising risks shortstack shove from Flash or isolation from Minni; folding too tight IP late in tournament when building stack. Cheap call to realize equity and play postflop in position.

I’m in the small blind with 7♣2♣. Pot is 250 and I only need to put another 50 to see a flop, but calling keeps this a multiway pot out of position with essentially no showdown value. Elon (button) limped a wide range and Flash (BB) is relatively short (~34BB) and often folds to aggression. I want to exploit that: a small isolation raise pressures the limp and the short stack, often picking up the pot preflop or isolating the weaker limper. I’ll min-raise to 200 total to maximize fold equity while keeping my commitment small. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop (look to c-bet only on favorable textures or check and fold to strong resistance).

I am in the Big Blind holding Kh Tc. Elon limped from the Button and Minni made a small raise to 200 from the Small Blind. I am getting excellent pot odds (100 to call into a pot of 400) to see a flop. King-Ten is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. While a 3-bet is possible, calling allows me to see a flop relatively cheaply and evaluate my position, especially since my stack is significantly smaller than my opponents'.

79o on the button facing a minraise to 200 total after my limp (now additional 100 into 500 pot, ~5:1 odds). Excellent position postflop in 3-handed play. Hand has decent playability with straight draw potential (78 broadway connector-ish), live cards, and reverse implied odds minimized deep vs Minni. Equity vs SB wide raising range after limp/call (~40%+ multiway) and BB calling range sufficient. Flash short (33BB) unlikely to shove light over call. Opponents historically passive postflop (frequent check/calls), allowing control and value extraction if improve. Late in tournament (110/150), prefer realizing equity IP multiway over folding marginal spot or risking raise getting 3bet/shoved. Call keeps pot small, plays postflop optimally.

I check. I opened preflop to isolate with 7♣2♣ but both Elon (button limp) and Flash (BB) called, so this is a 3‑way pot (pot 600) and I’m out of position. On K♠-6♦-3♥ I have no pair and essentially no real draws — with two board cards left and no clubs on the flop I cannot make a flush, and my only realistic outs are small pairs (2s/7s) which are weak even if they hit. The King heavily favors the callers’ ranges (limpers and the BB), so a continuation bet here would have poor fold equity multiway. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve chips; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if I face a very small probe I can reasonably exploit or if the action checks through and a very favorable turn appears (then re-evaluate).

I have flopped top pair with a King on a very dry board (6-K-3 rainbow). Minni, who made a small raise pre-flop, has checked. In a three-way pot, checking here is a balanced play. It allows me to see how Elon (on the button) reacts and can potentially induce a bluff from weaker hands or air. If it checks through, I can evaluate the turn; if Elon bets, I have a very comfortable call to see a turn card while keeping the pot size manageable.

Flop of 6d-Ks-3h is king-high with no flush draws really developing and disconnected low cards. My 97o has no pair, no made draw (minor backdoor straight potential with 7 between 6 and 9, but gutshot to 8 for 6-7-8-9-T, incomplete), and 9-high showdown value is poor, losing to any pair or better which both opponents could have (Minni raised preflop as SB wide, Flash defended BB wide). However, both checked to me on button in position, signaling weakness—Minni's check after her preflop raise suggests missed king or weak ace/draw/trap unlikely given passive history; Flash passive defender. Multiway pot (600) but shortstack Flash (33BB) folds often to aggression postflop, Minni deep but checks a lot (history of check/calls). Late tournament (110/150, 3-handed final table push), prioritize folding equity to chip up without showdown. C-bet 50% pot (300) exploits position, range advantage (limp-call pre as button wide), high fold equity (~60-70% combined based on passivity), thin value vs pure air/draws. If called, re-eval turn with position; check-fold turn likely if big bet. EV+ spot over checking (pots uncontested or induces bluffs). Avoid overbet risking shove from shorty.

I’m OOP in a 3‑way pot with 7♣2♣ on K♠-6♦-3♥. I checked the flop and Elon sized up to 300 into a 600 pot (pot now 900) — to continue I must call 300 or raise to at least 600. Calling 300 for a 900 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity) but my hand has essentially no showdown value: no pair, no real draw (only a remote backdoor club runner‑runner), so my equity vs a typical c‑bet range (Kx, pairs, draws) is well below break‑even. Multiway and out of position further reduce the viability of a speculative call or a bluff raise — raising to isolate is doable sometimes versus very weak players, but here Elon led the action and Flash is in the hand, so I’ll be playing a big pot with air. Standard, low-variance play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I have flopped top pair with King-Ten on a very dry board (6-K-3 rainbow). After checking to the button, Elon has bet 300 into a 600 pot. This is a standard probe bet. Given the lack of draws on this board, there is little reason to raise and potentially drive away worse hands or bluffs. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, evaluate the turn, and induce Elon to continue betting with a wider range of hands that I currently beat.

I have top pair (Kings) with a Ten kicker. The Jack on the turn is relatively safe, though it adds some potential straight draws and improves hands like KJ. However, given the pre-flop action (limp and min-raise), Elon's range remains wide. Having checked and called on the flop, checking again on the turn is the most logical play. It allows me to maintain pot control, catch further bluffs from Elon, and avoid bloating the pot against potentially stronger hands. If Elon bets again, I have a very easy call.

Turn board Jd is a blankish card that completes some straight draws like 9T or QT but doesn't heavily favor Flash's flop calling range (likely Kx weak kicker, mid pairs like 88/TT, stubborn draws that missed). I have position OOP heads-up now after Minni folded, with a polarized range from flop c-bet (value: KQ/KJ/sets/AK; bluffs: suited connectors/air realizing fold equity). Flash's check again signals weakness or trap unlikely given passive history (frequent check/calls to river but folds to consistent aggression). Pure air but excellent spot to continue bluff: pot 1200, bet ~2/3 pot (800) applies max pressure on his ~30BB stack (3024 left), offers bad odds (~1.5:1), exploits capped range. High fold equity (~65%+) as he can't call profitably without top pair/top kicker or better, which he'd check-raise flop often. Builds stack late (110/150 hands, 3 left) by winning uncontested pots IP vs shortstack. If called/shoved, fold; river shove over check feasible if checks through. Superior to checking (gives free card, commits river, lower EV).

I have top pair with King-Ten. The Jack of diamonds on the turn is a somewhat significant card as it connects with the board, but given Elon's pre-flop limp, it is difficult to put him on a specific range. His bet of 800 into a 1200 pot represents strength, but my top pair is too strong to fold in a 3-handed game against a wide button range. Calling allows me to control the pot size and reach a showdown while keeping his potential bluffs or weaker Kings in the pot. Raising would likely only get called by hands that have me beat.

On the river, the board has paired the sixes. My hand is top pair (Kings) with a ten kicker. Having check-called both the flop and the turn, my hand remains disguised but also reactive. In a heads-up situation on the river against Elon, who limped on the button and has shown aggression on previous streets, checking is the most prudent play. It allows Elon to check back with marginal hands that might not call a bet, or to continue bluffing with missed draws. If Elon bets, I will have a difficult decision, but for now, checking maintains pot control and follows the defensive line I've taken throughout the hand.

River 6h pairs the 6d, creating a paired board with possible trips, full houses (e.g., 66, K6, J6, 36), but no flush completes and straight draws (e.g., QT, 45) miss. Heads-up vs Flash (BB, 2224 left ~22BB) after Minni folded flop; he check-called flop 300 (pot ~900 after) and turn 800 (pot ~2800 now), capping his range tightly: primarily weak Kx (KQo/KJo/KTo/K9o, suited), top pair weaker kicker, mid-pairs (TT/99/88/JJ unlikely check-call twice), stubborn draws that missed (e.g., heart flush draw like Ahxh since 3h6h board), or slowplayed monsters (66 trips/set unlikely preflop wide defend, KK rare). My pure air 97o (9-high, backdoor potential missed entirely) has zero showdown equity vs his capped range—loses to any pair/draw. Position on button, deep stack (12263 ~122BB), late tournament (110/150 hands, 3-handed). Continuation bluff sizing (e.g., pot bet ~2800 puts him all-in for 2224, odds ~1.25:1) offers poor odds and low fold equity (~25-35% max; passives like Flash call down light per history of river check-calls with air/weak pairs, e.g., Hands #2,8,14,17+, won't fold two-pair+ or even TPNK). EV negative: ~0.3*5024 - 0.7*12263 <0 heavily. Checking preserves stack, denies equity realization if he bets (unlikely), takes uncontested pot if he bluffs (rare), and showdowns ace-high/draw possible tiny % (but still lose). Superior to betting thin (no value) or shoving (variance high late ICM). Fold equity absent after double barrel; check exploits passivity, maximizes EV long-term stack growth.

I have A♣ K♠ on the button — a top-tier, high-equity hand in position against two blinds. With only three players active, I want to take the initiative and isolate the blinds rather than limp. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) applies pressure to Flash (SB ~49 BB) and Elon (BB ~121 BB), keeps multiway action down, and keeps 3-bet frequencies manageable given my deep stack (22,713). Postflop plan: c-bet most favorable flops to continue representing strength; proceed cautiously on heavily coordinated/paired boards and re-evaluate vs 3-bets (call or 4-bet depending on sizing and reads, call shoves from SB given my equity).

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 2h. The Button (Minni) has opened with a raise to 300. Qc 2h is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. I am out of position against a player with a massive chip lead. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand, and calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a low-equity holding. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here to preserve my stack.

Facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB) from Minni, who is aggressive based on hand history (frequent raises and bets). My hand Jc3d is offsuit, disconnected, and weak with poor post-flop playability out of position. Pot odds to call 200 into 450 offer about 2.25:1, requiring ~31% equity, but J3o realizes poorly against a wide button range and is often dominated. Defending wide in BB vs BTN is standard, but this is clear fold to preserve stack for better spots. No reason to 3-bet bluff or call with trash.

I am on the button with A10 of clubs, a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is around 50 big blinds (4974 chips). This is an ideal spot to open for a raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the blinds. Given the tournament context and my position, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

Preflop in the small blind with 2s5d offsuit, an extremely weak hand with no high card value, suitedness, or straight potential. Facing a button raise from Flash to 300 into a 450 pot, requiring 250 to call. This hand plays terribly postflop with poor equity against likely calling or 3-betting ranges from Minni in the BB. No position advantage after the flop, and stack preservation is key in a tournament setting to maximize stack over 150 hands. Standard fold to avoid spew.

I'm in the big blind with Q♠8♠ facing a button open to 300. I only need to put in 200 more (300 total) into a 450 pot, so the call requires ~30.8% equity. Q8s plays very well heads-up — has flush/straight potential and blocks some Qx combos — and against Flash's frequent 300 open from the button I expect a fairly wide range, so my equity is in the mid-30s. 3-betting out of position with Q8s is suboptimal (it bloats the pot OOP with a marginal hand); folding is too weak. I'll call to see a flop and plan to continue aggressively on favorable spade/Q flops or play straightforwardly (check/call or fold) on dry, face-up boards.

Check. I have Qs8s on Kd Kh 7s — no pair and only a very small backdoor spade/straight possibility, so my equity is minimal. I called preflop because Q8s is playable heads-up, but this is a dry, face‑up two‑king flop that heavily favors the opener's range (lots of Kx/overpairs/strong hands). Out of position I should not lead or bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Checking keeps the pot small and lets Flash show aggression (or give me a free turn). Plan: if Flash makes a small c-bet (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot, ~200–225) I can call to float and try to take the pot on a later street or continue if I improve; if Flash fires a larger c-bet (> ~1/2 pot, ~325) I will fold — I have too little equity to continue. If checked to me on the turn I'll reassess and only invest further if I pair the queen/8 or pick up a meaningful spade.

On a very dry, paired board of Kd-Kh-7s, my Ace-high (Ac Tc) currently has decent showdown value but also serves as a great candidate for a continuation bet. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor from the button, my range contains many Kings. Minni's check-call range from the Big Blind often misses this board. A small bet of roughly one-third of the pot is effective here to either take the pot down immediately or define where I stand against her range. If she calls, I still have the lead and potential to improve or check down later streets.

Call 250. Flash’s flop wager is tiny (~250 into 900, <1/3 pot) so this is a cheap spot to float out of position with Q♠8♠. I have almost no immediate showdown equity vs a K‑heavy value range, but the price to see a turn is good and Q8s has backdoor/spade playability and blocks some Qx draws. 3‑betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and is too thin; folding is unnecessarily tight given the bet size and opponent’s wide button opens. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to a large turn bet (>~1/2 pot), but if the turn is a spade, a queen, a blank that induces a check, or otherwise favourable, consider betting/raising a delayed bluff or continuing if I improve.

Check. I have Q♠8♠ on K♦ K♥ 7♠ J♣ — I missed: no pair and after the turn a flush is no longer possible (only one spade on board with one card to come), so my hand has very little equity versus Flash’s preflop/MC-bet range (likely lots of Kx/overpairs). Flash’s flop c-bet was tiny and checking the turn shows either pot control with a real hand or outright weakness; bluffing out of position here is too risky and can be called/raised by many better hands. The pot is still small relative to my stack, so the prudent line is to check and see the river for a pair (Q/8) or a truly favorable card before committing chips. Plan: check the river if he checks; if he bets a small river stab I can call some sizing to try to take it down or bluff-catch; fold to a large river bet unless I improve.

On the turn Jack of Clubs, the board is now Kd-Kh-7s-Jc. I still have Ace-high with a gutshot to the nut straight (Queen). Minni's check-call on the flop could indicate a King, a Seven, a pocket pair, or a float with Ace-high. By checking behind on the turn, I control the pot size and ensure I see a free river card to potentially hit my straight or win with Ace-high at showdown. Betting again might not fold out a Seven or a Jack, and it certainly won't fold a King, so checking is the most prudent play to realize equity.

Board K♦ K♥ 7♠ J♣ 2♦. I hold Q♠8♠ — queen‑high with no flush/straight and no pair. Flash c‑bet a tiny 250 on the flop (into a big pot) and then checked the turn, which reads as pot‑control with a Kx or a medium pair, or a missed small bluff. The river didn’t improve me. Betting here would be a thin, high‑variance bluff: it might fold out worse high‑card hands but will get called by any Kx, Jx, pocket pairs, or ace/high hands that beat me, and I have poor fold equity vs the likely checked‑back range. Pot is 1,150 and villain still has ~4,424 behind — no need to manufacture a risky bluff when checking gets me to a free showdown. I’ll check back and conserve chips.

The board is Kd-Kh-7s-Jc-2d. I hold Ac-Tc, which gives me one pair of Kings with an Ace-Jack-Seven kicker. Minni defended her big blind pre-flop and check-called my continuation bet on the flop. This line strongly suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a 7 or a pocket pair, both of which now form two pair (Kings and Sevens or Kings and her pocket pair) and beat my hand. By checking the turn, I already decided to control the pot size. Minni has checked the river as well. While I could attempt a bluff, it is unlikely she would fold a 7 or any pocket pair on such a dry board after having called the flop. Checking back allows me to reach showdown and potentially win against her own missed floats or chop against other Ace-high hands like AQ.

3-handed on the button with SB folded, leaving only BB to act against. Dead money in pot from SB blind and BB (150 total). Q5o is a playable hand from this position due to strong post-flop position and potential to realize equity. Raising small to steal the dead money and take initiative, while denying BB equity. BB has 55BB stack, manageable if they shove. Standard sizing around 3x effective BB. If called, proceed cautiously with queen-high but leverage position.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I have A♣6♥ — an ace with a weak kicker. Out of position postflop vs the opener (and possibly the BB), this hand is worth seeing a flop but not worth a 3-bet-sized isolation or bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. A small min-raise to 500 would be a weak-looking re-raise and invite a larger 4-bet or a sticky defense from the BB; with deep stacks I prefer to realize equity and look for an ace on the flop. I’ll call the additional 250 (total committed 300) to see the flop and proceed based on the texture — bluff-catch or c-bet when appropriate, fold to strong aggression if I miss and face heavy action.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. The Button (Elon) has raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Minni) has called. I only need to put in 200 more to call into a pot of 700. These are excellent pot odds (22.2%) to see a flop with an Ace. Given the stack sizes and the multi-way nature of the pot, calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity without over-committing to a mediocre hand against two opponents.

I have A♣6♥ on a dry 2♠-4♣-T♥ flop in a 3-way pot (Elon opened, Flash called). I completely missed — only an overcard (A) and a very weak backdoor club/straight potential. In multiway pots continuation bets are much less profitable because many calling ranges (T, small pairs, connectors) will continue and I have little fold equity. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat a pot with a marginal ace-high. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: if checked to the turn I can re-evaluate and look to barrel when I improve (ace or club) or if the turn gives a favorable texture; if Elon fires a small c-bet (~1/3 pot ≈300) I’ll consider calling to see the turn, but I’ll fold to large raises. Preserving stack and avoiding marginal multiway bluffs is the priority here.

In this three-way pot on a dry 2s 4c Th board, I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel. Since the Small Blind checked and the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) is still to act behind me, checking is the most standard and prudent play. It allows me to see how the Button reacts and potentially see a free turn card if everyone checks, or evaluate based on the size of a bet if Elon continues.

Preflop I raised with Q5o from the button for initiative and position. Flop is 2s4cTh, a dry rainbow board with low connected cards and one broadway-ish ten. Both opponents checked, showing weakness. As the preflop raiser, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately, as ranges are wide from calling stations like Minni (limp-called) and Flash (BB defend). Q high has decent showdown value and blocks some overcards. Half-pot sizing denies equity to any weak draws (like 3x for gutshot), builds pot if called by worse, and pot-commits Flash's shorter stack if he shoves while leaving fold equity against Minni's deep stack. Position post-flop allows me to continue profitably.

I checked the flop to control the pot and realize equity. Elon fires 450 (~1/2 pot) into a three-way pot; I have A♣6♥ with no pair and only a weak backdoor club. Calling 450 requires ~25% equity (450/(1350+450)), but my realistic equity to make a winning hand by the river is below that (≈6 outs to pair by river ≈24% and many of those will still lose to better pairs/Ax). I don’t want to bloat a multiway pot out of position with a marginal hand, so I fold and preserve my deep stack for a better spot.

In this hand, I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3 for the wheel) on a 2s-4c-Th board. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has placed a continuation bet of 450 into a 900 pot (1/2 pot). To call, I need to contribute 450 to a total pot of 1800, giving me 4-to-1 odds (25%). My direct equity with 4 outs for the straight is approximately 8% for the next card. While I do have an overcard (the Ace), calling out of position with just a gutshot and a weak Ace is generally unprofitable, as I will likely face another bet on the turn and my Ace may not even be the best hand if it hits. Given the lack of a backdoor flush draw and the fact that I am out of position, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

On the button with A♣7♦ facing only the blinds (pot 150, to call 100). This is a clear open-raise spot: A7o is strong enough to steal and to play well in position. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — a standard size at this table that gives good fold equity against the small blind, discourages loose limp-calls, and keeps the pot manageable if the big blind calls. If I get a large 3-bet I’ll fold A7o; if called I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet appropriate flops or control the pot and realize my equity.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket fours (4s 4d). Minni has opened from the button for 300. Pocket pairs have significant value in three-handed play, and while 44 is a small pair, it is strong enough to continue. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially hit a set (set-mining) while keeping the pot size controlled against the chip leader. I will evaluate the post-flop action based on the board texture and the behavior of both Minni and Elon in the Big Blind.

Pre-flop in the big blind with 8c4c, facing a button raise to 300 by Minni (aggressive raiser from history) and a small blind call by Flash (stack 4974, somewhat loose defender). Pot is 700, calling 200 for ~3.5:1 odds. 84 suited has speculative value with flush and straight potential multi-way, but it's a weak hand out of position against two opponents. Post-flop playability OOP is poor with reverse implied odds if I hit. Minni's button range is wide but still favors folding trash here to avoid difficult spots. Effective stacks allow for discipline; preserve stack for better spots with 36 hands left. Folding maximizes EV long-term.

On a dry flop of 9c-7s-2d, my pocket fours are an underpair. While I am ahead of many of Minni's broadway cards and high-card hands, I am out of position against the chip leader. Checking is the standard play here to see how she reacts to this board. I will evaluate her bet size if she chooses to continuation-bet, but leading out myself doesn't accomplish much as I'm likely to only get called by hands that beat me or have significant equity against me.

I opened from the button and got a single call from the small blind. On the 9♣ 7♠ 2♦ flop I have A♣7♦ — second/medium pair with the best possible kicker. The board is very dry (no real draws) and the SB’s check indicates a wide, often weak calling range. This is an excellent spot to c‑bet for value and to deny equity to the rare floating hands. I’ll make a standard ~1/2 pot continuation bet (350 into 700) to extract from worse pairs, Ax hands and loose calls while keeping the pot manageable. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises large I’ll give his line respect (likely 9x or a set) and proceed cautiously rather than bloating the pot unnecessarily.

On a very dry board of 9-7-2 rainbow, my pocket fours are an underpair, but they still hold significant value as a bluff-catcher against Minni's wide button-opening range. Minni, as the chip leader, is expected to continuation-bet this flop with a high frequency, including many overcards like AK, AQ, or KJ that I currently beat. Folding to a single half-pot bet on such a disconnected board would be too exploitable. I will call the 350 to see a turn card and evaluate. If she checks back the turn, my hand might be good at showdown; if she continues to show strength on cards that hit her range or overcards, I can reassess.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds is another overcard to my pocket fours, making the board 9-7-2-J. This card hits a significant portion of Minni's button-opening range (hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, and JT). As the out-of-position player, I must continue to play cautiously. Checking is the most appropriate action here to see how Minni responds to the Jack. If she checks back, I may have a chance to see a cheap showdown or potentially value-bet/bluff-catch on the river. If she bets again, I will have to carefully consider the price and her likely range, but with three overcards now on the board, 44 has significantly diminished in value as a bluff-catcher.

Pot is 1,400 after my 350 flop c-bet was called. I hold A♣7♦ — second pair with the best kicker. The turn J♦ is a relatively bad card: it improves a large portion of Flash’s flop-calling range (Jx, some 9x combos) and also creates backdoor diamond possibilities. Flash’s line (call on flop, check on turn) looks like either a cautious top-pair hand or a medium-strength holding controlling the pot. Betting now risks getting raised or committed by hands that already beat me, while extracting little extra value from hands I beat. With position and a vulnerable but showdown-capable hand, pot control is the best play: check and re-evaluate on the river, calling reasonable bets and folding to strong aggression that polarizes to sets/Jx.

On the river, the board reads 9c-7s-2d-Jd-Td. My pocket fours are now an underpair to four of the five cards on the board. Minni checked the turn, which often indicates either a moderate-strength hand (like a 7 or a weak 9) or a complete miss. However, the river Ten of Diamonds completes several potential straights (any 8) and a backdoor flush possibility. There is no value in betting here, as I will not be called by worse hands and I cannot reliably expect to fold out hands that beat me. Checking is the only viable option. If Minni checks behind, I will likely lose to her showdown value, and if she bets, I will fold as 44 is an extremely weak bluff-catcher on this texture.

I have A♣7♦ — second pair (sevens) with the best kicker on a 9♣‑7♠‑2♦‑J♦‑T♦ board. Pot is 1,400. Flash called my flop c‑bet and then checked both turn and river, which reads like a wide, mostly medium/weak calling range (9x, 7x, small pairs, floats or missed draws). The river T♦ makes some hands (Jx, Tx, 9x) and two‑card diamond combos dangerous, but Flash’s passive line makes a strong, value‑heavy holding or a big two‑card diamond flush less likely. Betting here would be thin value (and can get check‑raised or commit me vs hands that already beat me); checking controls the pot and gets to showdown with a vulnerable but often‑best hand. I’ll check and call a small/medium bet, fold to a large polarized shove.

Holding King-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds. If called, I will be playing the rest of the hand in position.

K5o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x BB). Flash raises frequently from the button with a wide range, but K5o has poor post-flop playability, low equity against a calling range from BB or Flash's continuing range, and I'll be out of position if called. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (450:250), but implied odds are weak due to the hand's capped potential. Folding preserves my stack (12,563) for better spots with 35 hands left; no need to defend weakly here to maximize long-term EV.

Flash opened to 300 on the button and Elon folded, so it’s heads-up. I’m in the big blind with 9♦6♦ — a suited, speculative hand that plays well heads-up and has good post‑flop equity. The pot is 450 and I only need to put in 200 more (call to 300), giving me ~31% pot odds to see a flop. 3-betting with 9♦6♦ out of position is too thin: I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal holding or face a shove/4-bet from a shorter-stacked, aggressive button. I’ll call to keep the pot controllable and play the flop — continue on pairs/strong draws, fold or proceed cautiously if I miss.

I have middle pair (6s) with a decent kicker heads‑up on a 7♥-5♣-6♠ board. Flash opened wide from the button, so their check here can be weakness or a delayed C‑bet; many hands in their range are worse (missed overcards, weaker pairs, or draws). I want to both extract value from worse hands and deny free cards that give straights (4 or 8), while keeping the pot manageable given Flash’s ~4.3k stack and my much larger stack. A small bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse, folds pure air, and keeps pressure on draws. Plan: bet 250 now; if called proceed cautiously on the turn (check or pot‑control if a dangerous card comes), fold to a large check‑raise since I only have a single middle pair.

On a board of 7h-5c-6s, the big blind (Minni) leads out with a 'donk bet' of 250 into a pot of 650. This board texture is highly coordinated and significantly favors the range of the big blind, who can easily have hands like 56, 67, 48, or even sets and straights. My hand, King-Ten offsuit, is currently just King-high with two overcards and no direct straight or flush draw. Given that Minni is the chip leader and the board is extremely dangerous for my specific range, I do not have enough equity or a profitable bluffing opportunity to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Preflop on the button with Qs6d, a weak queen-high offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity multiway. Facing an unraised pot (SB limp/check, BB posted), but Q6o is not strong enough to call even from the button. Pot odds are 150:100 (1.5:1, ~40% needed), but this hand rarely makes strong hands and is easily dominated by better queens or pairs. Folding preserves stack for better spots, especially with deep stacks and history showing aggressive opponents. Goal is long-term maximization over 150 hands.

Heads-up vs Flash (BB, ~42bb). I have 5♦4♦ — a speculative, suited connector with good flop playability and strong implied odds. Calling 50 more (bringing my total committed to 100) gets excellent pot odds (150:50 ≈ 3:1) to see a flop cheaply. A min-raise to 200 would put more chips in play OOP against a fairly deep but defend-happy BB and likely won't pick up many folds, so I prefer to complete and play the flop cautiously: continue with strong draws/two pair+ or check/fold to heavy aggression when missed.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 3c, which is a very weak hand. The Small Blind (Minni) has limped in, and I have the option to check to see a free flop. Given the weakness of my hand, there is no merit in raising as a bluff here, but since I can see the flop for free, checking is the standard and correct play.

I hold 5♦4♦ on A♥ 3♦ Q♣ — no pair, only a backdoor diamond draw (need two diamonds to hit a flush) and very little showdown equity right now. Flash (BB) checked preflop and checked the flop, so their range is wide but also contains many A‑ or Q‑type hands that dominate me. Out of position with a marginal hand, I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot OOP when my equity is low. Betting here would invite calls from worse Qx and draws and raise/pressure from Ax that I can’t realistically continue against. I’ll check and plan to fold to meaningful aggression, only calling small probes where the price justifies seeing a turn or continuing if I pick up a diamond or a pair on the turn.

On a flop of Ah 3d Qc, I have bottom pair with my Trey of Clubs. The pot is small (200 chips) and my opponent in the Small Blind checked. While I have a pair, the board is very high with an Ace and a Queen, making it likely my opponent could have connected or is waiting to see a turn. Since I have some showdown value but a very vulnerable hand, checking back to keep the pot small and see a turn for free is the most prudent play.

I have 5♦4♦ on A♥ 3♦ Q♣ J♥ — no pair, only a runner‑runner diamond possibility. Flash checked preflop and the flop and has shown a relatively passive, wide checking range; many of his holdings that beat me (A/Q/Jx) are already in that range. Out of position with essentially zero showdown equity, I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a pure bluff that will often be called or put me in a tough spot. The pot is only 200 and my stack is deep, so the correct play is to check and fold to meaningful aggression. I may call very small probes if the price is right or continue only if I hit a real card (a diamond or a pair) on the river.

On the turn [Ah 3d Qc Jh], I have bottom pair with a pair of Treys. The board is very coordinated with several straight and flush possibilities developing. Minni (the Small Blind) has checked for the second street in a row after limping pre-flop. While my hand has some showdown value in a small pot, it is extremely weak on this board. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and keep the pot at 200 chips, which is consistent with the value of my marginal hand.

I have 5♦4♦ on A♥ 3♦ Q♣ J♥ 9♠ — no pair, just 5‑high. Flash has checked every street, which is consistent with a wide but largely made/marginal showdown range (Ax/Qx/Jx, small pairs) that dominates me. The pot is tiny (200) and Flash’s remaining stack is large enough that a small bluff will often be called; a large bluff to force a fold is unnecessary risk when I’m deep and can wait for better spots. Pot control is correct — I’ll check and concede unless he leads into me with a bet large enough to consider a thin bluff/call (in which case I’ll fold).

I have bottom pair (a pair of Threes) on a board that is very coordinated with high cards (Ace, Queen, Jack, Nine). Minni has checked on every street after limping pre-flop, which suggests a weak range. However, with bottom pair, I have some showdown value but very little value in betting. If I bet, it's unlikely a better hand will fold or a worse hand will call. Checking back allows me to reach showdown for free in a small pot and potentially win against a missed draw or a lower pair.

Three-way (button vs SB+BB) so I want to be aggressive and take the initiative. Q♣5♦ is marginal but playable on the button — raising will pick up the blinds often and lets me play post‑flop with position. I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size to apply pressure (300 total). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet; if called, continuation-bet on favorable textures and play cautiously with a single caller given my marginal hand and the short SB stack (4374) which increases my chances to steal or isolate the BB.

I am currently the short stack with 4,374 chips. I am in the small blind with 4h 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the button. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand out of position, especially as the short stack. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Preflop as BB facing a button min-raise to 300 (3x) after SB folds. My hand is 9d8s, offsuit connectors with straight potential and playability. Pot is 450, calling 200 more (total 300 committed). Getting about 2.25:1 odds, requiring ~31% equity. Minni's button range is wide given her frequent raising tendencies from history (many limps/calls but aggressive opens). 98o has ~35-40% equity vs a loose button range (top 25-35%). OOP but deep stacks (mine 12k, hers 22k), good implied odds if I flop draws or pairs. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks too much with marginal hand vs her stack; calling to realize equity and control pot size.

Flop is 5c-7s-7c, a paired board favoring villain's button range which includes many overpairs, sets, and draws. My 9d8s completely misses: no pair, no strong draw (minor backdoor spade draw with 8s and 7s, but irrelevant). Straight draws are weak or absent; just high card equity that's crushed. OOP first to act into deep-stacked aggressive Minni (history shows frequent continuation bets). Pot 650, stacks deep, but betting risks raising villain's range or getting check-raised while check-fold realizes no equity loss and preserves stack. Calling any bet would be spew without odds or fold equity. Check to induce bluffs or pot control, folding to bet maximizes EV long-term.

Heads-up on a paired flop (5c 7s 7c) with Qc5d — I have second pair (5s) and a backdoor club but my hand is vulnerable to any 7x or bigger pocket pair. Elon defended a wide preflop range by calling, so many of his hands are worse or unpaired overcards that will fold to pressure. I want to take the initiative and charge hands that have equity (overcards, gutshots) while denying free turns. A half-pot c-bet gives value and fold equity without bloating the pot. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot to fold out pure air and get called by worse; if Elon raises big I’ll presume a very strong holding (trips/quads/overpair) and fold; if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed cautiously.

9d8s completely whiffs the 5c-7s-7c flop: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot absent as 9-8 doesn't connect meaningfully to board). Pure air with zero equity against Minni's button c-bet range, which is strong on paired board (overpairs, pocket 7s, 75s, 57s, club draws). OOP after checking, facing ~1/3 pot bet (325 into ~650 pre-bet pot). History shows Minni aggressive with cbets post button opens. Calling commits more without odds/implied (no hit potential), raising is bluff suicide vs deep stacks (mine 12k, hers 22k). Check-fold preserves stack for better spots; long-term +EV.

On the button with 6s 5c and 150 in the pot, the price to call is 100 into a 150 pot. 65o is a speculative hand that plays poorly in multi-way pots but can occasionally find a straight. However, given my stack size of 4374 (approx. 44 big blinds), I should be looking for better spots. Calling on the button might be tempting, but it puts me in a post-flop situation where I'm likely to be folding to aggression unless I hit very hard. I will fold to preserve my stack for stronger holdings.

Preflop in SB vs BB (Flash folded from BTN). Hand A♣2♠ is playable with ace blocker, decent high card value, and steal equity heads-up. Minni deep (23k) but defends wide based on history of calling raises and limps. Raising steals blinds often or builds pot OOP with position postflop if called. Limp too passive; fold wastes SB. Standard size ~3BB (300 total) balances range, applies pressure without overcommitting.

I’m in the big blind with Q♠2♣ facing a 300 open from the small blind. This hand is marginal off-suit, but heads-up Qx has reasonable playability and the pot (400) is offering 2:1 immediate pot odds (I must call 200 more). I don’t want to 3-bet as a bluff — Q2o has poor blockers and I’ll be dominated often; a 3-bet would be a thin play out of position with deep stacks. Plan: call to 300 (i.e. put 200 more) to see a flop, then fold to heavy aggression on dry boards and continue only if I pair or pick up sufficient equity/backdoors. Preserve stack and play postflop selectively.

Flop board Qc 4h Kc is draw-heavy with two clubs to the king and queen, hitting villain's BB calling range (Kx, Qx, suited connectors, clubs). I hold Ac2s, giving me the nut flush draw plus ace-high equity and blocking villain's strong Ax clubs or AK. OOP as SB raiser, standard semi-bluff c-bet exploits fold equity against Minni's history of wide defense but folding to postflop aggression when behind (e.g., Hands 1,3,21,60). Bet sizing ~2/3 pot (400) balances value (possible AK/AQ blocks) with bluffs/draws, builds pot for flush, denies equity to floats/OESD. Checking risks giving free card to villain's draws/pairs; betting maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

I have Q♠2♣ on Q♣ 4♥ K♣ — top pair but a very weak 2-kicker. Pot is 1,000 and Elon put in 400, so calling 400 gives ~3.5:1 immediate odds. Elon is an aggressive preflop raiser, so his flop bet can be a continuation with missed broadways or a value bet with Kx/Qx/clubs. Raising here would be thin and frequently put me in tough spots against Kx or Qx with a better kicker, so I prefer pot control. I’ll call the 400 to see a turn, then fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve or get a clear read that he’s bluffing.

Turn 6c brings a third club, completing the flush draw and making this a highly coordinated board (Kc Qc 6c 4h). Holding Ac2s gives me the nut flush draw (9 outs, as Ac is the nut blocker), ace-high equity, and blocks villain's strong Ax clubs or AK. Minni called my flop c-bet of 400 into 800 on a KQcc flop, which hits her BB calling range (Kx, Qx, suited connectors, clubs) but she could have floats, pairs, or weaker draws. OOP with the nuts draw on a scary street, betting maximizes EV: generates fold equity against her non-nut improved hands/weak pairs (she folds to aggression postflop often, e.g., Hands 16,21,45,78), protects against her completing draws (OESD, etc.), builds the pot for river shove if I hit, and denies equity to her calling range. Checking risks a free card to her draws or induces her bluff/value bets I'm behind on, but pot control is secondary to semi-bluff value here. Sizing ~2/3 pot (900 total) balances value from blocked AK/AQ/club pairs with bluffs/draws, applies pressure deep-stacked while leaving room for river play. Pot odds justify: ~30% equity needed vs her range, I have >40% + fold equity.

Elon has been the aggressor this hand (preflop raise, c-bet on the flop and a sizable bet on the turn). I hold Q♠2♣ — top pair but a very weak 2-kicker and only one club. The turn (Qc 4h Kc 6c) brings a third club on board; I have 9 club outs to make a flush and 2 queen outs to make trips = 11 outs total. Probability to hit one of those on the river is ~11/46 ≈ 23.9%. Calling 900 into a 2300 pot requires about 28.1% equity to break even (900/(2300+900)), so my raw improvement equity is below the pot odds. More importantly, many of those outs are non-clean (if Elon already has two clubs, he has a made flush; if he has Kx or a better Q kicker I'm dominated), and raising OOP with Q2o is a poor play. I called the flop as planned to see a turn, but facing heavy turn aggression and the odds/reads above, folding is the prudent, +EV decision — preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot.

SB (Minni) has folded, leaving us heads-up against BB (Flash) with 4274 stack (~43BB). Pot is 150, blinds posted. 5s6c is a marginal hand but has straight potential and decent playability postflop from button position. Standard button vs BB strategy is to raise wide to steal blinds, as BB defends ranges are capped and fold equity is high. Raising to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure, builds pot in position if called, and is +EV over the long term in tournament play to maximize stack growth. Calling is poor OOP potential; folding misses value with position and dead money.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with Kc2h — a marginal hand that’s often dominated but has some playability (club blocker + chance to make top pair). The cost to see a flop is small (250 more, 300 total committed) relative to my deep stack, so folding would be too nitty. A 3-bet bluff with K2o out of position is suboptimal (vulnerable to calls/4-bets and I’d be OOP vs the button), so I’ll call and see a flop. Plan: proceed cautiously postflop — fold to heavy aggression unless I pair the King, make a club draw, or get good equity/pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with JTo, which is a solid hand to defend against a standard 3x open from Elon on the Button and a call from Minni in the Small Blind. The pot is offering 3.5:1 odds (200 to call into a 700 pot), which is more than enough to justify seeing a flop with two high cards that have decent connectivity and straight potential. Folding would be too tight given the price and the hand strength relative to a wide Button opening range. Raising (3-betting) is an option, but JTo plays reasonably well in multi-way pots and I'd prefer not to bloat the pot out of position against deep stacks.

Multi-way flop (3 players) and I’m out of position with Kc2h. The flop 7s-8c-9c is highly coordinated and favors the button/BB ranges (pairs, two-pair draws, straights, and club draws). My only positives are the Kc blocker and a very slim chance to improve (pair the King or runner-runner clubs — runner-runner flush is ~5% from here). Versus two opponents a c-bet OOP with this weak holding is low EV and will be called or raised by too many better hands. Plan: check and give up to meaningful aggression. If both check to the turn I’ll re-evaluate (only continue if I hit a King or get a very cheap card to bluff with); if faced with a standard bet or a raise I’ll fold (I might call a tiny probe of ~1/3 pot only in rare cases to see a turn, but otherwise fold).

On a board of 7s-8c-9c, I have flopped the nut straight with Td-Jh. This is a highly coordinated board with both straight and flush possibilities. In a three-way pot, it is standard to check to the pre-flop raiser (Elon) to see if he will continue with a C-bet. If Elon bets, I plan to check-raise to build the pot and charge any flush draws or lower straights. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely reveal the strength of my hand too early and might cause opponents to fold hands they would otherwise bet.

Flopped the nut straight with 5s-6c on 7s-8c-9c board (5-6-7-8-9). This is an incredibly strong hand, currently the nuts, with blockers to some flush draws (hold 6c on paired club board). Pot is 900 in a 3-way pot after both opponents checked, indicating potential weakness or traps, but with position and massive equity (straight can't lose to made hands except runner-runner flush or higher straight unlikely). Must bet for value against pairs (e.g., 88,99,77, overpairs), two-pair combos, weaker draws (OESD like 10J, flush draws like AcXc), and floats. Betting 2/3 pot (600) balances protection from flush draws/spade draws, extracts value from calling ranges, and denies equity to draws. Flash's short stack (4074) means he can call comfortably; Minni deeper but checked cold. Raising preflop from button with playable hand, now crushing range. Maximize stack by building pot with monster.

The flop (7s-8c-9c) is highly coordinated — lots of made hands, straight combinations and club draws. Elon bets 600 into a 1500 multiway pot; calling 600 to win a 2100 pot requires ~28.6% equity. My Kc2h has only small backdoor outs: runner-runner club flush ≈4.2% and ~12.5% to hit a king by the river; realistic equity versus a c-bet range that contains pairs, 8/9/7 combos, straights and club draws is well below the required ~29%. Raising OOP with K2o is a poor bluff here (vulnerable to calls/3-bets and multiway action), and folding preserves a big stack for better spots. I fold.

I have flopped the nut straight (J-T-9-8-7) on a highly coordinated board with two clubs. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed through with a two-thirds pot bet of 600. On such a wet board, slow-playing is risky as many turn cards (any club or any card that pairs the board or extends straight possibilities) could either kill the action or give my opponent a better hand. I need to raise here for value and protection, specifically targeting hands like overpairs, sets, two-pair, or strong flush draws. A raise to 1800 (3x his bet) is a standard value raise that builds the pot and puts Elon to a difficult decision with the non-nut parts of his range.

Flopped second-nut straight (5-6-7-8-9; nuts is T-J) with excellent blockers (5s blocks spade flush draws, 6c blocks club flush draws). Board extremely wet (club FD, spade FD, wrap/OESD for better straights like 10-Q/J-10). Flash's check-call preflop from BB then check-raise flop sizing to 1800 (committing ~80% of his 2274 stack) indicates polarized range: nuts (T-J), strong made (sets 77/88/99 ~unlikely check pre, two-pair 78/89/97s), combo draws (A-Kc/K-Qc for clubs+gut, KsQs spade+over). We crush 2P/sets (high equity), dominated draws, lose only to nuts (low density in his range: loose calling range pre, checked flop). Pot 3300, call 1200 (3.7:1, great), but calling risks turn FD/spade/club completion (many outs) or Flash checking weak turn (missing value). His short stack (474 behind ~10%) shoves turn anyway with value, but jams now folds draws (instant 3300 win), gets snapped by worse (~85% equity avg when called), denies 1 card equity. Min-raise to 3000 (I add 2400; he all-in 474 more into ~5700) perfect size: looks strong, no fold equity loss vs value (committed). Deep vs short in tourney: jam monsters OOP-equivalent spots to isolate/build stack, per GTO/tourney solver principles vs aggro shorties. Max EV long-term.

I have flopped the nut straight (J-T on 7-8-9). Elon's 3-bet on the flop is extremely aggressive, likely indicating a set (99, 88, 77), two-pair (98s), or the same straight. Given the board is heavily coordinated with a flush draw and straight possibilities, I want to get my remaining chips in now to maximize value and protect my hand against a club or board-pairing turn card. My remaining stack is 2274 chips, and with 1800 already committed this street, an all-in raise totals 4074.

Facing Flash's all-in check-raise to 4074 (his stack) after our flop bet of 600 and raise to 3000, heads-up on a 7s-8c-9c board with our 5s6c for the nut straight (5-6-7-8-9; only beaten by 10-J). Board is draw-heavy (two flush draws, gutshots), capping villain's calling range preflop from BB to broadways, suited connectors, Ax suited. His polarized all-in range: nuts (T-Js ~low density, needs specific suited connectors), sets (77/88/99 ~preflop caller but checked flop), combo draws (nut FD + gut like AcXc, KsQ*/J*s). We block FD outs (5s spade, 6c club), crush sets/TPTK/two-pair (~90% equity), ~70-80% vs nuts, ~20-30% vs pure FD. Pot 7974 to call 1074 offers 7.4:1 (13.5% equity needed); our equity crushes >30% even weighted conservatively. Folding misses massive +EV (stack to ~18k vs ~8.5k), calling builds stack vs shortstack aggression in late tourney (119/150). Standard GTO call vs BB check-jam range; maximize EV long-term.

Ks3c is a weak, unsuited king but on the button in a 3-handed pot it’s a profitable open-steal candidate. With deep stacks and table precedent for 3x opens, I want a size that preserves fold equity and discourages limp/calls — so I’ll open to 300 (3x BB) rather than the bare minimum. Plan: fold to any sizable 3-bet with this holding; if called use position to c-bet most dry/K‑high flops and play cautiously on wet or multiway boards to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s7s, a solid suited connector in a 3-handed game. The chip leader (Minni) has raised from the Button. Given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a pot of 450) and the playability of my hand, a call is appropriate to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option, 97s is a good hand to play a pot with post-flop even out of position.

Facing a button raise to 300 and SB call in the BB with 8s7h offsuit. This is a marginal speculative hand at best, with straight potential but offsuit and no flush draw possibility. Pot odds are 200 to call into 700 (3.5:1, needing ~22% equity), but multiway with two opponents, my equity drops to around 25% against a wide button range and calling SB range. Postflop, I'm sandwiched OOP between SB and button, making it hard to realize equity without a strong hand. Late in tournament (hand 120/150), preserve stack for premium spots rather than defend marginally. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop spots.

On this flop of Qs-8h-6c, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10) and a backdoor spade flush draw. Out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check and see how she proceeds. Lead betting into the button often looks weak or overly defensive. By checking, I can evaluate the bet size and decide if the pot odds justify continuing with my drawing equity.

I opened as button preflop to steal and Flashes called. The flop Qs-8h-6c is fairly dry and gives me little — K3o has no pair and only a backdoor club. In position I want to seize the initiative with a continuation bet to fold out unpaired hands and small pairs while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll bet about half the pot (350 into 700) to apply pressure; if Flash check-raises big I’ll treat it as strong and fold this marginal holding, but if called I’ll proceed cautiously and only continue with improvement or a very favorable turn card.

I am currently out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. The flop of Qs-8h-6c gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10 for a 6-7-8-9-10 straight) and a backdoor spade flush draw. Minni's bet of 350 into a pot of 700 offers me exactly 3:1 pot odds. While 9-high has little showdown value, the price is attractive enough to see a turn card. I have the potential to pick up more equity on various turn cards (any 5, 10, or spade) and can potentially outplay my opponent on later streets if I improve or if the board texture allows for a credible bluff. Given the stack depths and the pot odds, calling is a standard defensive play with this drawing equity.

The 8d pairs the board, which is an interesting card. I currently have 9-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10). Out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. Leading here would be a polarising move that represents an 8, but given I only have a draw, I prefer to check and see how Minni acts. If she checks back, I get a free card. If she bets, I will have to decide if the price is right to continue chasing my 4 outs.

I have K♠3♣ — no pair and no meaningful draw on Q♠‑8♥‑6♣ and the turn 8♦ just pairs the board. I c‑bet the dry flop and was called, which narrows Flash’s range to Qx, 8x, some 6x/underpairs or a float. With essentially zero equity and no backdoor draws, betting the turn would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity and a real risk of a check‑raise or being called by any Q/8/6. Prefer pot control: check and see what Flash does. Plan: fold to a clear value bet (don’t bloat the pot with K3), consider calling a tiny probe only if it’s negligible, and re‑evaluate the river for a targeted bluff or a fold if opponent shows strength.

On the river 9c, I have improved to a pair of nines. The board texture is Q-8-6-8-9. After calling a flop bet and seeing the turn go check-check, my pair of nines has significant showdown value. Minni's turn check suggests she likely doesn't have a trip eights or a very strong Queen, though she could be pot-controlling a medium-strength hand. Betting now would likely only get called by better hands (like a Queen) and fold out hands I already beat (like Ace-high or a six). By checking, I can reach showdown for free or evaluate a potential bluff if Minni decides to bet her missed draws.

I’m on K♠3♣ with no pair and no meaningful showdown equity on Q♠‑8♥‑6♣‑8♦‑9♣. I c‑bet the dry flop and was called, which narrows Flash’s range to hands with real pair value (Qx, 8x, 9x, 6x), small pairs or legitimate floats — many of which will happily check‑call a river probe. With the pot already 1,400 and stacks deep, the prudent play is pot control: check behind and avoid bloat. If Flash fires a tiny probe I’ll consider a light call depending on sizing and timing tells, but I will fold to any clear value bet.

I am on the button with 8h 6c, a weak hand that typically doesn't play well post-flop, especially in a three-handed game where ranges are wider but opponents have shown aggression. With a stack of nearly 100 big blinds, I have plenty of room to wait for better spots. Although it is only 50 more to call (the small blind has 50 in, the big blind 100), this hand is easily dominated and lacks high-card value or strong connectivity. Given the recent history of 3-betting and aggressive play from Minni and Elon, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary losses with marginal holdings.

Preflop in SB, folded to me, J9s suited connectors play well multiway or heads-up. Excellent implied odds if called, and fold equity vs BB. Minni defends wide from history but often checks flops. Min-raise to 200 applies pressure, steals blinds ~67% of time, or builds pot OOP with good hand. Calling 50 too passive postflop OOP; folding too tight. Stack 84BB allows aggression. Optimal balance for tournament.

Facing a small‑blind open to 200 with 5♠8♦ in the big blind. Pot is 300 and it costs me 100 more (I already have 100 in). 5‑8 offsuit is a weak, unplayable hand out of position, but the call is very cheap relative to my stack (21613) and gives ~4:1 pot odds to see a flop. Flop board 2c-Tc-5d is somewhat coordinated with two clubs for flush draw and possible straight draws like 34, 67, A4. My J9o (9s Jh) completely misses: no pair, no flush draw, marginal gutshot at best (needing an 8 for 5-6-7-8-9-T but no 6-7 on board, actually no straight draw; pure air with J-high). Check preserves stack, induces bluffs from her weak range, allows check-fold to bet or check-call thin if she checks. Tournament stage mid-late, no need to bluff into wide defender OOP on wet board. Goal: avoid spew, wait for better spots.

I have middle pair (5♠) with a weak 8 kicker on a dry 2♣-T♣-5♦ flop. Elon was the preflop raiser and checked the flop — that checkmore often shows weakness than strength, but it can also be a slow-play (T/2 or a slow-played pocket pair). Betting would protect against backdoor club/straight possibilities and charge overcards, but it also bloats the pot with a vulnerable one-pair hand and can commit me to a big pot if Elon raises.
