With deep stacks and a marginal made hand, I prefer pot control and to induce bluffs or get value from worse hands on later streets. Plan: check and call a reasonable bet; fold or proceed very cautiously to a large raise. For now: check.

Turn board 2c-Tc-5d-3h is draw-heavy with club flush draw, possible gutshots (e.g., 4x for wheel-ish, 67 for higher), and pairs/draws in her wide BB calling range (Ax suited, pairs, suited connectors from history). My J9o has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (spade and heart), no straight draw (J too high for gutshot). Preflop raiser image might get folds pre but now post two checks, she's weak or trapping. OOP with 83BB effective, pot 400 (tiny), betting bluffs into her floats/pairs/draws risks commitment OOP with air; fold equity low after check-check. Check induces her bluffs/strength shows value, allows cheap river or check-fold to bet. Tournament mid-late (hand 121/150), preserve stack for premium spots vs her deep stack. Optimal: check to realize equity realization neutrality and avoid spew.

I check. I have middle pair (5♠) with a weak 8 kicker on a very dry 2♣-T♣-5♦-3♥ board. Elon opened preflop then checked the flop and turn, which shows more weakness/pot control than strength, but my hand is still vulnerable to any Ten or overpairs and has poor showdown value against his raising range. Betting here just bloats the pot and will often fold out worse hands while getting called by better hands; with deep stacks I prefer pot control. Plan: check and call a reasonable river bet to extract value from worse hands/bluffs, but fold to a large raise. If Elon checks back, re-evaluate on the river based on the runout.

River board 2c-Tc-5d-3h-As completes no obvious flush (clubs missed, ace spade doesn't help) and no straight (possible wheel needs 4, broadway needs JQK). My J9o (Jh 9s) is pure J-high: no pair, no draw. Preflop minraise OOP vs Minni's wide BB defense (history shows she calls wide with Ax, pairs 55+, suited connectors). Check-check flop/turn reps weakness but preserves tiny pot (400). Deep effective stacks (83BB). Betting air low fold equity vs her capped range (missed draws, weak Ax/pairs likely check back); risks value from Ax/Tx. Checking maximizes EV: induces bluffs from missed draws (club flush, gutshots like 4x/A4), realizes showdown equity vs worse (87o, Kx worse), avoids spew OOP late tournament (121/150). Check behind if she checks or cheap fold/call.

The river Ace made the board slightly scarier, but my hand is just middle pair (5♠) with a weak kicker. Elon raised preflop then checked flop/turn/river — that line more often indicates weakness or pot control than a strong value hand. The pot is small (400) and stacks are deep, so betting here would bloat the pot with a vulnerable holding and often fold worse hands while getting called by better ones. I prefer pot control and taking this to showdown. Action: check. If Elon fires a small/medium sized bet I’ll call to catch bluffs/worse; I’ll fold to a large raise or shove.

Pocket deuces is a strong speculative hand from the button in an unraised pot (pot 150, to call 100). Excellent position, deep effective stacks (mine 83BB, opponents deeper), perfect for set-mining with implied odds if called. Raising takes the initiative, has good fold equity against loose blinds who often check/fold weak holdings, and builds a pot for when we hit. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative; folding too tight 3-handed. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300 total, balancing protection and fold equity.

I’m in the small blind with 7h6c facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250) and the big blind still to act. 7-6 offsuit is a very marginal hand from the SB: it plays poorly out of position and loses a lot of equity in multiway pots if the BB calls. A min-raise here is not a constructive isolation (I’d be 3-betting a weak hand and can easily get called or 4-bet), and calling 250 to play OOP against the opener (and potentially the BB) leads to difficult postflop decisions with limited equity. With healthy stack depth I can wait for better, more playable spots or a suited connector to defend. Fold and preserve chips for higher-EDGE situations.

I have been dealt a strong pocket pair, 8s8d, in the big blind. Elon has opened for 300 from the button, and Minni has folded the small blind. Pocket eights are a premium hand in 3-handed play. Since I am out of position against a loose-aggressive button open, I want to define the hand strength and take control of the pot. Raising (3-betting) is the standard play here to build the pot and potentially end the hand pre-flop or go to the flop with the initiative. I will raise to 1000, which is a standard 3x+ size from the big blind.

Pocket 22 is a strong set-mining hand in position against a BB 3-bet after my button open. Facing a 3-bet to 1000 (pot 1350, call 700), pot odds are excellent (~3:1). Heads-up postflop with position (BB acts first), deep effective stacks (~80BB), and massive implied odds if we flop a set (12% chance). Flash's BB 3-bet range is likely polarized: premium value (AA/KK/QQ/AKs) or bluffs (suited connectors, Ax suited). 22 has ~18% equity vs tight value, more vs polarized; we crush bluffs and realize equity well IP. Folding is too tight 3-handed deep; 4-betting risks stacking off OOP to value or folding equity not great with blocking premium pairs low. Calling keeps pot small, plays postflop flexibly: check/call if miss reasonable boards, bet if hit, or check-raise strong draws. Balances my opening range. Goal: maximize stack via implied odds over tournament.

On this monotone 9-high board (4d 3d 9d), I have an overpair to the two low cards and the 8 of diamonds, which gives me a flush draw. As the pre-flop 3-better, I have the range advantage, although monotone boards are tricky. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 1/3 of the pot (675). This size allows me to get value from pocket pairs lower than eights, or hands with a single high diamond, while also protecting my hand against random overcards that don't have a diamond. If Elon raises, I can reconsider based on the sizing, but for now, I want to maintain the initiative.

Flop is extremely wet: three diamonds, low coordinated cards (3d4d9d), perfect for Flash's BB 3-bet bluff range (suited connectors, Adx, flush draws) while bricking many overpairs (QQ+/AKo). My 2s2d is disguised strength: bottom pair + nut low-end flush draw (9 clean outs, ~35% equity standalone) + position IP. Combined equity crushes range (~48% vs polarized 3-bet: e.g., 55% vs bluffs, 40% vs QQ+). His 1/3-pot c-bet weak, inviting raise. Semi-bluff raise: high fold equity vs overpairs/medium pairs fearing flush/set; value from worse draws/pairs; denies equity/turn barrels; low SPR commitment (~3.5 post-raise). Call realizes equity well but passive, invites free turns/misses value. Fold absurd. Size 3x bet (standard vs small bet IP) to 2025 total balances value/bluff from calling range, ~half-pot post-call SPR ideal for turn shove if improves/misses.

I have an overpair (8s8d) to the 9-high board and the 8 of diamonds, which gives me a flush draw. In a 3-handed 3-bet pot, the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) is low, and my hand has significant equity against Elon's entire range, including sets and flushes. While Elon's flop raise is strong, I cannot fold such a high-equity hand on this texture. By raising all-in (covering Elon's effective stack), I maximize my equity and force Elon to commit with his draws or made hands. This also protects my hand from difficult turn decisions if an overcard or a fourth diamond rolls off. Given Elon's stack size of 5264, I will raise to a total that puts him all-in.

Facing a large re-raise from Flash on a monotone diamond flop 4d-3d-9d with 2s2d (bottom pair + flush draw, 9 flush outs ~18% one street, high equity overall ~40-50% vs his polarized BB 3-bet range: crushes bluffs/draws, live vs overpairs/sets). Pot odds excellent: 9339/3239 ~2.9:1 (need 26%, have much more). Position IP, low SPR post-call (~0.4:1 turn, shove any reasonable card). His huge 4-bet sizing after small c-bet + my raise reps nuts (AdXd flush, 99/44/33 sets) or polarized bluff jam (overpair fearing flush/set, missed draw). Can't have nuts always (limited 2-diamond combos in range), blocks Ad2d lowly. Folding -EV (strong hand, good odds); no raise (all-in 5264 < min 7289). Call realizes equity, balances aggression from prior raise, exploits history of Flash's aggression (e.g., Hand 119 big pots with draws). Maximizes EV long-term in 3-handed tourney.

The pot is 12,578 and Elon has only 2,025 remaining, creating an extremely low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of approximately 0.16. I have a pair of eights and the eight-high flush draw with the 8d. While the King of Hearts is an overcard to my pair, I am completely pot-committed in this 3-bet pot given the amount already invested. Betting the remaining 2,025 to put Elon all-in is the only logical play to realize my equity and force Elon to commit his remaining chips with any part of his range.

Facing an all-in call of 2025 (my remaining stack) into a 14603 pot on the turn with 22s2d (bottom pair + nut diamond flush draw: 9 flush outs, 3 set outs, ~30-35% equity vs Flash's polarized BB 3-bet range post massive flop cooler action). Pot odds ~7.2:1 require only ~12% equity; we have far more (blocks Ad2d/2x flush combos lowly, crushes missed draws/bluffs from his small flop c-bet + huge 4-bet which reps nuts/sets but overplays overpairs often). Kh blank bricks runner-runner straights/draws, denies few outs; position irrelevant all-in. History shows Flash aggressive (Hand 119 huge pot with draws, Hand 112/120 checks), but folding here -EV: realize equity well IP effectively, SPR ~0 commits us. Calling maximizes EV 3-handed deep tourney, exploits odds over nitty fold.

Heads-up on the button with a medium pocket pair (7♦7♠) and deep stacks — this is a clear open-raise spot. Limping or just calling surrenders initiative and gives the BB good odds to see a cheap flop; a small-ish raise both builds the pot with a hand that plays well postflop and generates fold equity against a wide BB calling range. I raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to charge worse hands, define Flash’s range, and leave room to maneuver postflop. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (fold or defend depending on sizing); vs a single call I’ll proceed for value/control on the flop.

With Qd 3c in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x raise from Minni on the Button. This is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am heads-up in this tournament now, I should maintain a solid range and not defend with bottom-tier trash like Q3 offsuit. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In heads-up play, King-high hands like King-Trey offsuit are well above the median hand strength. From the small blind (button), I should be playing a wide range of hands to maintain pressure. A standard raise is preferable to a call or fold here to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or at least define the range of the Big Blind.

Facing a standard button open to 300 from the SB/BTN, I’m in the big blind with A♣? (A♥4♠) — an offsuit ace with decent showdown value and an ace-blocker. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives me ~3:1 immediate pot odds and keeps my range wide; a 3-bet here would be thin and often dominated by better Aces or face too much resistance deep-stacked. I’ll defend and see a flop — plan to proceed cautiously out of position: c-bet/raise only with strong top-pair/top-kicker, good draws, or strong hands; fold to heavy aggression with no improvement.

Check. I have A♥4♠ — a pair of fours with the ace-kicker on Jc-6c-4h. Heads-up vs the BTN’s wide open-raise my hand has good showdown value and an ace blocker, but it’s vulnerable to Jx and club hands. Out of position with deep stacks I want to control the pot and keep worse hands and bluffs in the field rather than bloating the pot by barreling. I’ll check to induce c-bets and call a reasonable small/medium bet to realize equity; if Flash check-raises or posts a large overbet (polarized to Jx/strong club draws), I’ll fold/re-evaluate rather than committing a big portion of my stack.

I raised pre-flop and now have a range advantage on this board, as my opening range contains more high pairs and strong Jacks than Minni's defending range. I hold King-high with a backdoor club flush draw. Given that Minni has checked, a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) is appropriate to take down the pot immediately or to set up future bluffs on later streets. In previous hands, Minni has shown a willingness to fold to flop continuation bets when she doesn't connect.

I have A♥4♠ — middle pair (board 4♥6♣J♣) with an ace blocker. Flash (BTN) c-bet small: 300 into a 900 pot. Calling is the best option here: I get ~4:1 immediate pot odds (call 300 to win 1,200), my hand has decent showdown value heads-up and blocks many A-high combos, and raising out of position with a vulnerable pair would be thin and often face better Jx/club combinations. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, then re-evaluate — fold to large turn overbets or continued strong aggression without improvement, but check/call smaller turn bets to realize equity and protect stack.

I have A♥4♠ — a pair of fours (board 4♥6♣J♣7♥) with a useful Ace blocker but very vulnerable to Jx, club hands, and overcards. Flash bet the flop and then checked the turn, which smells like a missed draw, a pot-control Jx, or a hand slowing down. Out of position with a marginal pair and deep stacks, I want pot control and to keep worse hands and bluffs in the pot rather than inflating it where I’ll often be behind. Checking preserves my stack, realizes showdown value, and lets me call a reasonable river bet or fold to heavy aggression. Plan: check to the river; call small/medium river bets for showdown, fold to polarized overbets, and value-bet only if I improve.

On the turn 7h, the board becomes slightly more coordinated, potentially completing a straight for 58 or improving 7x hands. Having bet the flop with King-high and been called, my hand has very little showdown value, but checking back allows me to control the pot and potentially realize my equity if a King falls on the river. Minni has shown a willingness to call flop bets with various parts of her range, and double barreling here into a range that called the flop on a board that didn't change the texture significantly is risky without a made hand or a strong draw. I will check behind to keep the pot manageable.

I improved to trips on the river (A♥4♠ with board 4♥ 6♣ J♣ 7♥ 4♦) — a very strong hand heads-up. Flash’s line (BTN open, c-bet flop, check on turn and river) looks like many Jx, single pairs, missed club draws or Ace-highs that will call a value bet. Checking ships the pot for free and misses value. I want a size that gets called by worse pairs and Jx but doesn’t fold out too much: pot = 1200, so I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (800). This extracts value from Jx/7x/6x/Ax and other one-pair hands; the only hands that beat me are extremely unlikely (the lone remaining 4 for quads). If Flash raises big, it’s likely quads or a highly polarized river bluff and I’ll re-evaluate, but the thin EV here is to bet 800 for value.

On the river, the board pairs the four, making the final texture 4-6-J-7-4. I have King-high with the King of Clubs. After I checked back the turn, Minni leads out for 800 into a 1200 pot. While her lead could potentially be a missed club draw or a straight draw (like 58), I block the King-high club draws with my King of Clubs. Minni called my flop continuation bet, suggesting she has at least some piece of the board or a draw that may have improved or is now being turned into a bluff. However, King-high is very difficult to call with here as I lose to any pair (Jacks, 7s, 6s, 5s) and any Ace-high bluffs. Given the bet size is two-thirds of the pot, the risk-reward ratio for a hero call with King-high is not favorable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 4♠3♠: this is a playable, suited connector in position and a great candidate to open wide. Opponent is the big blind with a similar deep stack, and table tendencies show standard 3x opens from the SB are effective at stealing the blind and seizing initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to build the pot in position, apply pressure to BB’s weaker holdings, and keep my postflop options (c-bet, delayed bluffs, and fold to strong 3-bets) flexible. If faced with a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll leverage position to play postflop aggressively on favorable textures and pot-control on adverse boards.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit in the big blind, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised from the button to 300. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, the hand has very poor playability out of position. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal holding. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

In a heads-up situation, 8-7 offsuit is a reasonable hand to play aggressively from the small blind/button. I have a healthy stack and want to maintain pressure on Minni. Raising to 300 is a standard opening that allows me to take control of the pot early and utilize my position post-flop.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I have Qc6d in the big blind with deep stacks (~225bb). The pot is 400 and I need 200 to call, giving roughly 3:1 pot odds (break-even ≈33%). Heads-up the opener’s range is wide, and Q6o has reasonable equity and playable postflop lines, so defending is +EV. A tiny min-3bet to 500 won’t fold enough hands and would put me out of position with a weak kicker, while a larger 3bet is unnecessary with this holding. I’ll call to see a flop and plan to proceed based on texture (continue when I pair or pick up strong draws, otherwise fold to heavy pressure).

I called preflop with Q♣6♦ and the flop is 9♦-7♣-8♣. I have no pair and only two outs to a straight (5 or T) — ~8–9% to improve by river — and I don’t have a realistic club flush draw (would need both turn and river). The board heavily favors the opener’s raising range and they’ll c‑bet frequently. With deep stacks I don’t want to commit chips with such weak equity. I’ll check to see what Flash does; plan is to fold to a standard-sized continuation bet but consider calling a very small probe to float and exploit later only if the price and dynamics justify it.

I have flopped two pair (eights and sevens) on a very coordinated board. While my hand is currently very strong in a heads-up pot, it is extremely vulnerable to many turn cards, including any club, six, five, ten, or jack which could complete straights or flushes. Minni has checked to me, and I must bet for value and protection. I want to get paid by top pair (nines), straight draws, and flush draws. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a standard sizing that achieves these goals while maintaining control of the hand.

Call 400. On 9♦-7♣-8♣ with Q♣6♦ I have an open‑ended straight draw: any 5 or any 10 (4+4 = 8 outs). That is roughly 31.4% to make a straight by the river (1 - (39/47)*(38/46)). The pot is 1000 and the bet is 400, so calling 400 to win 1400 requires ~28.6% equity to break even (400/1400). My draw equity (~31%) slightly exceeds the break‑even threshold and there are implied odds if I hit, so a call is +EV. I won't raise — a raise bloats the pot with a marginal hand and risks committing stacks versus a range that can have made straights/sets. Plan: realize the draw by calling; if I hit a 5/10 on the turn I'll proceed for value, if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I'll fold unless the price or read justifies continuing.

I missed my straight on the turn (9♦-7♣-8♣ -> 9♣). With Q♣6♦ I don’t have a made hand yet; I do have a one-card flush draw (Qc + 7c,8c,9c = 4 clubs) and the 5s/10s still give me a straight. Raw outs to improve to a straight or flush on the river ≈ 15/46 ≈ 33%, but many of those outs are “dirty” (a club could give Flash a higher club (A♣/K♣), some 5s/10s can produce full houses vs opponent combos that include a 9, etc.). Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop, which looks like a pot-control or weak/missed line rather than heavy strength. With one card to come and decent but not dominant equity, the lowest-variance / highest-expected-value line is to check and realize my equity rather than bluffing/semibluffing OOP into a potentially sticky opponent and bloating the pot when I’m behind. Plan: check the turn. On the river, if I complete a clean-looking straight/flush I’ll bet for value (and be cautious vs action that indicates a higher flush/full house); if I miss and face significant river aggression I’ll fold most of the time, but I’ll call small probes where my showdown equity justifies it.

The 9c on the turn is a significant card that changes the board texture considerably. It pairs the top card and completes the club flush. My two pair (eights and sevens) is now potentially counterfeited or beaten by any 9 (trips) or any club (flush). Since Minni called the flop bet, her range includes many nines, clubs, or straight draws that might have just improved. Checking back is the best option here to control the pot and see a river card, as my hand still has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value against a range that likely improved on this turn.

I rivered top pair (Q♣6♦; board 9♦-7♣-8♣-9♣-Q♠). This is a wet, tricky board: paired nines and three clubs mean many hands (9x, club flushes) beat me, and several Qx/overcards/weak holdings are in the opener’s wide preflop/c-bet range. Flash bet the flop and then checked the turn — a line that often represents missed draws or pot-control with medium hands rather than absolute strength. With that line I can credibly extract thin value from worse hands (Kx, Jx, missed draws, some weaker Qx who will call a modest river bet) while not bloating the pot into obvious monsters.

I’ll make a modest value bet rather than check: bet ~½ pot (700 into 1400). Size is big enough to charge pure air and many marginal overcards, but small enough that I don’t commit too many chips if I hit resistance. If Flash raises substantially I’ll assume a club flush/9x/full house and fold; if he calls I’ll go to showdown and accept that my weak kicker will lose sometimes but the line and sizing maximize my expected value against his wide checking range.

On the flop, I had a very strong hand with two pair (eights and sevens). However, the 9c on the turn was an extremely poor card for me; it paired the board's top card and completed a possible club flush, meaning my flopped two-pair was effectively downgraded to two-pair (nines and eights). Checking back on the turn for pot control was necessary. The river Queen is another overcard that could give Minni a higher two-pair if she has any Queen in her range. However, Minni's 700 bet into a 1400 pot is a sizing that I must call in a heads-up match given the 3:1 pot odds. My hand still beats a pair of sevens, lower pocket pairs (like 66 or 55), or missed straight draws that might be bluffing. While she could certainly have trips, a flush, or a Queen, the range of hands I beat is wide enough to justify a call here.

Heads-up on the button (I am the SB) with J♦8♠. With deep stacks (~240bb) and only 50 to call, I should open-raise rather than limp to seize initiative and exploit the BB's wide defending range. J8o is perfectly fine to open heads-up — it plays well postflop in position and has good equity against a calling range. The min raise is 200, but I'll use my standard 3x sizing to 300 to generate meaningful fold equity while keeping postflop playability and a reasonable SPR. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold; if he calls I can play the pot in position. Raise to 300.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Nine offsuit (Q9o) is a solid hand to defend from the Big Blind against a standard 3x raise from the Button/Small Blind. I am receiving 2:1 odds on a call, and the hand has decent playability and high-card value. Re-raising (3-betting) is an option but might be unnecessarily risky given the current stack depths and my position; calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity.

On a very dry, paired board of 2-5-5, my Queen-high has some showdown value but is mostly a hand I want to play cautiously. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check and see how she reacts. This board texture is generally better for the Big Blind's range (which contains more 5x hands), but since I don't have a 5, checking allows me to control the pot size and decide whether to float or fold based on the size of her continuation bet.

Heads-up in position on 2♦-5♠-5♣ with J♦8♠. The board is low and paired and Flash checked, so my wide raising range contains plenty of hands that can profitably C-bet. I have two overcards and decent fold equity versus missed hands and floats, but no pair myself, so I want to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. I'll make a small standard continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 200) to fold out weaker hands and gather information. If called I’ll evaluate the turn and barrel selectively; if I face a substantial check-raise I’ll fold most of the time given my lack of pair.

On this extremely dry, paired board (2-5-5), Minni's small bet of 200 into a 600 pot represents a standard continuation bet that she would make with a large portion of her range. Queen-high has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot on such a disconnected board, and I am getting 4:1 odds to call. Folding would be over-folding against such a small sizing. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn, as my hand is currently ahead of her air bluffs and has equity against her overcards.

On the turn card 7c, the board remains paired and relatively disconnected. Having called a small flop bet with Queen-high, I still have reasonable showdown value against Minni's potential air and overcard bluffs. However, as the out-of-position player, I should check to the aggressor to see how she proceeds. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the action goes check-check.

I opened preflop and c-bet the 2♦-5♠-5♣ flop (I bet 200, Villain called). Pot is 1,000 and the turn is 7♣. I hold J♦8♠ — no pair and no realistic draw (with only one spade on board a five‑card flush is impossible). Villain checked the turn after calling my small flop c‑bet, which looks like either a weak float/overcard, a medium pair, or a slow‑played stronger hand. Betting again as a bluff is thin: his calling range contains many hands that will simply call/buy a cheap showdown (Kx, Qx, small pairs) and I have very little fold equity or equity if called. I have position and can see the river for free to realize my two overcards’ equity and keep the pot manageable. Plan: check back the turn, induce bluffs or call small river bets, and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

On a very dry, paired board of 2-5-5-7-3, Queen-high has some showdown value in a heads-up pot. Minni's check on the turn suggests she may have a marginal hand she wants to take to showdown or is giving up with air. Betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold (like small pairs) and is almost never called by worse. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to realize my showdown value and see if my Queen-high is sufficient to win the pot against her missed overcards.

I have J♦8♠ on 2♦-5♠-5♣-7♣-3♦ — no pair and no meaningful draw. Villain called my small 200 flop C-bet and then checked the turn; that line looks like medium-strength hands (small/mid pairs, occasional 7x) or showdown floats. My river bluff has very little fold equity — many hands in his calling range will simply call a small bet — and I have almost no showdown equity if he has a pair or better. Betting only bloats the pot with a marginal hand. With the pot small (1,000) and deep stacks, the prudent line is to check and take it to showdown (or induce a bluff), rather than risk chips on a thin bluff.

In a heads-up situation with a very weak hand like 5c 3s, the best play is often to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand, especially against an aggressive opponent like Minni who has shown a tendency to raise or outplay marginal hands post-flop. With deep stacks and approximately 22 hands remaining in the tournament, preserving chips is more important than contesting a small pot with the bottom 10% of starting hands.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. 2♦4♣ is weak, but in position I can raise for fold equity and to seize initiative. A minimum raise to 200 is standard here — it’s small enough to keep the pot controllable but applies pressure to the BB. Plan: take the flop in position if called and c-bet favorable textures; fold to a large 3-bet since this hand performs poorly versus strong reraises.

I am holding 6s 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although the pot odds are 3:1 (calling 100 into a pot of 300), 62o plays extremely poorly post-flop, especially while out of position against a stack leader like Minni. I have a healthy stack of over 160 big blinds and there is no reason to defend with junk. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Six offsuit is a hand that should be played from the button. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and exert pressure on the big blind, as playing aggressively from the button is standard strategy in this phase of the tournament.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♦ J♥ facing a button open to 300. Flash has been opening fairly often, so AJo is ahead of a lot of his raising range and is a good 3-bet candidate for value and fold equity. I’ll raise to 900 total (adding 800 more) — big enough to fold out weaker broadways and many speculative hands while still keeping the pot controllable with ~160BB effective stacks. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I have a playable hand with good equity and initiative postflop; if he 4-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to a polarized shove).

In this heads-up situation, I opened Q6o from the button, which is standard. However, Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I have the advantage of position post-flop, Q6o is a weak hand that is frequently dominated by a 3-betting range. Calling would require me to commit more chips with a hand that has poor playability and low equity against stronger hands. Given the tournament stage and the need to preserve my stack for more favorable spots, folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice.

Heads-up on the button with AdJd — a very strong opening hand. I want initiative and fold equity plus the positional advantage postflop, so raising is correct. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total) builds the pot, isolates the big blind and keeps stacks deep for postflop play without overcommitting. Calling 50 would be too passive and surrender initiative; 3-betting bigger isn’t necessary here against a single big blind. I’ll raise to 200.

In a heads-up situation, King-Seven offsuit is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise. Minni has been frequently opening from the button, and the price to call is excellent (100 chips to win a 300 chip pot). I will call to see a flop and utilize my high-card value.

On a board of Ace-high and Jack-high (As, 2d, Js), my King-high (Kc, 7s) has very little equity. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I will check to the Button and evaluate their response. Leading here would likely be ineffective as a bluff given how many high cards hit Minni's opening range.

I have A♦J♦ on A♠ 2♦ J♠ — top two pair in a heads-up pot after my preflop raise. Flash checked the flop, which can be weakness or a disguised monster, but my hand is strong and I have blockers to AA/JJ (reduces set frequency). I should bet for value and to charge any Ax, Jx and any backdoor draws while denying a free card. A bet of ~75% pot (300 into 400) extracts value from worse hands and is large enough to fold out some turn equity without overcommitting. If I face a big check‑raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely a set) and proceed cautiously.

On this Ace-high board (As, 2d, Js), my King-high has very little equity against Minni's range, which includes many Aces, Jacks, and spade draws. Minni's continuation bet is strong on this texture, and without a pair or a significant draw, there is no reason to continue in this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Jack-Trey offsuit. While heads-up play allows for a wide opening range from the button, J3o is near the bottom of that range. Given Minni's recent aggression and 3-betting frequency (as seen in Hand #130), I prefer to fold this hand and look for better spots to commit chips rather than playing a weak hand in a potentially bloated pot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♣3♠ — marginal but perfectly fine to open in this spot. I want to take the initiative, leverage position, and put pressure on the big blind’s wide defending range. A standard 2xBB/min-raise (to 200 total) keeps the pot manageable while maximizing fold equity and lets me play postflop in position. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll give up K3o; if he calls I can c-bet appropriate flops and use positional advantage to realize equity.

I am being offered 3:1 pot odds to see a flop with a suited hand. While 8-3 suited is a weak starting hand, the price to call 100 chips into a 300 chip pot is favorable for defending the big blind against a button open, especially in a heads-up context. I will call to see the flop and hope for some connectivity or a diamond draw.

The flop has come 6s-2s-6h, pairing the board. I have eight-high with no connectivity or draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check and see how Minni proceeds. Leading here would be a pure bluff into a range that likely contains better high cards or pairs, and I have very little equity to improve.

I was the preflop aggressor heads-up and the flop (6♠ 2♠ 6♥) checked to me. I hold K♣3♠ — only K‑high with a useless runner‑runner spade backdoor and no pair. Flash’s defending range is wide but contains a lot of overcards and small pairs that missed this board; many of those hands will fold to continued aggression. I’ll continuation‑bet to take the initiative, charge any real draws and fold out single overcards — a size of ~60–65% pot is large enough to generate fold equity but small enough to avoid committing my stack with a marginal hand. Plan: if I get a check‑raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (check back a blank, pot control vs signs of strength, consider a small second barrel only if the turn is favorable and villain looks weak).

On a board of 6s-2s-6h, I have eight-high with no flush or straight draws. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 260 into a 400 pot. Given that I have no equity or realistic way to improve on this board, I cannot justify a call. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K5s is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Being on the button (Small Blind), I want to take control of the hand and build the pot. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play with position.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m the big blind holding 5♠2♦. I’ve already got 100 in and must call 200 to see the flop. 5-2 offsuit is the bottom of the range: heavily dominated, almost no backdoor play, and very hard to play out of position. Calling 200 into the 400 pot gives about 33% break-even equity — this hand’s actual equity versus a standard HU open is well below that once you account for postflop positional disadvantage. 3-betting this hand is pure spew. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament, the correct, risk-averse and +EV play is to fold and wait for a much better spot.

AKo on the button (heads-up) — a premium hand that should not be limped. With deep stacks (≈243BB) and one opponent, I want to take the initiative, build a pot when I'm likely ahead, and deny cheap flops to speculative holdings. I will raise to 300 total (3x BB) — big enough to get value and fold equity but small enough to keep postflop play manageable. If Flash 3-bets, I’ll usually call to play AKo in position against a polarized range, and I’ll consider a 4‑bet or shove only versus very specific large 3-bet sizings or reads. Overall: raise for value and to leverage positional advantage.

With Hand #135, I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4d, a very weak hand. The opponent, Minni, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. Given the stack sizes and the late stage of the tournament (Hand 135/150), I need to be selective about the hands I play out of position. 94 offsuit does not play well post-flop and is well below the range of hands I should be defending with, even against a standard button raise. Folding here preserves my stack of 15,537 for better opportunities.

Holding King-Six suited on the button in a heads-up situation is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. This hand has good playability post-flop due to its flush potential and high-card value.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300 from the SB. I’m in the BB with T♥ J♠ — a strong heads-up hand that plays well both as a 3-bet and postflop. Folding is passive and calling surrenders initiative and leaves me OOP; I want to apply pressure and get value/fold equity. I’ll 3-bet to 900 total (about 3x the open) — large enough to force folds from some of his wider openers and to build a pot with a hand that flops well, but small enough to keep postflop decisions manageable. Plan: if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable broadway/paired flops and proceed cautiously on dry low-card boards; if he 4-bets large I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack. Raising to 900 maximizes EV here.

Holding King-Six of hearts in a heads-up match, I have a strong enough hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially with the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. Minni has 3-bet my opening raises several times recently (Hands 70, 73, 106, 130), and I have folded each time. Continuing here prevents her from profitably 3-betting me with a wide range and allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good high-card value and flush potential.

Heads‑up, 3‑bet pot: pot = 1,800. I hold T♥ J♠ on 6♣ A♥ Q♣. I have no pair and only a single‑card Broadway draw (any K gives me Broadway: T‑J‑Q‑K‑A — 4 outs) and negligible runner‑runner equity. Flash called my 3‑bet preflop, so his calling range is wide but contains many A‑x, Q‑x, broadways and medium pairs. This flop favors the preflop caller (lots of A/Q combos), but there are plenty of hands in his range that miss and can fold to pressure.

Plan: make a standard heads‑up c‑bet of ~50% pot (900). That size gives good fold equity against missed hands and keeps the pot manageable if called by Ax/Qx/pairs. If he folds we take down the pot; if he calls I keep a clear plan — give up to heavy action unless a King comes (completing my straight), and proceed cautiously on the turn. If he raises big, I’ll fold my two‑card draw rather than gamble large with only 4 outs.

On a board of Ace-High, Queen-High, and Six-Low, holding bottom pair (Sixes) after calling a pre-flop 3-bet is a precarious position. Minni's 3-betting range heavily favors high cards like Ace-King, Ace-Queen, and big pocket pairs, all of which have me significantly beat on this texture. While I have the King of hearts for a backdoor flush draw and the six for a pair, the board is too favorable for Minni's perceived range to justify a call or a raise. Continuing here would likely lead to more difficult decisions on later streets against an opponent who has shown consistent aggression. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) — Q♣2♥ is weak but perfectly playable in position. Limping gives the big blind cheap odds and forfeits initiative; I want to use my positional advantage and apply pressure. A standard 3xBB open (raise to 300 total) balances fold equity and keeps the raise small relative to our deep stacks. Plan: if called I’ll continue to C‑bet favorable flops and leverage position; fold or re-evaluate only if Flash 3-bets big (Q2 is easily dominated).

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With an effective stack of around 145 big blinds, I have plenty of room to play. Ace-Nine is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I will call to keep the pot manageable and see a flop, as raising might force out the weaker parts of Minni's range that I dominate, while a call allows me to play post-flop with a strong starting hand.

On this paired board (6h-8d-8h), I have Ace-high with a Nine kicker. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they react to the board. Ace-Nine has some showdown value and can certainly improve on many turns, but it's not strong enough to lead out with a bet here.

I raised pre‑flop and have Q♣2♥ on a 6♥ 8♦ 8♥ flop. I have no made hand but I do have the initiative and a small backdoor heart draw. Heads‑up, a continuation bet here will often take the pot away from unpaired hands and small pocket pairs that will check behind. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (300 into the 600 pot) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash check‑raises I’ll fold (Q2 has too little equity); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue aggressively if I improve or get a favorable card.

I have Ace-high with a Nine kicker on a paired board of Eight-Eight-Six. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of half the pot. Ace-high is a very strong hand in heads-up play on this type of board, as it is difficult for my opponent to have connected. I will call the 300 to see a turn card, as my hand has significant showdown value and I want to keep Minni's potential bluffs in the pot rather than forcing them out with a raise.

The turn has brought the Ace of Spades, giving me top pair. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, I will continue with a check. This allows Minni to continue her aggression if she is bluffing or holding a marginal hand, and it helps me control the size of the pot in case she has a stronger hand like an eight.

I c‑bet the flop with Q♣2♥ and Flash called. The A♠ on the turn is a horrible card for my air — it helps lots of hands in his calling range (A‑x, 8x, 6x, some two‑pairs) and significantly reduces my fold equity. I have no pair and only tiny runner‑runner heart/flush chances, so a double‑barrel would be a thin bluff that will get called by most hands that already beat me. With pot control and stack preservation as priorities (pot = 1,200; stacks deep), I’ll check the turn, see a river, and only continue if I improve or get a very favorable river action. If the river misses me I’ll fold to any meaningful bet; if I pair my Q or pick up extra outs I can consider a value raise or bluff depending on sizing.

On the river, the board is 6h-8d-8h-As-Tc. I have top pair with an Ace and a Nine kicker. Minni checked back on the turn after the Ace fell, which significantly caps her range; it's unlikely she has an Eight, a strong Ace, or a monster like a full house, as she likely would have continued betting for value or protection on such a draw-heavy flop (flush draw was present). By checking the river, I allow Minni to bluff with her missed draws or weak holdings that wouldn't call a bet. If she checks behind, I have high confidence my top pair is the winning hand.

I miss — Q♣2♥ has no pair on 6♥ 8♦ 8♥ A♠ T♣. I c-bet the flop and got called, the turn A killed much of my fold equity and I checked to control the pot. The river Ten doesn’t improve me and a bluff here is very thin: Flash called the flop and then checked turn and river, so he’s unlikely to fold enough to make a river bluff +EV. Preserving stack and avoiding a losing bluff is the priority. I’ll check and take showdown for free; if Flash now leads a meaningful bet I’ll fold (only consider calling very tiny probes).
In a heads-up situation, K5o is a solid hand to play aggressively from the button. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up vs the button opener — J♣7♥ is marginal but playable from the big blind. Flash opened to 300 from the small blind, and I only need to call 200 into a 400 pot (pot odds roughly 2:1, ~33% equity required). 3-betting out of position with J7o is suboptimal — I’d rather see a flop and use positionless post-flop play (check/call or fold to heavy aggression) than commit more pre-flop. I’ll defend my blind with a call and reevaluate on the flop based on texture and opponent action. Total to put in pot after calling: 300.

I defended preflop to see a flop cheaply. Flop is 4♦ K♣ 9♣ and my J♣7♥ has no pair and only a runner-runner club possibility (both turn+river clubs — ~4%); effectively I have very little showdown equity vs a preflop opener who often has Kx, club holdings or broadways. Out of position, leading here with a weak, non‑drawing hand is spewy and vulnerable to a check‑raise or easy value bets. I’ll check to control the pot, see what Flash does, and fold to significant pressure; I may call a very small bet but will let go to larger bets.

I have flopped top pair with Kings. While my kicker (5) is weak, in a heads-up pot, top pair is a very strong hand. The board has a flush draw (clubs) and some straight possibilities (9-T-J region), so I should bet to protect my hand and extract value from weaker kings, nines, or draws. A standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) is appropriate here to maintain the initiative.

Heads‑up OOP on a K♣9♣4♦ flop with J♣7♥: I missed — no pair and only a runner‑runner club flush (~4%). Flash opened preflop and c‑bet small (300 into a 900 pot), which could be a standard low‑size continuation or a probe for folds. 3‑betting OOP with J7o is spewy and often dominated; folding surrenders decent pot odds. Calling 300 gives me ~25% pot odds to see a cheap turn and preserves flexibility. Plan: call to see the turn, and fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pick up a club, a pair, or clear initiative/reading of weakness.

Heads‑up, OOP with J♣7♥ on 4♦ K♣ 9♣ 9♠. I have no pair and the turn killed the backdoor club possibility — I now only have 6 outs (three J’s and three 7’s) to make a pair on the river (~6/46 ≈ 13%). Pot is 1,200. Against any reasonable turn bet (for example 300) I would need ~16.7% equity to profitably call, so I don’t have the odds to chase. Flash’s profile: opened and c‑bet small on the flop (likely Kx, club holdings, broadways or 9x); the paired 9 on the turn actually helps parts of that value range. Out of position with a naked jack and no draw, the correct line is pot control and to fold to aggression. I’ll check and see the river for free; if Flash bets I’ll fold unless it’s an absurdly tiny probe (and even then it’s marginal). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I have top pair with a weak kicker. The nine pairing on the turn is a 'scare card' that makes trips a possibility for Minni, though somewhat unlikely given the action. Checking back here controls the size of the pot and allows me to see the river for free. I still have significant showdown value, but my hand isn't strong enough to comfortably bet for value over three streets, especially given the board texture and the risk of a check-raise.

I have J♣7♥ on 4♦ K♣ 9♣ 9♠ T♠ — I missed everything and have no showdown value. Flash opened and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn and checked the river, showing weakness but also giving me very little fold equity with a bluff (they can comfortably call with Kx, Tx, 9x or a medium pair). The pot is only 1,200 vs deep stacks, so risking chips for a thin bluff is unnecessary. Best line is to check and take the free showdown — preserve my stack and move on to better spots.

I have top pair with Kings, but my kicker is very weak (5). The board is somewhat coordinated with a pair of nines and a possible straight with QJ. Minni has checked to me on the river after calling my flop bet and checking the turn. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown with a hand that has significant value but is vulnerable to being check-raised if I bet. Since I'm unlikely to get called by many worse hands and will almost certainly be called by better ones, checking is the most reliable way to realize my equity.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be opening a wide stealing range — T5o is marginal but perfectly playable in this spot. Limping/calling 50 surrenders initiative and gives the BB cheap odds; raising gets fold equity and lets me play the rest of the hand in position. I’ll make a standard heads-up open to 300 to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call small resistance occasionally, and c-bet selectively on favorable flops to extract value or take the pot down.

With Q9o in the Big Blind against a standard 3x open from the Button (Minni), I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call. Q9o has sufficient equity against a typical button opening range to see a flop. Given my current stack deficit (approx. 15.7k vs 23.8k) and the proximity to the end of the match (hand 139 of 150), I need to defend my blinds and look for opportunities to chip up without over-committing with marginal hands pre-flop.

I am first to act on a dry J-6-4 rainbow flop. Holding Queen-high with two overcards, I have some equity but no made hand. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here, allowing me to see Minni's continuation bet sizing before deciding whether to peel a turn card or fold. Leading out would be inefficient as I have very little to represent on this texture.

I opened preflop on the button and got called. Flop 6♣-4♥-J♦ is a very dry, rainbow texture that heavily favors my preflop raising range. I hold T♦5♣ — no pair and limited showdown equity — so this is a textbook continuation-bet spot to seize the initiative and fold out unpaired hands that called preflop. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to apply pressure (400 chips). If Flash folds I take it; if called I’ll reassess on the turn and continue only with improvement or a clear read/blank to barrel, otherwise slow down and control the pot.

On a J-6-4 rainbow board, my Queen-high has very little equity against Minni's continuation bet of 400 into 600. While I have two overcards to the middle cards and one overcard to the Jack, calling here out of position without even a backdoor flush draw is generally a losing play. Minni's sizing (66% pot) indicates a strong range or a determined bluff on a dry texture. Given the 11 hands remaining and my current stack deficit, I need to be more selective about the pots I contest. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity to regain chips.

I am holding 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I am on the button in a heads-up situation, the hand has extremely poor playability and equity. Minni has shown a willingness to defend the big blind and play aggressively post-flop. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant stack to protect, I will choose to fold this hand rather than risk chips on a high-variance steal attempt with the bottom of my range.

Heads-up on the button with K♣4♣ — a suited King is worth opening for initiative. Calling 50 would surrender position and fold equity; folding is too weak. I want a standard heads-up open to apply pressure and steal the BB, and 300 (≈3x the BB) is a normal, clear sizing that’s consistent with my previous opens. With deep stacks I can continue postflop if called; I’ll fold to a large 3-bet but otherwise play position and fold equity to my advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with J3o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While J3o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation, I am getting 2:1 odds on a call. However, J3o is significantly below the average defending range even against a standard button open. Looking at the tournament status, we are near the end (Hand 141/150). Minni is the chip leader and has been aggressive with button opens. I have a healthy stack of over 150 BBs. Given the weakness of J3o and the fact that I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot to challenge Minni's lead.

With 75s on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a solid speculative hand with good post-flop playability. Raising to 300 is a standard open to exert pressure on the big blind and seize the initiative. Given that we are approaching the end of the tournament (Hand #142/150) and I'm currently trailing in chips, I need to stay active and look for profitable spots to close the gap.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who raised to 300 from the SB. I’m in the BB with Ts3h — a marginal but playable hand in heads-up. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total), which gives ~33% break‑even equity (200 into ~600). Versus a typical wide button opening range T3o has enough equity to continue, and with deep stacks I can realize that equity postflop. I’ll avoid a light 3‑bet out of position with a weak kicker; instead I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, continue or raise when I hit a ten or pick up strong draws. Call 300 total.

Heads‑up OOP with T3o on Kd 9s 8h — no pair, only a backdoor heart and very weak showdown value. This is a K‑high, relatively dry board that heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range. With deep stacks I don’t want to build a pot out of position with air, so I’ll check and re‑evaluate based on Flash’s action. Plan: check to control the pot; if Flash checks back I can realize equity or take a free turn stab later. If Flash c‑bets small (~≤1/3 pot) I’ll consider calling as a float to try and bluff later; if Flash c‑bets medium/large (>~1/3–1/2 pot) I’ll fold. Checking preserves stack and keeps options open.

On a board of 8h-9s-Kd, I have an inside straight draw (needing a 6) and backdoor club potential. Minni has checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. This board includes a King, which generally favors my perceived range as the opener. By betting here, I can take down the pot immediately if Minni has missed or set up a semi-bluff for later streets if I am called. Given the tournament stage and my chip deficit, maintaining aggression in small-to-medium pots is necessary.

Heads‑up out of position on a K‑9‑8 rainbow flop with T3o. Pot is 900 and Flash c‑bets a small 300 (≈1/3 pot), so a call costs 300 and gives me ~20% break‑even equity (300 / (900+2*300) = 20%). Flash (the button) opens and cbets wide in heads‑up spots, so this small size is consistent with a lot of bluffs/mediocre hands as well as some Kx/broadways. T3s has little immediate showdown value but some backdoor heart/fold equity and decent float potential vs a small c‑bet. With deep stacks and a wide opponent range I’ll call to see the turn, plan to evaluate the turn card and opponent’s sizing: fold to heavy turn pressure with no improvement, bet/raise for value if I pair my ten (or improve), and look to bluff on favorable turn/river runouts if given a good opportunity.

I’m out of position with T♠3♥ on 8♥‑9♠‑K♦‑6♥. I have no pair and very little equity: no realistic flush (I only have one heart), only a one‑card river straight (7) plus lone pair outs (T or 3) — roughly ~7 outs total (~15%) to improve. Flash is the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop small, so his range contains a lot of Kx/9x/broadways and many hands that will continue; I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with air. With pot control and stack preservation as priorities, I’ll check the turn and re‑evaluate the river. If Flash bets a small probe I may call as a float, but I’ll fold to meaningful pressure; if he checks back I can consider a small bluff on a safe river or value any pair. Checking keeps options open and avoids committing chips with a marginal hand.

On a board of 8h-9s-Kd-6h, my 5c-7c has completed a straight (5-6-7-8-9). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has checked to me again on the turn after calling my flop bet. I need to continue building the pot and extracting value from hands like a King, a Nine, or two-pair combinations. Given that I am currently trailing in the tournament with only a few hands remaining, maximizing value on my strong holdings is crucial. I will bet 800, which is two-thirds of the pot, to keep her range wide while still charging her for any draws or marginal made hands.

I’m OOP with T♠3♥ on 8♥‑9♠‑K♦‑6♥. Pot is 2000 and it costs 800 to call, so I need ~28.6% equity to justify a call (800 / (2000+800)). My realistic river outs are: 3 tens + 3 threes + 4 sevens = ~10 outs → ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve to a pair/straight, and many of those hits (a pair) will still be behind common Kx/9x value hands. A heart flush is impossible and my hand has very little showdown value right now. Flash’s line (open pre, small c‑bet, now a larger turn bet) is consistent with a lot of value (Kx/9x/broadways) as well as some bluffs, but my pot‑odds and raw equity don’t justify calling OOP; raising as a bluff is too thin here given stack depth and position. Fold and wait for a better, more profitable spot.

Heads-up on the button with J♦8♥ — a playable, reasonably strong hand in heads-up play. Flash (BB) will defend wide, so I want initiative and fold equity rather than limp/call. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): big enough to apply pressure and build a pot when I have the lead, but small relative to our deep stacks so I can fold to a big 3-bet without risking too much. Plan: if called, use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on dry or heavily resisted boards; if 3‑bet large, fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300. To call, I need to put in 200 more. While the pot odds are 2:1, this hand performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and a gap of approximately 8,000 chips to close, I need to be selective about the pots I contest to avoid bleeding chips. I will fold this junk hand and look for a better opportunity to make a move in the final few hands.

In heads-up play, 7-6 offsuit is a standard hand to open from the button. Raising to 300 allows me to exert pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand with position. Given the stage of the tournament and my stack size, maintaining an aggressive button strategy is key to accumulating chips.

Heads-up in the big blind with Qc Jd — QJ offsuit is a strong, playable hand against a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more (300 total). With very deep stacks and being out of position, I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal 3-bet; calling keeps the pot manageable, gets good pot odds, and allows me to see a flop where QJ has good equity against a wide button raising range. Plan: call, evaluate flop — continue aggressively if I pair/land a strong draw, fold or pot-control if I miss and face heavy aggression.

I have top pair (Qc pairing the Qd on board) with a decent jack kicker and one diamond (Jd) — a weak backdoor diamond possibility. Heads-up vs a wide button opening range, this is a spot to bet for value and to deny free cards to overcards/draws. A half-pot c-bet (≈300 into 600) extracts value from worse Qx, medium pairs and floats, charges hands with A/K and single-diamond combos, but keeps the pot controlled given our deep stack. If called I'll re-evaluate on the turn (value-bet good turns, pot-control or fold to strong action on scary cards).

Minni has led into me on the flop with a 'donk bet' of 300 into a 600 pot. My 7-6 has connected for middle pair on a board of Qd-6d-4h. While the Queen is an overcard, middle pair in heads-up play is a strong enough hand to continue, especially given that Minni's lead could represent a wide range of hands including draws (like a diamond flush draw or straight draws) or even a smaller pair. Calling is the most prudent action here; it keeps the pot manageable, allows me to re-evaluate on the turn, and maintains my positional advantage without overcommitting to a hand that is currently just a marginal made hand.

On the turn I hold Qc Jd on Qd 6d 4h Kd. I currently have a pair of queens (now second pair because the King is on board) with a jack kicker plus a very strong diamond draw (four diamonds to the board with my Jd). Heads-up versus Flash’s calling range this is a hand to bet for value and protection: many worse Qx, pairs and draws will continue, and I also deny free rivers to overcards. The pot is 1,200 — I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (800) to charge draws and extract value while keeping the pot manageable given the K on board. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I still have ~12 clean outs to improve and will re-evaluate the river (value-bet safe rivers, pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on dangerous runouts). If he raises big on the turn I’ll assume a made flush or very strong two-pair/trips and give it up to large aggression.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds appears, which is another overcard to my pair of sixes. Minni's decision to lead into me on both the flop and the turn (double barreling after a donk lead) is a strong signal. While she could be semi-bluffing with a diamond draw or a straight draw, my third pair (sixes) is now extremely vulnerable. The pot odds are roughly 2.5 to 1, but with two overcards on the board and the potential for a flush draw to complete on the river, calling here is speculative. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 144 of 150) and preserving my stack is important to stay competitive, I will fold this marginal hand rather than risk a significant portion of my chips on a weak bluff catcher.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦7♠ — not a premium holding but perfectly fine to open from the button. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is too passive; I want the initiative and some fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 total (my usual 3x BB open here) which pressures the BB, keeps the pot manageable relative to my deep stack (24,663) and is consistent with my prior open-sizing. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold Q7o; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable, dry or Q-high boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated/overcard-heavy boards.

With only 6 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant stack deficit compared to Minni, I need to be selective about the pots I contest, especially out of position. Minni has been opening frequently from the button. 9h 4c is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop. Even with 2:1 pot odds to call, the lack of playability and potential for reverse implied odds make this a standard fold in the Big Blind.

Holding King-Eight of hearts (K8s) on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a very strong starting hand. I want to build the pot and exert pressure. A standard 3x open raise is appropriate here to take control of the hand.

Heads-up vs a standard button open to 300. I hold T♥5♦ in the big blind. Facing a 3x open, calling 200 more into a 400 pot gives me pot-odds that require roughly ~33% equity to continue — versus a wide BTN opening range T5o is borderline but playable. I’m out of position and this is a weak, often-dominated hand, so I don’t want to 3-bet and inflate the pot with little fold equity (a tiny 3-bet to 500 will get called too often). Plan: call to see a flop, fold to significant aggression on dry boards, and continue only with a pair, good backdoor draws, or favorable runouts. Preserve my deeper stack and use position/stack leverage in later hands.

I completely missed this dry, rainbow flop (Jh 8c 4d) with T5o — no pair, no realistic draw (I only have one diamond, so a flush by river is impossible). I’m out of position against the preflop opener and the pot is small relative to effective stacks, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal bluff. Standard, low-variance play is to check and fold to meaningful pressure; if Flash checks back I can re-evaluate on the turn, but I’ll give up to a c-bet here.

I have middle pair (Eights) with a King kicker on a board of Jack-high. This is a solid marginal hand. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet to extract value from draws (like T9, 76, or 56), smaller pairs (4x or pocket pairs), and Ace-high. A 50% pot bet (300) is standard in this spot to maintain initiative and protect my hand equity.

Dry, rainbow flop (Jh 8c 4d) — I hold T♥5♦ with no pair and no realistic draw (flush is impossible from here). Flash was the preflop raiser and now puts in a 300 c-bet into a 600 pot, so calling 300 requires ~25% equity. I only have ~6 clean outs (3 tens + 3 fives), ~24% to hit by the river — slightly below the pot-odds threshold — and I’m out of position. Raising as a bluff is spewy here and will often get me punished or inflated into a pot I can’t profitably win. I planned to check-fold dry boards, so I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K♦7♥. In heads-up Kx is a playable, often-dominant hand against a big-blind’s wide defending range. Flash has shown a fairly loose/aggressive preflop profile, so I want to take the initiative and leverage position postflop. Raising to 300 total (≈3x BB) applies pressure, gains fold equity, and builds a pot I can play in position while still keeping my stacks deep. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (K7o is usually a fold to a big 3-bet), but opening here is the +EV line.

With only 4 hands remaining in the tournament and trailing by approximately 88 big blinds, I need to look for opportunities to win pots. A5o is a strong hand heads-up against a button raise. While 3-betting is an option to exert pressure, calling allows me to see a flop with good pot odds (3:1) and potentially outplay the opponent post-flop or hit an Ace. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament, protecting my stack while staying competitive is key, but I will opt for a call here to keep the pot controlled and evaluate the board.

On a flop of 5s-8d-2s, I have middle pair with an Ace kicker (A5o). In a heads-up pot where I called a pre-flop raise from the big blind, checking to the aggressor is the standard play. My hand is strong enough to call a bet, but leading out (donking) usually doesn't accomplish much against a wide button range. I will check and evaluate Minni's response, intending to call most standard bet sizes to see a turn.

Heads-up in position after I opened preflop; Flop (5♠ 8♦ 2♠) is very dry and Flash defended a wide range by calling. I have K7o — no pair but good high-card and initiative. A continuation bet exploits his wide checking range, folding out many overcards and weaker holdings while getting value from worse Kx, gutshots and some small pairs/draws. I’ll bet ~50% pot to keep the pot manageable and retain the ability to fold to a sizable check‑raise; if he just calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn, if he raises big I’ll usually fold K7o.

On a board of 8-5-2, my middle pair (Fives) with an Ace kicker is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot, especially against a wide button-opening range. Minni's 300-chip bet into a 600-chip pot is standard. Calling is the most effective play here as it allows me to keep Minni's bluffs and weaker draws in the pot while controlling the size of the pot with a marginal made hand. Raising might force out hands I'm beating and bloat the pot when I am likely to be behind better eights or overpairs. I will call and see how the turn develops.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Fives) on the turn. In a heads-up pot against an aggressive button-raiser like Minni, checking is the most effective play to induce bluffs or entice bets from weaker Aces, Eights, or draws. Donk-betting into the turn Ace would likely telegraph the strength of my hand and scare off her air. With only four hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip deficit, I need to maximize value; checking to the aggressor allows me to keep her entire range involved and potentially set up a larger pot on the river or via a check-raise.

Board is 5♠ 8♦ 2♠ / A♣ on the turn, pot 1,200. I opened pre and c‑bet the dry flop (300) and Flash called — his range is wide: small/medium pairs (5s/8s/2s), spade draws, floats and some broadways. The A♣ is a high‑impact card that improves lots of hands in his calling range (any Ax, A2/A5 combos) and also removes many of the spade draw bluffs. I have K♦7♥ with no pair and very little showdown value, so barreling the turn would be a pure bluff on a card that helps his range and can easily be met with a call or a raise that I’m losing to.

With deep stacks and position, the lowest‑variance +EV line is to check back, keep the pot manageable, and see the river — then decide on a delayed bluff or fold to aggression. If Flash leads the river I’ll fold most of the time; if he checks I can consider a targeted river bluff depending on the card. For now: check.

I have a full house, Aces full of fives, which is an extremely strong hand on this board. The turn went check-check after an Ace arrived, suggesting that Minni is unlikely to have an Ace herself but may be holding a marginal hand like a pair of eights or a medium pocket pair. These hands will likely check behind to reach a cheap showdown if I check again. To maximize value, I am leading out for 800 chips, approximately two-thirds of the pot. This sizing is designed to look like a bluff or a thin value bet, encouraging a call from her remaining range.

I hold K♦7♥ — no pair or backdoor equity. The pot is 2000 and Flash bets 800 on the river, so calling 800 to win 2000 requires ~40% equity. Flash’s line (call preflop, call flop, check the A on the turn, then lead the paired-A river) is exactly the sort of line that includes many Ax (trips) and full-house combinations plus thin value hands; while some missed spade draws or floats could bluff, their frequency is unlikely to give me the ~40% needed. My hand has almost no showdown value vs that range, so folding is the lowest-variance +EV play here.

We are on hand 148 out of 150. I am currently trailing Minni by approximately 7,800 chips. While I need to make up ground, doing so with 8-2 offsuit is mathematically poor, even from the button. Folding here preserves my stack for the final two hands where I might find a better starting hand or a more advantageous situation to capitalize on. The risk of playing a very weak hand into the chip leader at this stage outweighs the potential 150 chip gain from a successful steal.

Heads-up on the button with T♦8♣ and deep stacks (~239bb). This is a playable hand with good post‑flop playability and I want to seize initiative from the BB. A standard open raise to 300 (3x the big blind) gives fold equity, avoids being too passive, and keeps my range wide and balanced. If Flash 3‑bets big I can fold this marginal hand; if he calls I can use position to c‑bet or probe the flop. Raising to 300 is the optimal mix of pressure and pot control here.

With only two hands left in the tournament, I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Minni opened for a standard 3x raise. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to see a flop. Jack-Nine has reasonable playability in a heads-up pot, and folding would be overly conservative. I will call to defend my blind and see if I can improve on the flop without overcommitting my stack right before the final hand.

With two hands left in the tournament and playing heads-up, I'm in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. I called a pre-flop raise. The flop comes 3-2-3, which is quite dry and favors neither player's range significantly, although the pre-flop aggressor generally maintains the range advantage. Since I have only Jack-high and no direct draw other than a very thin backdoor straight, I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. I am looking to minimize losses or potentially pick up the pot if they check back and I improve on the turn.

Heads‑up, I raised pre‑flop with T8o and Flash just called. Flop 3s‑2h‑3h is very dry but paired and checked to me — a perfect spot to take the initiative. I have no pair or draw, but my pre‑flop raising range contains many overcards and some hands that credibly hit this board; Flash has shown a tendency to check back a lot and fold to pressure. A continuation bet around 2/3 pot applies healthy fold equity against his calling/checking range while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: bet to take it down; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check behind blanks or give up to significant raises), and fold to polarized check‑raises (sets/quads/full houses) since I have little showdown value.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament, I am defending my stack to ensure a strong position for the final hand. On a flop of 3-2-3 paired, my Jack-Nine offsuit has completely missed. Minni is leading with a 2/3 pot bet (400 into 600), which is a strong continuation bet on such a dry board. While it is possible Minni is continuation betting with air, my Jack-high is behind much of her range including overcards like Ace-high, King-high, and Queen-high. Calling here would be speculative and likely -EV given the bet size and the lack of a draw. I will fold and preserve my 15,737 stack for the final hand.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently in second place, trailing Minni by approximately 8,476 chips. To overtake her and finish in first place, I would need to win a pot where she contributes at least 4,238 chips. King-Six offsuit is a standard raising hand from the button in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and attempt to maximize my final stack, while keeping in mind that overcoming the chip lead in a single hand without a massive confrontation is unlikely.

Heads-up vs a 3x open from the SB. QhTs is a playable hand in the BB — decent equity and good postflop playability versus a wide opening range. The call is cheap: pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 (≈33% equity break-even). A min 3-bet to 500 would be too small to credibly polarize and would bloat the pot out of position with a hand that’s not a clear 3-bet shove or value. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot controlled, and proceed based on texture (continue with top-pair/two-pair/strong draws, fold to heavy action on dry, face-card heavy runouts).

I called preflop to keep the pot small and see this flop. I completely missed: QhTs has no pair and only a runner-runner heart possibility (we have one heart with two on board). The flop is wet enough that betting OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and commit me with very little equity, and the board is paired (44) so the opener’s range likely contains many hands that connect. I’ll check to control the pot and gather information. Plan: if Flash fires a small c-bet (≈≤1/2 pot, ~300) I can call to realize equity (backdoor heart plus overcards) and re-evaluate on the turn; if they barrel big into the pot I’ll fold. If they check back I can take a stab on favorable turns (a Q or heart) or check down safe runouts.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I was the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a paired board (7-4-4). King-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play, but I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to take the initiative and attempt to win the pot immediately. Given the large stack deficit to Minni, I need to maximize my chips, though overtaking her lead in a single hand without an all-in confrontation is mathematically difficult.

Flash fires a small c-bet (300 into the 900 pot — pot is 1,200 after his bet). I have QhTs: no pair, two overcards and a runner‑runner heart (backdoor) draw. The bet is small so the call only needs ~25% equity to be break‑even. My raw chance to pair by river plus the tiny runner‑runner heart possibility is below that, but heads‑up Flash will c‑bet a wide range here (air, overcards, some draws), and I block some heart combos with Qh. I’m out of position and QhTs is too marginal to raise and bloat the pot. Plan: call to realize equity and see the turn; if I hit a Q/T or a favorable heart I can continue, but I’ll fold to heavy turn aggression on a blank.

Check. I already called Flash’s small turn bet instead of raising — Q♥T♠ has no pair, only two overcards, and the runner‑runner heart flush is dead after the 2♦. I have ~6 outs (3 queens + 3 tens) ≈ 13% to improve on the river, which is well below the ~25% break‑even a 300 bet offered. I’m out of position on a paired board (7♥ 4♣ 4♥ 2♦) versus a wide SB c‑bet range; bluff‑raising OOP here would bloat the pot with very little equity. Checking keeps the pot under control and lets me see the river for free. Plan: if I pair up (Q or T) or a very favorable heart comes on the river I can consider betting for value or calling; if I miss and face sizable river aggression I’ll fold to preserve my stack.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently in second place, trailing Minni by approximately 8,526 chips. To win the tournament, I would need to win a pot where Minni loses at least 4,264 chips, likely requiring an all-in confrontation which is improbable given the board and current action. On this turn, the Deuce of Diamonds is a blank. My King-high still has decent showdown value against Minni's potential missed draws or lower high-card floats. Since she checked to me again, I will check behind to take a free river card. This preserves my current stack and allows me to either win at showdown or potentially value bet/bluff on the river depending on the final card, aligning with the goal of maximizing my final stack size.

Check. I hold Q♥T♠ and missed the river — the board is 7♥-4♣-4♥-2♦-7♣ and I have no pair. Flash c‑bet the flop then checked turn and checked the river, which is consistent with a wide, marginal showdown range (or a slowplayed 4x/7x trap). I have almost no showdown value and very little fold equity OOP with such a dry river vs a player who checked back; bluffing to win 1,200 from stacks of 23k/15k is low-ROI and risks getting called by any pair or even a stubborn 7/4. Checking keeps the pot small and takes the least risky line — see the showdown and preserve stack depth for better spots.

This is the final hand of the tournament. The board has double-paired with Sevens and Fours (7h-4c-4h-2d-7c). My best five-card hand is Two Pair, Sevens and Fours, with a King kicker (7-7-4-4-K). Minni has checked the turn and the river, suggesting she likely doesn't have a Seven, a Four, or a strong pocket pair. While my King-high loses to any Ace-high or small pocket pairs (55, 66), it beats all other non-paired high card hands (like QJ, JT, etc.). Given the stack sizes, I am significantly behind in the tournament rankings and cannot realistically overtake first place without an unlikely all-in confrontation where Minni calls and loses. Therefore, the most professional play to maximize my final stack size is to check behind and take my showdown value, as a bluff is unlikely to be profitable enough to change the tournament outcome.
